barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Permit to Work (PTW)
Role: Administrative control to ensure proper authorization and planning
LOD Basis: Pre-work authorization system
supporting_text: ["A Permit to Work (PTW), Lift Plan, and Task Risk Assessments were completed prior to starting operations."]
incident_summary: On May 2, 2024, during 5-year recertification load testing of the Forward Starboard knuckle boom crane (KBC #1) on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse, a winch brake assembly failure occurred at approximately 70 metric tons while attempting to reach the full load test of 93.5 MT. The failure resulted in uncontrolled descent of a water bag test load, causing a Loss of Primary Containment (LOPC) due to hydraulic test port failure. The water bag contacted the take-on station corner, ripped open, and descended into the Gulf of Mexico. Property damage totaled $168,150. Root cause was determined to be crane overload due to improper sea lift area designation combined with lack of personnel training on Automatic Overload Protection System (AOPS) functionality and inadequate procedures.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Permit to Work Standard Operating Manual for Slip & Cut Drill Line
Role: Procedural control to ensure safe execution of slip and cut operations
LOD Basis: Primary control to prevent deviation from safe procedures

incident_summary: On 7 March 2024 at 1500 hours, during slip and cut drill line operations on the Seadrill West Neptune drillship, a 2-inch diameter drill line (approximately 19 feet long, 148 pounds) slipped from a nylon web sling being used with an air tugger and fell freely to the rig floor. A Seadrill employee attempting to exit the area tripped over coiled drill line and fell, and was subsequently struck by the falling drill line on his hardhat, middle back, and left shoulder. The employee sustained a torn labrum and shoulder strain requiring 10-12 weeks of physical therapy.

barrier_role_match_text:

incident_summary: On July 8, 2024, at 1908 hours, while pulling out of the hole with drill pipe on the jack-up Enterprise 205 at South Timbalier Block 23, the traveling block struck the crown saver assembly with approximately 117,000 lbs overpull, resulting in $11,210 in damages to the crown saver and crown-o-matic. The driller lost situational awareness by focusing on pipe tally while pulling, and the crown-o-matic failed to trip when contacted by the drill line because the barrel was not set properly. The incident was not immediately reported to BSEE.

incident_summary: On July 11, 2024, at 0730 hours, a contract pipeline operator from Quality Process Services suffered a serious head injury when struck by a winch assist handle during a routine lifeboat capsule inspection at the High Island A-5 Manta Ray Gathering Facility. The handle remained in the socket when the powered winch was engaged, causing it to rotate and strike the operator in the head. The operator sustained a fractured skull requiring surgery and was transported via USCG to UTMB in Galveston. The incident was caused by failure to remove the winch assist handle before engaging the powered winch, compounded by a bypassed safety device and lack of a Job Safety Analysis.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Preventive Maintenance Daily Checklist
Role: Identify degraded lifting equipment before use
LOD Basis: Routine inspection procedure to detect equipment degradation
supporting_text: ["Although a Preventive Maintenance Daily checklist was performed by the day and the night crew, the checklist did not clearly identify inspecting the stinger as a step in the checklist.", "Failure to inspect the stinger contributed significantly to the accident."]
incident_summary: On March 13, 2024, at approximately 20:10 hours, a 4x40' basket of plug and abandon tools weighing 13,000 pounds was being offloaded from the M/V GOL Cowboy to the upper cantilever of the jack-up Enterprise 264 using the port side crane. The stinger, rated for 9.80 tons in vertical pull, failed due to internal corrosion buildup from moisture in the socket. The basket fell and landed on the control room, P-tanks, and platform on top of the living quarters, causing structural damage estimated at $7,500. No injuries occurred. The failure to inspect the stinger prior to use was identified as a significant contributing factor.

incident_summary: On June 18, 2023 at 18:35 hours, an injured person (IP) suffered amputation of the middle and ring finger of his left hand while adjusting chains on a marine growth removal system (MGRS) during conductor removal operations at Platform Gail. The IP placed his left hand fingers under the chain during an assumed pause in conductor lifting. The conductor resumed rising, tightening the chain and trapping the IP's hand, resulting in amputation of finger tips between the fingernail and first knuckle. The incident occurred due to inadequate verbal communication regarding the 'all stop' command, lack of risk assessment for the MGRS methods employed, and deviation from the approved Application for Permit to Modify (APM) which specified use of diver methods with water jets.

incident_summary: On June 18, 2023 at 18:35 hours, an injured person (IP) suffered amputation of the middle and ring finger of his left hand while adjusting chains on a marine growth removal system (MGRS) during conductor removal operations at Platform Gail. The IP placed his left hand fingers under the chain during an assumed pause in conductor lifting. The conductor resumed rising, tightening the chain and trapping the IP's hand, resulting in amputation of finger tips between the fingernail and first knuckle. The incident occurred due to inadequate verbal communication regarding the 'all stop' command, lack of risk assessment for the MGRS methods employed, and deviation from the approved Application for Permit to Modify (APM) which specified use of diver methods with water jets.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Casing makeup procedure specifying ½ - ¾ turn requirement
Role: Procedural control to ensure proper connection engagement
LOD Basis: Contractor's running procedure
supporting_text: ["The casing only making up \u00bc - 3/8 turn instead of \u00bd - \u00be turn as specified by the contractor's procedures for proper make up", "A review of still pictures from the rig video indicated that the connection rotated only \u00bc - 3/8 turn during the makeup, instead of the \u00bd - \u00be turn specified in the contractor's procedure"]
incident_summary: On January 18, 2024, during drilling operations at GC 478, OCS-G35662, Well 004 on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse, a 22" casing connection failed at the rig floor. The S-90/MT pin connection of joint 16 was cross-threaded and made up only ¼ - 3/8 turn instead of the required ½ - ¾ turn. When the flush-mounted slips holding joint 17 were opened to install anti-rotation keys, the axial load transferred to the improperly made-up connection, causing the pin to separate from the box. Seventy-two joints of casing fell partially into the hole and onto the seafloor, breaking apart and impacting critical subsea infrastructure. No injuries or environmental impacts occurred, but damages exceeded $15 million.

incident_summary: On 20 July 2017 at 16:45 hours, Arena Offshore, LP experienced a dropped objects incident during drilling operations at Eugene Island Block 251C. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Job Safety and Environmental Analysis (JSEA)
Role: Administrative control to identify hazards and safe work procedures
LOD Basis: Pre-job hazard identification and planning
supporting_text: ["Although the JSEA did address disassembling tools, hand placement, and pinch points, it failed to address the possibility of lifting the tool string too high and it failed to address how to handle the C-plates safely and the existing hazard of caught between C-plates."]
incident_summary: On April 22, 2024, at 0815 hours, an injured person (IP) assisting a wireline helper to disconnect wireline tools on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse had his right hand caught between upper and lower C-plates weighing 175 lbs. The IP placed his hand under the upper C-plate to stabilize the tool string while the helper released the tools, causing them to fall 4 inches and crush the IP's hand. The IP sustained lacerations to the right hand ring and middle fingertips, a broken ring finger tip, and required 14 stitches. The IP was evacuated and released to light duty on 4/22/24 and full duty on 5/22/24. Root causes included inadequate training on wireline tools, lack of deepwater experience by the helper, poor communication about hazards, and failure to identify pinch point hazards.

incident_summary: On March 28, 2024, a Transocean Subsea Superintendent fell approximately nine feet through an open hole in a BOP work platform while performing maintenance on the Deepwater Atlas drillship. The hatch cover securing the hole had been damaged and removed during a pre-lift check without proper communication or barriers being installed. The IP was wearing a safety harness but was not tied off. He sustained a knee injury and abrasions and was evacuated for medical treatment. The incident was caused by failure to communicate the open hole, flawed hatch cover design, and lack of proper hazard analysis and barriers.

incident_summary: On May 31, 2023, at 09:00 hours, a Rope Access Technician (RAT #2) employed by Southey Contracting experienced an uncontrolled descent of approximately 90 feet while inspecting the Seawater Reject Caisson (SWRC) on the Stampede tension leg platform. The technician's working rope slipped through an improperly threaded I'D descender device at the anchor point. During the fall, the technician's left leg struck a SWRC support clamp, causing lacerations and ligament damage. The fall was arrested approximately 8 feet above the waterline when the anti-error catch safety feature of the I'D engaged near the stopper knot. The injured person was rescued by an alternate rope access team and evacuated via helicopter for medical treatment.

incident_summary: On May 31, 2023, at 09:00 hours, a Rope Access Technician (RAT #2) employed by Southey Contracting experienced an uncontrolled descent of approximately 90 feet while inspecting the Seawater Reject Caisson (SWRC) on the Stampede tension leg platform. incident_summary: On May 31, 2024 at 01:00 hrs, during offloading and backloading operations at GC 52 A Platform, a stinger attached to the crane's main block latched into a lace on the crane boom. Crane Operator 2 (CO-2) observed this and stopped operations, notifying the Person In Charge (PIC). The PIC instructed Crane Operator 1 (CO-1) to lower only the boom without lowering the main block, contrary to CO-2's recommendation to lower both simultaneously. As the boom was lowered, the stinger pulled tight and snapped a weld on the boom lace. The damaged crane continued in operation until June 6, 2024, when BSEE inspectors removed it from service. The boom tip was replaced at a cost of $70,000.

incident_summary: On May 31, 2017 at 1410 hours, an employee was severely injured when a 10,000 lb construction tool house being lifted by a platform crane came into contact with channel iron protruding from a structural member. The load shifted and struck the rigger who was positioned between the tool house and handrails, causing a fractured pelvis. Contributing factors included lack of line-of-sight communication between crane operator and rigger, ineffective radio communication, and the rigger being positioned in a pinch point hazard zone.

incident_summary: On 22-Feb-2014, an Exxon Mobil crew member attempted to lower an 85-pound hydraulic hose from a crane landing 38 feet above the deck using an improper technique. The crew member held a 1/2" rope to control the descent while another crew member fed the coiled hose over the railing. When the full weight of the hose went over the railing, the injured party lost control of the rope, pulling his left hand into the railing and tearing skin at the base of the thumb. The injured party was evacuated via Medevac and received medical treatment.

incident_summary: On March 12, 2016, at 10:06 hours, a fire occurred in accommodation cabin 417 on the drill ship Maersk Valiant during drilling operations in Alaminos Canyon 475. The smoke alarm system was activated and personnel mustered. Fire Teams responded and extinguished the fire using fire hoses after handheld fire extinguishers proved ineffective. The fire was extinguished at 10:26 hours. Forensic analysis could not definitively identify the ignition source; the Samsung tablet could not be eliminated as a possible source but could not be proven. The source was classified as undetermined. Damage was estimated at $50,000 with no injuries reported. A similar fire had occurred in the B-deck linen closet on December 13, 2015.

incident_summary: On March 12, 2016, at 10:06 hours, a fire occurred in accommodation cabin 417 on the drill ship Maersk Valiant during drilling operations in Alaminos Canyon 475. The smoke alarm system was activated and personnel mustered. Fire Teams responded and extinguished the fire using fire hoses after handheld fire extinguishers proved ineffective. Forensic analysis could not definitively identify the ignition source; the Samsung tablet could not be eliminated as a possible source but could not be proven. incident_summary: While lifting a 2.69 Ton discharge module from a 14-P-220 Mud Pump using a 3 Ton chain hoist trolley, the internal chain guides failed in a bent condition, causing the module to fall approximately 3 inches onto metal grating. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Iron Roughneck torque application and verification
Role: Ensure drill pipe connections are properly torqued to specification
LOD Basis: Primary control to prevent improper torque
supporting_text: ["Operator failed to properly torque the drill pipe connection", "Drill crew failed to torque and verify the torque of the connection", "All connections were checked and found to be properly torqued using the Iron Roughneck"]
incident_summary: On 1 April 2022, during well abandonment operations on the Helix Q-4000 semi-submersible rig in well SA001 (G-19409 Lease, Alaminos Canyon 815), four sections of 5-7/8 inch Heavy Weight Drill Pipe (HWDP) and the Bottom Hole Assembly (BHA) weighing 6000 pounds and 123 feet long disconnected from the drill string and dropped to the sea floor. The disconnection occurred on 31 March 2022 at 15:42 when a loss of 5000 pounds was recorded on the drill string weight indicator, but was not identified until 1 April 2022 at 08:20 via ROV monitoring. The root cause was determined to be an improperly torqued drill pipe connection. The lost equipment came to rest 92 meters from well center and 377 meters from the center of the safe operating zone. No subsea infrastructure damage was sustained. Recovery and inspection costs totaled $2,300,000.

incident_summary: On May 5, 2016, at 1339 hours, an injured person's right leg became entangled in a 24-foot tagline during a blind crane lift of a cargo box from the Safe Welding Area to the pipe rack on the Perdido platform. incident_summary: On 08-FEB-2016 at 1315 hours, an H&P Mechanic was struck in the face by a flange associated with a butterfly valve while tightening bolts on a flange connection secured in a bench vise. The flange rotated and released from the vise due to excessive force applied during torqueing of the second-to-last bolt. The mechanic sustained lacerations above the right eyebrow and down the bridge of the nose, requiring medical evacuation and stitches. Contributing factors included improper body positioning, excessive force applied to the tool, and excessive wear on the vice jaw.

Barrier: Visual monitoring of derrick condition

incident_summary: During tripping in the hole with 22 inch casing on the landing string, a Floor Man monitoring the hydra racker drag chain noticed excessive derrick shaking and observed smoke from the main side crown cluster. The first reduction 78 inch sheave in the crown cluster was found to have a vertical crack and had shifted downward approximately 3 inches. The root cause was identified as weld defects near the root of the weld between the web plates and the hub as noted in NOV Product Bulletin No. 87819987.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Working-at-heights permit requirement
Role: Administrative control to prevent unauthorized work at height
LOD Basis: Permit system designed to inform all rig workers of hazardous work location and trigger operational shutdown
supporting_text: ["the IP positioned himself over the handrail located in the derrick, outside of the designated safe-observation area, and onto the upper fingerboards without the proper work permits", "the investigation revealed there was no working from heights permit issued during this operation"]
incident_summary: On October 12, 2024, at 0540 hours, a Derrickman (Injured Person/IP) working on the upper fingerboards of the Pipe Racking System during tripping operations was caught and pinned between the upper PRS arm and a stand of pipe. The IP had repositioned himself from the designated safe observation area onto the upper fingerboards without a proper working-at-heights permit to manually secure a malfunctioning fingerboard latch in the open position. While attempting to tie the latch, the PRS initiated an automatic move to retrieve a stand of pipe in the same row, extending the upper arm and pinning the IP. The IP was freed by manual intervention and evacuated for medical treatment.

barrier_role_match_text:

incident_summary: On 8 December 2023 at approximately 08:30 am, a Floorhand sustained a lost time injury when struck by the Top Drive System (TDS) on the Noble Globetrotter II at Alaminos Canyon 735. The injured person was positioned on the port pipe racker upper access platform approximately 80 feet above the drill floor, attempting to troubleshoot a slack rope alarm. The injured person failed to notify the Driller of their return to the platform. When the Driller lowered the TDS, the lower port TDS roller assembly contacted the injured person, folding their upper torso around the handrail. The injured person sustained a punctured lung, fractured ribs, and fractured scapula. The incident resulted from inadequate communication, lack of hazard identification on the JSA, absence of engineering barriers, and no visible warnings identifying the moving equipment hazard.

incident_summary: On October 3, 2024, at approximately 9:00 am, an arc flash fire occurred in the SCR house on Shell's Perdido SPAR when a Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR) failed. During wireline logging operations and cement pumping for hydrate mitigation, the cement unit's electronic panel lost power. Personnel entering the SCR room heard a loud popping sound and observed smoke. Investigation identified arcing between DC buss bars, arcing on circuit breaker heat sinks, and a failed buss insulator in SCR cabinet #1. The fire was not detected by the rig fire and gas alarm system and was only identified when personnel entered the SCR house. Property damage was estimated at $25,000.

incident_summary: On 4 September 2023 at approximately 13:20, approximately 24 barrels of Synthetic Based Mud (SBM) was discharged into the Gulf of Mexico from Alaminos Canyon (AC) 857, H&P 205 Drilling Rig located on the Shell Perdido Spar. incident_summary: On June 21, 2023, at 0930 hours, the Variable Frequency Device A (VFD-A) failed at the Perdido Spar platform in Alaminos Canyon Block 857, releasing smoke and activating smoke detectors in the Subsea Motor Control Center room. The failure was caused by the B-phase reactor on the VFD-A failing to ground, resulting in smoke generation. The automated facility shut-in system functioned as designed, triggering personnel muster. Cabinet A reached 350°F while other cabinets reached 210°F. No injuries or pollution were reported. The estimated replacement cost was $65,000.

incident_summary: On July 18, 2021, the Diana Hoover Spar facility experienced loss of mooring line #4 due to chain link failure. The 160 mm R4 grade chain link, in service for approximately 20 years, fractured at the crown and flash weld. Investigation by Stress Engineering Services determined the fractures were caused by hydrogen embrittlement resulting from improper heat treatment during manufacturing, which resulted in high hardness, low ductility, and low impact toughness. The facility remained stable with the remaining 11 mooring lines per 2010 Facility Model analysis. Estimated repair cost is $10-15 million with 6-9 months required for replacement chain manufacturing.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Transformer Design and Installation
Role: Primary electrical system component
LOD Basis: Equipment design to prevent failure
supporting_text: ["the loss of power was due to a failure of a 11,000 volt to 110-volt transformer", "the faulty transformer sent 11,000 volts to ground"]
incident_summary: On July 17, 2021 at 10:30 hours, the Transocean Offshore Deepwater Pontus Drillship experienced a complete loss of power while conducting well completion operations on the SA 008 Well in Alaminos Canyon Block 815. The loss of power was caused by failure of an 11,000 volt to 110-volt transformer that sent 11,000 volts to ground, triggering the ground fault sensing device to trip low voltage breakers open. This caused all six power generating units to shut down, resulting in vessel drift. The crew successfully activated the Emergency Disconnect System to disconnect the LMRP from the subsea BOP within 35 seconds. The Chief Electrician manually reset the breakers to restore power after the vessel had drifted approximately 1,000 meters. The LMRP sustained damage from dragging on the seafloor for approximately 320 feet. No injuries or pollution occurred. Total property damage was estimated at $2,150,000.

Role: Detect failed cement squeeze and valve leakage
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["The pressure test failed to hold 1000 psi over 15 minutes as required per 30 CFR 250.1715"]
incident_summary: On 18 March 2022 at 1800 hours, during abandonment operations on Shell's Alaminos Canyon 815 well SA001 from the Helix Q-4000, a pressure test of a five-barrel cement squeeze failed to hold 1000 psi. Investigation revealed the chemical injection tree valve 1 (CIT1) on the subsea tree was leaking approximately 188 barrels of a 50/50 mixture of Mono Ethylene Glycol and seawater into the Gulf of Mexico. The CIT1 valve had not been functioned to verify closure prior to the pressure test; operators relied on documented position from well handover procedures. Manual ROV intervention and hydraulic functioning of the valve eventually stopped the leak.

incident_summary: On December 3, 2021, at approximately 13:00 hours, the riser elevation crane on the Diamond Ocean Blackhawk drillship dropped one end of a riser joint into the riser holding area during drilling operations at MC 82. The incident resulted in damage to the aft stairway and handrail. Investigation revealed that the crane's hydraulic flow control valve had become worn and stuck in the fully engaged position, preventing proper indication of latch status to the crane operator. The evening crew flagger was new to the position and had not been trained on latch verification procedures.

incident_summary: On 26 April 2017 at approximately 0830 hours, a Deckhand was injured while offloading a 550 gallon diesel tote tank from the M/V Miss Julie at platform BAA 105A. The Deckhand attached the crane's stringer to a pre-slung four-part wire rope sling, signaled to lift, but then moved toward the tote tank without stopping the lift when he noticed one sling leg was caught on a shackle. The tote tank lifted while unbalanced, swinging and pinning the Deckhand between the tank and starboard gunwale. The Deckhand sustained a fracture to his lower right leg and two fractured left ribs, requiring surgery.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Surface Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV)
Role: Primary barrier to prevent uncontrolled hydrocarbon discharge
LOD Basis: Subsurface safety device designed to isolate well
supporting_text: ["apparent failure of the Surface Control Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV) in Well TD004", "rod piston seals of the Surface Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve had failed, allowing oil and gas to enter the hydraulic lines", "The probable cause of the leak is an equipment failure related to the rod piston seal on the SCSSV"]
incident_summary: On 6 June 2022 at 1030 hours, a subsea leak of approximately 9 barrels of hydrocarbon was discovered at Well TD004 (Slot 47/MC822-13/TD004) on the Thunderhorse platform in Mississippi Canyon Block 778. The leak was caused by a rod piston seal failure in the Surface Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV). The incident was preceded by a platform power loss on 5 June 2022 that triggered an unplanned shut-in and revealed increased hydraulic fluid consumption. The leak was detected through ROV inspection and resulted in a visible sheen observed in satellite imagery and during aerial surveys. BP's subsea leak detection system (C-ROC Algorithm) was active but could not detect this relatively small discharge.

Role: Visual detection and confirmation of subsea leaks and equipment condition
LOD Basis: Direct visual inspection of subsea equipment and discharge
supporting_text: ["a subsea leak was observed by a Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) during a subsea inspection", "On 11 June 2022 at 1500 hrs (NRC# 1338496), BP observed (by the ROV) a crude oil release (through bubbles) coming out the subsea control module vent"]
incident_summary: On 6 June 2022 at 1030 hours, a subsea leak of approximately 9 barrels of hydrocarbon was discovered at Well TD004 (Slot 47/MC822-13/TD004) on the Thunderhorse platform in Mississippi Canyon Block 778. The leak was caused by a rod piston seal failure in the Surface Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV). The incident was preceded by a platform power loss on 5 June 2022 that triggered an unplanned shut-in and revealed increased hydraulic fluid consumption. The leak was detected through ROV inspection and resulted in a visible sheen observed in satellite imagery and during aerial surveys. BP's subsea leak detection system (C-ROC Algorithm) was active but could not detect this relatively small discharge.

incident_summary: On 8 June 2022 at 1310 hours, production personnel at MC 778 Thunderhorse South reported an external subsea leak at the P2 Riser Touchdown Connector - Pipeline End Termination (PLET) No. 5. The leak was discovered during a ROV survey initiated following a platform blackout on 5 June 2022. An estimated 12.8 to 128.1 gallons of crude oil was released. The root cause was determined to be connector failure on the PLET, potentially due to design flaw, improper installation, or manufacturing defects. The leak was temporarily sealed using a Connector Actuation Tool pending permanent replacement.

incident_summary: On 6 October 2019 at 14:40, the Motor Vessel Ms. Emily struck the Breton Sound (BS) 54 #13 platform operated by Cantium, LLC. The vessel was traveling at 10 knots or less on a west northwest course when large ground swells began pushing it towards the platform. The Captain attempted to avoid collision by steering full right rudder and shifting engines to reverse, but all 4 engines died due to a safety feature that automatically shuts down engines when shifting from forward to reverse gear without allowing the required 6 second delay. The vessel's bow struck the platform, causing structural damage estimated at $150,000. There were no injuries, pollution, or loss of primary containment. No personnel were on the platform at the time of the allision.

incident_summary: On 13 October 2020 at 0730 hrs, the shrimp boat Master Paul (86 feet long) collided with the upper deck of the unmanned BS 55 EB Platform in Breton Sound Block 55, Gulf of Mexico. The vessel's outrigging contacted the platform structure, causing extensive damage including a broken starboard boom, damaged extended walkway, foghorn, navigation aid light, and davit support. The only flowing well (EB-12) was remotely shut in and secured. No injuries or pollution resulted. Total damage assessed at $125,000. Platform returned to production on 15 October 2020.

incident_summary: On 8 November 2022 at 0645 hours at MP 42 Delta platform, a contract operator (Quality Production Management) assisting with offloading a 500-gallon chemical transporter from a field boat sustained a crush injury to his left hand. As the crane lowered the transporter to the platform deck, it swung toward the injured personnel who was holding a tag line. The injured personnel placed his left hand on the transporter to stop the swinging motion, but the transporter contacted a stationary transporter on deck, crushing his hand between the two units. The injured personnel lost consciousness and was evacuated for medical treatment. Expected recovery time: 2-3 months.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Slip and Cut Procedure
Role: Administrative control requiring CMC cylinders to be fully retracted to beams before proceeding
LOD Basis: Procedural requirement to prevent pressure differential buildup
supporting_text: ["According to the slip and cut procedure, the Driller is responsible for lowering the CMC cylinders back to the beams before proceeding forward to the next step.", "The Driller did not follow Transocean's Slip & Cut procedure by retracting the CMC cylinders all the way down to the beams before proceding forward to the next step."]
incident_summary: On September 21, 2020, at approximately 2100 hours, the Driller on the Transocean Deepwater Conqueror attempted to take drill string weight with the Crown Motion Compensator (CMC) while the CMC cylinders were at mid-stroke. The Driller opened the CMC valve without fully retracting the cylinders to the beams as required by procedure. The CMC rapidly dropped 13 feet, shearing all 7 bolts on the starboard equalizing bumper assembly bracket (48 pounds), which fell 203 feet to the drill floor. The port bumper assembly had 6 of 7 bolts sheared. A software logic error in the CMC auto-equalizing feature prevented pressure equalization before the Olmsted valve opened, causing the rapid retraction. No injuries were reported.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Back Pressure Valve (BPV) for NBK 0420 Chemelectric Oil Treater
Role: Prevent overpressurization of treater by maintaining system back pressure
LOD Basis: Primary pressure control device on treater inlet

incident_summary: On 19-OCT-2022, during facility startup after a three-day shutdown, the back pressure valve (BPV) for the NBK 0420 Chemelectric Oil Treater failed to operate as designed. The valve remained open, allowing 94 PSI of gas into the treater, causing the Pressure Safety Valve to relieve to the relief header and vent flare. The vent flare emitted a fine mist of oily water to the atmosphere. An estimated 0.774 gallons of oil entered Gulf of Mexico waters, with satellite imagery later detecting a sheen measuring 2.44 nautical miles in length and 2.67 nautical miles at the widest point. The incident was reported to the National Response Center.

incident_summary: On 27 January 2022 at approximately 1625 hours, a fire occurred near the starter of a rental gas compressor on Main Pass 73-A production platform. The fire resulted from an improperly tightened JIC fitting on the engine's starter exhaust hose that backed off over time due to vibration, combined with a leaking Flow Safety Valve (FSV) that allowed natural gas to migrate to the hot exhaust area (>900 degrees). incident_summary: On 25 February 2022 at 0530 hours, a pollution event occurred at West Delta 73 C production platform. An estimated 660 gallons (later revised to approximately 111 gallons) of unrecoverable oil was discharged into the Gulf of Mexico through the platform's vent boom. The incident resulted from a combination of a Water Skimmer float device failure (pinhole defect) and failure of the Level Safety High (LSH) device on the Vent Scrubber to operate as designed. Contributing factor was operator fatigue, with OP2 working 21 hours (0600 on 24 Feb to 0300 on 25 Feb) and becoming extremely fatigued before the incident occurred.

barrier_role_match_text:

incident_summary: On 13 May 2022 at approximately 1125 hours, a lead crane mechanic employed by Seatrax was struck and pinned by a falling boom mid-section while removing connector pins from an Aero-G15 crane on platform WD 74 F. The mechanic had struck a connector pin with a sledgehammer to remove it prior to rigging the mid-section to the bridle, contrary to proper procedure. The pin ejected unexpectedly, causing the boom sections to split and fall, pinning the mechanic's right leg between the boom and sledgehammer handle. The mechanic sustained a compound fracture and was evacuated by helicopter to hospital. Root causes included failure to follow proper Job Safety Analysis procedures, inadequate communication between mechanics, and failure to use required Boom Connector Safety Pins that had been left at the hub facility.

incident_summary: On August 14, 2014, at 2020 hours on the Noble Globetrotter drilling rig, the tensioner ring disengaged from the slip joint while the crew was scoping out the slip joint in preparation for diverter installation. The riser became unsupported, causing the Hands Free Goose Neck (HFGN) to separate from the tensioner ring. This resulted in extensive damage to the Lower Marine Riser Package (LMRP), numerous riser joints, tensioner ring, coflex housing, service lines, mux reels, hot line, and top-drive components. The root cause was the Sub-sea Engineer's failure to ensure that the locking dogs and secondary lock were fully engaged on the tensioner ring.

incident_summary: On June 25, 2019, a 162 lb packer setting tool detached from the wireline rope socket and free fell approximately 40 ft to the rig floor during well completion operations on the Deepwater Asgard Drillship. The incident was caused by a breakdown in communication between the winch operator and E-line operator during rigging down operations. The winch operator slacked off before the E-line operator, causing the lubricator weight to set down on the packer setting tool, which allowed the electric line to pull free from the rope socket. No injuries occurred; damage was limited to an indentation in the drill floor and a wooden plank.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Hose assembly rated for appropriate collapse pressure
Role: Structural integrity of chemical injection line
LOD Basis: Primary containment barrier
supporting_text: ["The hose assembly was rated for the appropriate collapse pressure", "The system had been installed in 2015 and tested and demonstrated full pressure integrity", "The hose itself is rated for a differential pressure in collapse of 3,262 psi but once the outer jacket is compromised the collapse resistance of the wire braiding is lost"]
incident_summary: On April 30, 2018, Murphy reported a subsea umbilical leak from a chemical injection line (Hydraulic Flying Lead) for the De Soto Canyon 4 subsea well. The leak was discovered by ROV during a subsea survey. Two leaks were identified: one on the HFL between the manifold and SUTA, and another on the pressure cap of the PLET. The chemical discharged was paraffin inhibitor containing naphthalene. The outer jacket of the hose was damaged due to chafing against the mounting bracket, compromising collapse resistance. Differential pressure across the inner sheath went negative, causing collapse and tearing of the internal pressure sheath. Maximum estimated volume discharged was 900 bbls, though BSEE calculated worst case discharge at 37,000 to 1.6 million gallons based on May 2, 2017 start date.

incident_summary: On April 30, 2018, Murphy reported a subsea umbilical leak from a chemical injection line (Hydraulic Flying Lead) for the De Soto Canyon 4 subsea well. The leak was discovered by ROV during a subsea survey. Two leaks were identified: one on the HFL between the manifold and SUTA, and another on the pressure cap of the PLET. The chemical discharged was paraffin inhibitor containing naphthalene. The outer jacket of the hose was damaged due to chafing against the mounting bracket, compromising collapse resistance. Differential pressure across the inner sheath went negative, causing collapse and tearing of the internal pressure sheath. Maximum estimated volume discharged was 900 bbls, though BSEE calculated worst case discharge at 37,000 to 1.6 million gallons based on May 2, 2017 start date.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Detection of gas presence before explosion
LOD Basis: Detects hazardous gas condition to alert workers
supporting_text: ["both IPs' handheld gas detector detected the presence of gas, alarming both of the IPs"]
incident_summary: On 24 June 2022 at approximately 1147 hours, two Gulf Island Services (GIS) employees were performing construction work on Main Pass 260 Platform P, a right-of-way platform owned by Destin Pipeline Company located 55 miles offshore in 300 feet of water. While installing a skillet to isolate the flare scrubber and flare, workers unbolted a 10-inch flange and determined the skillet did not fit. While waiting for the correct size skillet, a handheld gas detector alarmed, pressure was heard escaping from the flange, and an explosion occurred with a large plume of smoke. Both workers reported burning sensations and smoke inhalation. Both were transported to an onshore medical facility and released the same day without restrictions. No pollution or facility damage occurred. BSEE investigation identified three plausible causes: pyrophoric iron (FeS) combustion when exposed to oxygen through the opened flange (most likely), galvanic cell corrosion from a chain hoist generating electric current, or hydrate release within the piping.

incident_summary: On September 21, 2016, at approximately 19:15 hours, a crane crew was repositioning Product Tanks on the deck of Motor Vessel Candy Barrel. The second P-Tank was lifted and repositioned alongside the first tank. The tank was initially placed at an angle. The Crane Operator picked the tank up and swung it over the water then back towards the boat. As the load swung starboard, the operator stopped it over the first tank. Vertical movement of the boat caused the bottom tank to strike the suspended tank multiple times, shock loading the crane. The wire rope parted, the suspended tank fell onto the tank below, the boom buckled and fell into the Gulf of Mexico. Estimated damage: $800,000.

incident_summary: During milling operations on well A-6 with reverse circulation to unplug a rock bit, a weld failure occurred at the connection of the original 16" casing and a recently butt-welded 16" riser. The crew increased pump rate during reverse circulation, causing the rock bit to pack off and rapidly increase internal pressure in the 16" casing to over 2000 psi, exceeding the reduced burst pressure of the welded casing. The casing burst along the recently welded seams. No injuries or environmental spills occurred.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Crane dynamic load testing and safe working limit establishment
Role: Verify crane capacity before operations
LOD Basis: Pre-operational verification of equipment capability
supporting_text: ["The lessee had previously dynamically tested the crane to approximately 35,000 pounds. The lessee also established a 'safe' working limit of 30,000 pounds.", "The force of the falling work string then shock loaded the crane causing the crane boom to catastrophically buckle and collapse"]
incident_summary: During a rigless Plug and Abandonment operation at East Breaks 160 A on 01 December 2015 at approximately 2230 hours, a work string became lodged in the well casing while being lifted by the platform crane. After initial attempts to free the string using the crane failed, the crew attached a hydraulic jack system while leaving the crane still connected to the work string. When the hydraulic jack applied 275,000 pounds of pulling force, the work string suddenly released from the downhole bind, rebounded upward 4-6 feet, and then fell back into the hole. The abrupt release of energy threw the hydraulic jack's traveling slips out of the upper casing bowl, eliminating support for the work string. When the work string fell back down, its full weight was suddenly transferred to the crane's hoist system, shock loading the crane boom. The boom catastrophically buckled and collapsed onto the hydraulic jack work basket, folding in half with the jib end suspended approximately 15 feet above the manned Crane Operator's cab. No injuries, fire, or environmental pollution occurred. Damage exceeded $160,000.

incident_summary: On 9-December-2019 at 0700 hours, during a blind lift of 41 joints of 3-1/2" drill pipe with BHA and mechanical cutters at East Breaks 160 A platform, the work string fell through improperly seated DU-Regular slips after the crane lowered the drill pipe approximately four feet. The tool joint contacted the single joint elevators, causing a shock load to the crane. The root cause was failure to ensure personnel properly inspected slips and bowls and operated equipment per manufacturer's instructions. Contributing factors included incomplete seating of slips, inadequate lubrication, and JSA deficiencies.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Pressure Safety Valve on MBF-V940 Fuel Gas Scrubber
Role: Prevent overpressurization upstream of fuel gas filter
LOD Basis: Primary pressure relief device upstream of MAK-F960A
supporting_text: ["The Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) on the MBF-V940 Fuel Gas Scrubber was set for 270 PSI, it did not relieve until 390 PSI.", "During testing of safety devices the PSV on MBF-V940 was set to relieve at 270 PSI; it did not relieve until 390 PSI, thus allowing 390 PSI or greater pressure to the MAK-F960A Fuel Gas Filter."]
incident_summary: On 9 May 2015 at approximately 1728 hours, the MAK-F960A Fuel Gas Filter vessel experienced catastrophic overpressurization during facility restart following a pressure upset. The vessel top was blown off, struck a nearby handrail creating sparks that ignited streaming fuel gas. The incident resulted in a fire that was extinguished by operators using portable fire extinguishers. Root causes included severe corrosion of vessel mounting tabs, failed pressure safety valves both upstream and downstream of the filter, unauthorized modifications to the filter vessels that removed differential gauges and altered level indicators, and a single-stage pressure reduction system that was inadequate for the operating conditions.

barrier_role_match_text:

incident_summary: On 9-May-2018 at approximately 10:30 hours, a fire occurred in the temporary quarters of Platform EB 165 A. A mechanic discovered charred soot on the temporary quarters entrance door while conducting holding checks on SSVs during compressor maintenance shutdown. The alarm was sounded, muster was initiated, and personnel investigated. No visible flame was detected. Fire investigation determined the fire started on a lower bunk mattress, with the probable cause being a cracked reading light bulb, though the cause was ultimately determined to be undetermined and accidental in origin. The fire self-extinguished due to lack of oxygen. Estimated damage was $75,000.

incident_summary: On 21 March 2015 at approximately 1500 hours, a contract worker was struck in the upper lip by a swivel socket attached to a Pneumatic Torque Unit (PTU) when it slipped off the nut of a stud bolt during production header maintenance at the Nansen SPAR. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Counterbalance valve in boom hoist system
Role: Prevent uncontrolled descent of boom and load
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["Debris was found in the orifice of the counterbalance valve", "The debris clogged the orifice to the point the counterbalance valve was held open and could not close"]
incident_summary: On 13-Dec-2019 at approximately 16:40 hours at East Breaks 643 Boomvang SPAR, a 60-ton West crane was lifting a 33,700 lb crown section onto the derrick. As the crane operator began swinging the load to the right, the crane boom began to lower on its own. After descending approximately 15-20 feet, the operator regained control and placed the load on the elevated pipe rack. Investigation revealed debris in the boom hoist counterbalance valve orifice, which held the valve open and prevented the mechanical brake from setting. A contributing factor was the crane operator not fully releasing the joystick or setting the brakes.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Umbilical centralizers
Role: Guide and protect umbilical during subsurface lowering
LOD Basis: Primary mechanical control to prevent umbilical contact with hard tank guide

incident_summary: On 21-Sept-2015 at approximately 1745 hours, a chemical release occurred at Anadarko Petroleum's East Breaks 643 Boomvang SPAR during umbilical lowering operations. The umbilical hung on the hard tank umbilical guide, resulting in a breach and release of approximately 41 barrels of methanol and ethylene glycol. Biological marine growth caused umbilical centralizers to become stuck, causing the pull head to lie over at an angle and impact the hard tank guide, cutting the umbilical and three inner tubes.

LOD Basis: Early detection of hazardous conditions
supporting_text: ["A contracted Fire Watch worker stated he bump tested the gas detector and took samples at various intended hot work areas (B2, A1 and B3 piling). The firewatch did not question why the sump pile surface lid was open", "At 13:40 hours on 10 August 2017, two welders began installing angle-iron. incident_summary: At 13:50 hours on August 10, 2017, a welding flash fire occurred at the +12 level of EB-110A platform during hot work to prepare for temporary work platform installation. A welder using an oxygen/acetylene cutting torch to shape angle-iron on the B3 piling directed the torch toward an open out-of-service sump pile containing hydrocarbon fluids. The torch ignited vapors from the sump, causing a flash fire. One welder (Welder B) sustained burns to hands and fell approximately 12 feet into the water. He was retrieved and transferred to UTMB Hospital. The fire self-extinguished. No environmental impact or additional injuries occurred.

incident_summary: On 21-May-2025 at approximately 1:45 p.m., a crane incident occurred on the Nansen Spar in East Breaks 602 block. Contract scaffold builders were erecting scaffolding around the crane boom rest and used multiple 1/4-ton lever hoists as bracing, attaching them to both the scaffolding and the crane boom lattice without notifying the Lifting Authority. After a lunch break, the Crane Operator resumed lifting operations without conducting a pre-use inspection or checking for changes. When the operator lifted the boom out of the rest, one of the 1/4-ton lever hoists broke and fell approximately 30 feet to the flare boom mezzanine deck. The broken lever hoist weighed 0.5 pounds and caused minor property damage ($500). No personnel injuries were reported.

After a lunch break, the Crane Operator resumed lifting operations without conducting a pre-use inspection or checking for changes. When the operator lifted the boom out of the rest, one of the 1/4-ton lever hoists broke and fell approximately 30 feet to the flare boom mezzanine deck. The broken lever hoist weighed 0.5 pounds and caused minor property damage ($500). No personnel injuries were reported.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Depressurization of vessel prior to component removal
Role: Critical mitigation to prevent pressurized release
LOD Basis: Primary control to eliminate pressure hazard before opening vessel
supporting_text: ["the vessel was not depressurized and the hot oil was not drained to a safe level below the TC", "critical mitigations were not executed prior to removing the TC from the vessel"]
incident_summary: On June 23, 2014, at approximately 10:30 A.M., the Person in Charge (PIC) at Arena Offshore East Cameron 328 B facility was replacing a faulty pneumatic temperature controller (TC) on Heater Treater #2 (NBK-2770). The TC was installed directly into a thread-o-let within the liquid medium of the vessel instead of inside a thermo-well as recommended by the manufacturer. Prior to removal, the vessel was not depressurized and hot oil was not drained to a safe level. When the TC was removed, hot pressurized oil blew out of the one inch opening, striking and burning the PIC on his left bicep. Oil continued to flow across the deck, striking the line heater approximately 20 feet away. Operations personnel manually shut down the facility and installed a nipple and ball valve to isolate the leak. A total of 12 gallons of crude oil was discharged into offshore waters.

incident_summary: On 21-Feb-2015 at 0400 hours, while permanently abandoning well A1 on the EC-346A platform, an unsecured vee-door gate (120 lbs) that had been removed and leaned against a storage container fell and struck a worker on the right knee. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Pre-operation inspection of davit and rod assembly
Role: Detect corrosion and fastener integrity issues before operation
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["There was no inspection of the condition of the davit and rod assembly prior to placing the weight of the manway hatch on the davit and rod assembly.", "The severe corrosion and location of the washer on the davit, and the corrosion of the davit was not detected."]
incident_summary: On October 19, 2017 at approximately 1911 hours, a construction employee severely injured his left foot when a 24-inch manway hatch from a water skimmer (MBM-7110) dropped and struck his foot. The hatch was being opened using a davit pole assembly for cleaning operations. The piping supporting the rod assembly failed due to severe corrosion of the davit and improper placement or detachment of the washer fastener. The employee suffered lacerations and multiple fractures to the left foot. Root causes included lack of pre-operation inspection of the davit and rod assembly condition, and inadequate Job Safety & Environmental Analysis (JSEA) that failed to capture hazards associated with the manway hatch removal.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Cat walk back stop to support casing joints
Role: Prevent casing joint from slipping down cat walk
LOD Basis: Primary engineering control to prevent hazard
supporting_text: ["The probable cause of the finger injury was attributed to a casing joint slipping down the cat walk because it was not supported by the back stop."]
incident_summary: An Ensco Floorhand sustained a severe finger injury on 14 February 2014 at 14:45 hours while assisting with picking up 7-inch casing joints at the v-door area on the Ensco 90 jack-up rig. The worker installed a lift nubbin in casing joint #2 and tightened it with a valve bar. When tightening the nubbin, casing joint #2 slid down the cat walk, pinning the worker's right index finger between the valve bar and the end of casing joint #3. The finger was nearly amputated at the first knuckle joint. The worker was evacuated to LSU Medical Center where surgical amputation of the distal phalanx was required. The probable cause was attributed to the casing joint not being supported by the back stop, allowing it to slip.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Personal protective equipment to reduce head injury severity
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["The employee's hardhat struck a wheel valve handle forcing his head and neck back"]
incident_summary: On August 5, 2019 at approximately 0930 hours, an employee fractured his right ankle due to an awkward fall after coming in contact with a wheel valve handle at Cox Operating's Eugene Island 257 C platform. The employee was repairing a leaking Temperature Safety Element (TSE) located over the Flare Scrubber while standing on a ladder approximately 3 feet above the deck. Pain and swelling developed on August 8, 2019, and medical evaluation confirmed a right ankle fracture.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Engine Shutdown Device (ESD) on generator package
Role: Automatic shutdown of engine upon overspeed detection
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["Operators discovered that the ESD on the generator package tripped, but the generator continued to run even though the air shut off tripped", "The safety system did sense the engine overspeed and in response tried to close the air shut off valve, but the valve failed to fully close"]
incident_summary: On October 21, 2020 at 0647 hours, Cox Operating operators at EI 259C observed a fire at unmanned platform EI 259A. The fire originated from the diesel generator exhaust. Investigation revealed that a governor actuator linkage failure caused engine overspeed, leading to turbo lockup and oil seal leakage. The air shut off valve failed to fully close due to oil gumming up an o-ring seal, allowing the engine to continue running and increasing exhaust temperature. Carbon buildup in the exhaust was ignited by high temperatures. A preventive maintenance inspection in January 2020 had noted suspected exhaust leak but it was not properly documented in the maintenance system.

incident_summary: On October 21, 2020 at 0647 hours, Cox Operating operators at EI 259C observed a fire at unmanned platform EI 259A. The fire originated from the diesel generator exhaust. Investigation revealed that a governor actuator linkage failure caused engine overspeed, leading to turbo lockup and oil seal leakage. The air shut off valve failed to fully close due to oil gumming up an o-ring seal, allowing the engine to continue running and increasing exhaust temperature. Carbon buildup in the exhaust was ignited by high temperatures. A preventive maintenance inspection in January 2020 had noted suspected exhaust leak but it was not properly documented in the maintenance system.

incident_summary: On 18 January 2016 at approximately 21:30 hours, a Floorman sustained a crush injury to his left thumb while assisting with laying down a pump in joint assembly using an air hoist on the H&P 105 platform rig. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Dynamic positioning system
Role: Maintain vessel station keeping to prevent movement away from platform
LOD Basis: Primary control to maintain safe vessel position during well operations
supporting_text: ["the FMS Endurance lost its station keeping and started moving away from the EI-355A platform", "the DP system was failing to maintain the vessel's position", "the DP system, gyro and throttles all went out at once due to some type of electrical interference"]
incident_summary: On 1 October 2014 at approximately 17:00 hours, the offshore supply vessel (OSV) Endurance, a dynamically-positioning (DP) capable vessel, lost station keeping while conducting well operations on Well A002 at Eugene Island Block 355A platform. The vessel moved off location, causing the Well A002 production tree to be severed at the wellhead flange and fall overboard. A lubricator connected to the wireline unit fell onto the platform deck, damaging handrails and grating. An estimated 300 ft by 50 ft area of oil was spilled. Investigation revealed that a recently installed communications tower located within 1 foot of the DP system caused electrical interference, resulting in simultaneous failure of the DP system, gyro, and throttles. No injuries were reported.

The compressor had been shut down via E-stop relay, but residual energy in the fan belt was not properly isolated or dissipated. The incident was caused by human error (inattention to task) and failure to implement Stop Work Authority, Job Safety Analysis, and Lockout Tagout procedures.

supporting_text: ["The AI found that stop work should have been implemented and the JSA should have been revised as new potential hazards were introduced due to the handrail's third leg being stuck in the socket.", "Human Performance Error- Not following proper procedure: The construction crew attempted to dislodge the handrail by hand while tension was applied by the crane. As per the Job Safety Analysis (JSA), stop work should have been implemented and the JSA revised as the potential hazards changed from the original JSA."]
incident_summary: On 12 February 2021 at approximately 0900 hours, a Quality Construction and Production crew was removing a heater tube from heat exchanger HBG-700 on Cox Operating's EI 360-E facility. During removal of a handrail, the third leg became stuck in the socket. The crew used a crane with a nylon strap to apply tension while attempting manual removal with hands and hammer. An employee positioned a 5-foot pry bar under the kick plate and stood up using his foot for leverage while another employee pushed the handrail. The handrail broke free under crane tension and struck the standing employee in the face, causing lacerations, fractured nose, and fractured orbital socket. The employee was transported offshore and admitted to hospital for orbital socket surgery on 16 February 2021.

incident_summary: On September 26, 2020, at approximately 1300 hours, a crane operator on Cox Operating's EI 361A facility discovered the boom section closest to the cab was bent. A construction crew was offloading equipment using the platform crane. A 6' x 12' basket of scaffolding weighing approximately 16,300 lb was placed directly under the crane, causing the boom to be oriented at a vertical angle only inches away from the boom angle kick out. The crane operator lowered the load too fast, causing the boom to forcibly contact the boom angle kick out, resulting in structural damage to the crane boom. The estimated damage was $51,600.

incident_summary: On 14-FEB-2019 at approximately 0930 hours, a fire was discovered in the upstairs sleeping quarters of Platform C in the Gulf of Mexico. Two Contract Compressor Mechanics detected smoke while replacing a piston. After approximately 30 minutes, the fire alarm sounded. Four personnel mustered and observed flames in the upper left corner of the sleeping quarters. Believing it to be an electrical fire from the window AC unit, they used a 30 lbs extinguisher followed by a 125 lbs extinguisher to suppress the fire. The mattress and surrounding items were removed through an emergency exit and the mattress was eventually tossed overboard. All personnel received medical treatment with no restrictions. The ignition source could not be determined due to discarded evidence including the mattress and an offshore bag.

incident_summary: On 20 July 2019 at approximately 08:30 hours, a contract welder employed by Smart Fabricators of Texas, LLC sustained a three centimeter laceration to his right hand palm while placing a 2 foot by 2 foot section of metal grating into a scrap iron basket aboard the WFD 250 jack-up rig. incident_summary: On February 11, 2019 at 1640 hours, a helicopter mechanic fell while attempting to retrieve grease guns from the top of helicopter N438PH. The mechanic had recently purchased oversized work boots online that did not fit properly. While standing on a 2.5" x 4" helicopter fold-out step and holding a pitch change link, the mechanic's left foot slipped due to the boot folding inward. The mechanic lost grip and fell backwards, striking the boarding step and heliport deck. A grease gun subsequently fell and struck the mechanic's face. The mechanic sustained a broken coccyx, broken nose, and an 8-stitch laceration above the right eye. The mechanic was wearing a safety helmet and safety glasses which reduced injury severity.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Welded tabs securing walkway to TLQ structure
Role: Structural connection to prevent walkway separation
LOD Basis: Primary structural support for walkway attachment
supporting_text: ["the welded tabs securing the walkway to the TLQ structure were not sufficient to provide a long-term permanent attachment point", "The tabs lacked welded surface area which would have provided additional support for the tabs", "the method in which the tabs were welded was meant to simplify any future demolition or relocation of the TLQ"]
incident_summary: On 16 July 2025 at 1810 hours, an external walkway platform on the third floor of the Temporary Living Quarters (TLQ) on Devils Tower SPAR platform unexpectedly detached from the TLQ structure by 12 to 18 inches while three Nabors employees were standing on it. The walkway remained partially secured by stanchions and stairway bolts. All personnel safely evacuated. Investigation revealed the TLQ, originally designed as temporary housing in 2002, had remained on site for 23 years. The welded tabs securing the walkway lacked sufficient welded surface area and were subject to fatigue and corrosion in the marine environment. Visual-only inspections in May 2024 failed to detect the deteriorated weld condition.

The failed pin, which was bent into a U-shape, struck a Supreme Services employee in the face, causing a subdural hematoma, blunt trauma, an open fracture of the right side of the base of the skull, and multiple closed fractures of facial bones. The incident resulted in a required evacuation via medevac helicopter. Root causes included: (1) installation of a spool riser with an 11.00-inch inside diameter above a seal ring with an 11.44-inch outside diameter, causing the casing to become stuck; (2) the crane operator exceeding the safe working load limit of the casing lifting pin (60,000 pounds) while attempting to lift the stuck casing with the main hoist; and (3) incorrect installation of the two-part sling with D-rings not placed directly adjacent to the casing. Independent testing demonstrated that when D-rings were positioned 3-4 inches from the casing (as they were during the incident), the pin failed at 102,500 pounds, compared to successful performance at 150,000 pounds when D-rings were properly positioned adjacent to the casing.

supporting_text: ["the relief valve installed on the bottle during a preventative maintenance program in December 2023 was rated for 9,000 psi instead of the appropriate 2,400 psi", "Had the cylinders been fitted with the correct relief valve, the over-pressurization could have been avoided", "the installation of a relief device with a burst pressure greater than the cylinder's design pressure contributed to the failure"]
incident_summary: On April 28, 2025, at approximately 10:30 a.m., a nitrogen bottle ruptured and exploded during refilling operations on the BW Pioneer FPSO in Walker Ridge Block 249. The bottle had been mistakenly assumed to be rated for 6,000 psi when it was only rated for 2,400 psi, and was recharged to more than double its maximum capacity. Additionally, an incorrect relief valve rated for 9,000 psi had been installed instead of the appropriate 2,400 psi valve. The explosion caused moderate to severe structural damage to the bottle rack, manifold, diesel fuel tank, and nearby equipment. A crane operator located approximately 18 feet away was struck by flying debris, sustaining minor bruises, a small neck lesion, and temporary hearing impairment. The root cause was over-pressurization due to equipment failure (incorrect relief valve) combined with human error (failure to identify correct pressure rating) and inadequate procedures and training.

The bottle had been mistakenly assumed to be rated for 6,000 psi when it was only rated for 2,400 psi, and was recharged to more than double its maximum capacity. Additionally, an incorrect relief valve rated for 9,000 psi had been installed instead of the appropriate 2,400 psi valve. The explosion caused moderate to severe structural damage to the bottle rack, manifold, diesel fuel tank, and nearby equipment. A crane operator located approximately 18 feet away was struck by flying debris, sustaining minor bruises, a small neck lesion, and temporary hearing impairment. The root cause was over-pressurization due to equipment failure (incorrect relief valve) combined with human error (failure to identify correct pressure rating) and inadequate procedures and training.

Role: Prevent uncontrolled well fluid release during wireline operations
LOD Basis: Primary downhole safety barrier

incident_summary: On 27 April 2020 at 03:00 hours, while C&D Wireline LLC was conducting wireline operations to set Bottom Hole Pressure gauges, the wireline tool string parted at the Surface Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV) at 427 feet. The severed wire was pushed up and out the stuffing box, causing a small release of well gas through the stuffing box pack off assembly and high-pressure grease injection pack off hose. The influx of gas ruptured the small grease seal unit oil reservoir. The well was immediately secured with wireline BOPs and crown valve closure. No injuries or environmental damage occurred.

incident_summary: On November 16, 2020, approximately 458 barrels of synthetic based mud (247 barrels of base oil) were unintentionally discharged into the Gulf of Mexico during fluid transfer operations on the Nabors MODS 200 rig at Ewing Banks Block 873. The discharge resulted from improper valve alignment, specifically an overboard discharge line (dump line valve) left in the open position. Contributing factors included failure of personnel to follow procedures and verify valve alignment prior to pumping, and falsification of documentation indicating verification had been completed when it had not.

incident_summary: On March 11, 2015, the H&P 203 rig accidentally sheared a 5 7/8" drill pipe while setting a test plug. The Blind Shear Rams (BSR's) closed unexpectedly on the drill pipe after a contact switch in the rig floor panel for the close side of the shear ram function was found stuck in the closed position. A rig crew member pressed the 'Push to Operate' button intending to perform a different function, but due to the stuck contact switch, the BSR's fired automatically, shearing the pipe and leaving a test plug, pup joint, and 22.35 feet of drill pipe in the hole.

incident_summary: On 02 February 2020 at 1230 hours, a Motor Vessel engaged in pipeline riser disconnection and abandonment operations at the Ewing Banks 921-A tension leg platform experienced an allision with the platform. The vessel's 300-ton winch became overwhelmed by the pipeline weight, the shackle wedged into the TLP sheave, and despite 100% power application, the vessel was pulled toward the platform resulting in collision. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Wellhead coating maintenance program
Role: Prevent corrosion of alignment pin plug
LOD Basis: Preventive maintenance to protect equipment from corrosive environment
supporting_text: ["The failure of the alignment pin plug is likely due to corrosion. This corrosion was caused by a failure of the coating. The coating was not properly maintained by Fieldwood.", "Fieldwood's failure to maintain proper coatings against the saltwater laden environment led to excessive corrosion and subsequent failure of the alignment pin plug."]
incident_summary: On 10 January 2022, a well control and pollution event occurred at WD 80 D production platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The incident originated from the D-36 well where an alignment pin plug on the tubing head had been leaking gas since 11 January 2021. During a coiled tubing operation to set a plug and clean scale, the tool became stuck downhole and required mechanical extraction, which exacerbated tubing-to-casing communication. This caused the A Annulus pressure to rise from 50 psi to 2000 psi, resulting in excessive fluid returns to the wet oil tank. Approximately 107 gallons (2.5 barrels) of hydrocarbons were spilled. The platform was evacuated and remediation efforts continued through March 2022. Root causes included equipment failure (packer assembly design flaw, alignment pin corrosion due to inadequate coating maintenance), human performance error during coiled tubing operations, and organizational failures in communication and urgency to remediate.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Routine Corrosion Assessment and Maintenance Program
Role: Prevent piping corrosion and loss of containment
LOD Basis: Preventive maintenance is the primary defense against corrosion-induced piping failure
supporting_text: ["Failure of the Operator to perform routine corrosion assessment maintenance on piping was the primary cause of this incident", "The condition of the piping showed evidence of a lack of routine maintenance", "The corrosion that allowed the H2S to leak was a direct result of a lack of routine piping maintenance on the facility"]
incident_summary: On 29-May-2016 at 1310 hrs, a hydrogen sulfide (H2S) gas release of 400 ppm occurred on Main Pass 299-FP Platform during pigging operations on the flowline from MP 299-A to FP Platform. While the crew drained fluids from the pig trap, H2S sensors detected high concentrations of sour gas. The H2S alarm sounded and the platform was shut in. All personnel donned SCBAs and mustered. After an initial all-clear declaration, the H2S alarm sounded again. The response team located a gas leak in the 2-inch pipe outlet from the MBF-935 Buyback Gas Scrubber below the south side cellar deck. Investigation determined the leak was caused by extensive exterior corrosion that compromised the piping integrity. The root cause was identified as failure of the operator to perform routine corrosion assessment and maintenance on piping. The field was subsequently shut in indefinitely as continued operation was deemed uneconomical.

incident_summary: On February 7, 2024, at approximately 09:30 am, a loss time injury occurred when a Danos construction worker attempted to climb an out-of-service scaffolding ladder attached to the ExxonMobil Pipeline Company Motor Control Center/Control Building (ZZZ-006) on the Galveston A 244 JP platform. The ladder, which had been placed out of service on January 1, 2024 with the Self-Retracting Lanyard (SRL) removed and no visual identification tags, shifted when the worker reached the 3rd rung, causing him to jump to the deck and sustain injuries to both legs. The incident resulted from multiple failures including inadequate scaffolding construction, lack of proper out-of-service marking, missing fall protection equipment, inadequate work permits that did not address working at heights, poor communication between operators and contractors, and lack of coordination between multiple contractors on the platform.

supporting_text: ["Step 26 of the OP was where the operators were supposed to 'close & secure receiver door.' The OP listed a process safety advisory, which stated, 'When closing the door, use the [side] gear ratchet and do not over tighten. LOOSEN [SIDE] RATCHET WHEN CLAMP IS TIGHTENED.'", "The operator who closed the door stated that he fully tightened the side ratchet, but did not loosen or disengage the side ratchet binder from the closure door.", "Neither OP-1 nor OP-2 stated that they conducted a visual inspection to ensure the clamp ring was properly secured."]
incident_summary: On November 8, 2017 at 1:18 a.m., an explosion occurred at the 30-inch sales gas pipeline pig launcher on the Enchilada platform (GB Block 128) operated by Shell Offshore Inc. Two operators were preparing to launch a pig when the pig launcher closure door failed explosively, propelling the door and other items overboard. A fire ensued, fed by natural gas from the pipeline. One operator sustained concussion, back injuries, and second-degree burns; the other suffered minor injuries. All 46 platform personnel were safely evacuated. The root cause was incomplete closure of the clamp rings due to procedural failures in securing the closure door, combined with inadequate hazard analysis, improper management of change, and insufficient supervision.

incident_summary: On November 8, 2017 at 1:18 a.m., an explosion occurred at the 30-inch sales gas pipeline pig launcher on the Enchilada platform (GB Block 128) operated by Shell Offshore Inc. Two operators were preparing to launch a pig when the pig launcher closure door failed explosively, propelling the door and other items overboard. A fire ensued, fed by natural gas from the pipeline. One operator sustained concussion, back injuries, and second-degree burns; the other suffered minor injuries. All 46 platform personnel were safely evacuated. The root cause was incomplete closure of the clamp rings due to procedural failures in securing the closure door, combined with inadequate hazard analysis, improper management of change, and insufficient supervision.

incident_summary: On 14 March 2018 at 16:40 hours, a Helix Energy Solutions Roughneck sustained serious injuries to his left hand during well abandonment operations on the Helix Q4000 semisubmersible vessel. While hoisting a wireline valve assembly using a coiled tubing lift frame (CTLF) attached to a multipurpose tower, the worker observed the assembly beginning to shift and potentially contact the CTLF. In an attempt to reposition the assembly, the worker placed his left hand on the static line above the sheave on the CTLF main winch. His hand became trapped between the main winch sheave and the static line cable, resulting in partial amputation of the left thumb and severe laceration of the left ring finger. The worker was evacuated by helicopter and required surgery.

incident_summary: On June 29, 2015 at approximately 1200 hours, a contract employee was injured while attempting to reposition a storage cabinet to be blasted and painted. incident_summary: At approximately 23:00 hours on 23 May 2014, the Diamond Ocean Onyx semi-submersible drilling rig reported a loss of synthetic base mud (SBM) during abandonment operations on Well #1. Initial report of 107 bbl was revised to 61 bbl after strapping calculations. The spill occurred when mud line valves on the Schlumberger Cement Unit were left in the open position after cleaning operations and were not checked prior to the cement job. When the Cementer began pumping cement from Batch Mixing Tank #2, the tank overflowed, causing SBM to spill into secondary containment areas and then into drains leading to the overboard discharge line, which also had open valves. The Toolpusher closed the overboard drain line valves at 23:30 hours, preventing further discharge. No injuries or property damage occurred.

incident_summary: On 3 April 2021 at approximately 1310 hours, a contract chemical employee (IP) was assisting a Production Operator in testing a boarding shut down valve (BSDV) on the Cardamom Field at Shell's GB 426-A platform. While the IP was holding an unsecured hose used to drain residual fluid, the hose became pressurized when the equalizing valve BSDV 2471B was opened at the control panel. The high-pressure release caused the metal fitting on the hose to strike the IP's left hand and back, resulting in a closed displaced fracture of the fifth metacarpal bone. The IP was evacuated to an onshore medical facility for treatment.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Use of OEM certified replacement parts for heavy duty seat puller
Role: Prevent use of non-certified equipment that may fail under load
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["the probable cause of the incident was attributed to a replacement threaded pulling stem that was not an original equipment manufacturer (OEM) certified part required for the HD seat puller", "since the 2-inch threaded stem was not OEM part, it failed during a single rapid overload event"]
incident_summary: On 25 April 2016 at 15:20 hours, an H&P employee was injured while removing a seat from a mud pump cylinder on the H&P 406 rig on Shell's Auger TLP. The crew was using a heavy duty seat puller with a replacement 2-inch threaded stem (not OEM certified) to pull mud pump seats that had been modified with relief grooves. When pressurized to 4500 psi, the non-OEM threaded stem failed and ejected, striking the worker's right hand and causing a fracture. Metallurgical analysis confirmed the stem failed due to overload beyond its design capacity.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Job Safety Analysis (JSA)
Role: Administrative control to identify hazards and safe work procedures
LOD Basis: Pre-job planning and hazard identification
supporting_text: ["the JSA failed to identify potential hazards of impact between the elevators with a stand of drill pipe in the when it is traveling down while a stand of drill pipe is simultaneously being removed from the fingerboard", "The JSA did not include information related to inexperienced employees"]
incident_summary: On 10 December 2021 at 0030 hours, a lifting incident occurred during Well A019 tripping in the hole operations on H&P Rig 406 fixed on Shell's Auger TLP. A single joint of 3.5-inch drill pipe was damaged when the Lower Pipe Racker Operator (LPRO) pulled his end ahead of the Upper Pipe Racker Operator (UPRO), causing the top of the drill pipe stand to get under the elevators as the traveling block was coming down. The pipe bowed and threads were damaged. No injuries occurred. Root causes included LPRO inexperience (second operation, 8 months on rig), inadequate Job Safety Analysis, and inadequate Job By Design Template that failed to specify waiting until block is below fingerboard before removing next stand.

incident_summary: On 10 December 2021 at 0030 hours, a lifting incident occurred during Well A019 tripping in the hole operations on H&P Rig 406 fixed on Shell's Auger TLP. A single joint of 3.5-inch drill pipe was damaged when the Lower Pipe Racker Operator (LPRO) pulled his end ahead of the Upper Pipe Racker Operator (UPRO), causing the top of the drill pipe stand to get under the elevators as the traveling block was coming down. incident_summary: On 29 May 2017, a third-party SubC technician sustained a serious crush injury to his left hand while adjusting Belleville springs on a subsea BOP lower gripper assembly aboard the Noble Don Taylor drill ship. The technician placed his left hand in a gap between the connector plate and landing sleeve for anchoring while using a ratchet extension with his right hand. When the Noble Subsea Engineer actuated the gripper to unlatch and block positions, the connector plate fell and trapped the technician's hand against the landing sleeve. incident_summary: On 10 January 2021 at 15:35 hours, a welder acting as a Rigger sustained a crush injury to his right index finger when he placed his hand on an air hoist cable during lifting operations of a riser insert in the Starboard riser bay hold area. The finger was drawn up and squeezed between the cable and sheave. The injured party was evacuated and diagnosed with a hairline fracture requiring orthopedic surgery. The incident was caused by improper hand placement and contributed to by supervisor failure to request crane use, crew failure to stop the job, and inadequate task assessment in safety documentation.

incident_summary: On 7 June 2017, the Noble Don Taylor drillship under contract to Shell reported an unauthorized discharge of approximately 94.2 barrels of 15.1 ppg synthetic oil base mud during exploration operations on Well DC005 (Cardamom) in Garden Banks Block 427, Gulf of Mexico. The spill was caused by failure of the upper variable bore ram (VBR) aft bonnet door cartridge seal on the subsea blowout preventer. Contributing factors included buildup of debris in the upper VBR body, failure to conduct proper hole cleaning and flushing of the BOP as recommended in NOV Product Information Bulletin released 11 August 2015, and inadequate communication of known risk of loss of seal integrity.
