barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Seal Sub (Mud Pin) installation requirement
Role: Physical barrier to prevent fluid migration
LOD Basis: Cameron LoadKing 3.5 Flange Riser design specification
supporting_text: ["the ultimate root cause of the SOBM spill incident was due to the lack of the installation of a Seal Sub (Mud Pin) in between the flex joint termination spool and first riser joint (Joint #64)", "BSEE visually verified the absence of the Seal Sub (Mud Pin) during an on-site investigation on 7 April 2015, when the BOP was retrieved to the surface"]
incident_summary: On 23 March 2015 at 0505 hours, the Rowan Reliance drill ship under contract to Cobalt International Energy reported a discharge of approximately 2200 barrels of 15.3 ppg synthetic oil base mud (SOBM) during well operations on Well 002 (North Platte #2) in Garden Banks Block 958 at 4846 ft water depth. The primary cause was the failure to install a Seal Sub (Mud Pin) in the flange connection between the flex joint termination spool and the first riser joint (Joint #64). Contributing factors included inadequate procedures, insufficient crew handover, failure to recognize the SOBM buildup during ROV coverage, command structure issues, and under-torqued bolts (1250 psi instead of required 1600 psi). Of the 2200 bbl lost, 1557 bbl entered open water and 643 bbl were lost in open hole. Property damage totaled $151,353.

incident_summary: On March 28, 2021, at approximately 1921 hours, personnel observed a steady flow from the Lower Cement Port 1 on the 38-inch Low-Pressure Wellhead Housing during riserless drilling operations with the Transocean Deepwater Poseidon drillship at Garden Banks Block 962. incident_summary: While removing a gearbox coupling and mud agitator shaft for repair on the T.O. Discoverer India, the injured person (IP) and mechanic deviated from the written risk assessment (WRA) procedure. The IP installed support beams out of sequence and attempted to adjust a safety clamp without first securing it properly. While hammering an Allen wrench onto a corroded bolt head on the agitator shaft, the 750-pound shaft and coupling suddenly separated from the gearbox output shaft and fell, crushing the IP's fourth and fifth fingers between the wrench and support beams. The incident was preceded by three prior unexpected separations of the agitator shaft from the gearbox output shaft (2010, 2014, 2015), but no additional precautionary measures were implemented.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Mud gas separator with liquid leg
Role: Separate hydrocarbon gas from return fluids
LOD Basis: Primary engineering control to prevent gas entering shaker house
supporting_text: ["The drilling crew stated the liquid leg of the mud gas separator was compromised allowing hydrocarbon gas to flow through the mud gas separator into the shakers.", "Liquid leg can be impacted differently with mud, brine, and in this case nitrogen in the circulating system."]
incident_summary: On 15 November 2024 at 0837 hours, a gas release occurred in the shaker house aboard the Valaris DS-16 drillship during well abandonment operations. While displacing the kill line and landing string back to calcium bromide brine after nitrogen displacement, hydrocarbons were released into the shaker house atmosphere. The incident was triggered by a compromised liquid leg in the mud gas separator, allowing hydrocarbon gas to bypass the separator and enter the shaker house. A hydrocarbon alarm activated, prompting evacuation and crew muster. Gas levels returned to zero and operations resumed without further issues.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Drilling control system torque monitoring
Role: Detection of abnormal torque conditions
LOD Basis: Real-time monitoring system
supporting_text: ["the drilling control system observed a consistent torque of 40,000 to 45,000 foot-pounds", "a torque spike of 71.8 kilo foot-pounds force (kft-lbf) occurred"]
incident_summary: On 03 August 2025 at 1934 hours, during drilling of the 26-inch top-hole open water section of Well SP202 at Garden Banks Block 959, a torque spike of 71.8 kft-lbf occurred while drilling salt at 9699 feet depth. This caused a 410 kips reduction in hook load, full loss of torque, loss of hook load, and loss of pump pressure. The drill string separated below the water line. Approximately 4128 feet of 6.625-inch drill pipe fell to the seafloor, with an additional 5350 feet of bottom hole assembly and drill pipe left in the wellbore. The ROV tether was trapped in at least three locations by the fallen pipe. No injuries were reported and no personnel were in the red zone at the time of incident.

barrier_role_match_text:

incident_summary: On 22-November-2019 at Garden Banks 491, during tripping in operations with an 11-7/8" Easy Drill Subsurface Valve, a loss of power to drilling equipment occurred. incident_summary: On August 13, 2020, a Floorhand working in a riding belt on the H&P 100 rig was hoisted to guide wireline tool string out of a lubricator. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Drawworks brake system
Role: Load holding and descent control
LOD Basis: Primary mechanical brake system designed to hold suspended load
supporting_text: ["When the Driller tried to set the brakes on the drawworks, he received a 'Maintenance Torque up Time Out for Internal Brake Release' fault alarm", "The breaks shut, but due to the momentum for the top drive, failed to stop the traveling block from falling to the rig floor"]
incident_summary: On June 9, 2014, while troubleshooting a brake fault alarm on the drawworks system during pipe transfer operations, the Chief E.T. performed a hard reset of drive #4 without confirming that the drawworks brakes were set. Due to miscommunication between the Toolpusher and Chief E.T., the reset interrupted motor control while the top drive was still being held up, causing the traveling block to freefall. The Assistant Driller activated the emergency hydraulic dump, which caused the brakes to slam shut but failed to prevent impact with the rig floor. Damage occurred to drill pipe, the Driller's shack, and the traveling assembly. Root cause analysis identified sticking hydraulic solenoid valves due to debris accumulation and improper hydraulic pump installation.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Manufacturer configuration specification for instrument gas supply
Role: Design specification to prevent incorrect gas supply
LOD Basis: Design control to ensure correct supply medium
supporting_text: ["the manufacturer configured the rental compressor package to use instrument gas for supply. When installed on the facility, personnel connected instrument air"]
incident_summary: On 23-DEC-2018 at approximately 1800 hours, an explosion occurred inside Cooler HAL-4160B on the Allegheny Sea platform (OCS-G 7049, Green Canyon 254-A) operated by Eni US Operating Co. Inc. The explosion was caused by ignition of a lower explosive limit (LEL) mixture of air and natural gas within the process piping. The root cause was that instrument air (instead of instrument gas as configured by the manufacturer) was connected to the temporary rental Booster Compressor CAE-5140A. This allowed continuous air from a Kimray pressure control valve to enter the inlet suction scrubber and mix with gas from the vent system through an open Blowdown Valve (BDV). The explosion separated cooler end caps, damaging the cooler module, PSV, discharge piping, ESD station #7, main deck south guardrail, and other equipment. The ESD station shut in the facility, but the compressor engine continued running until manually shut down by the mechanic after the emergency stop button failed. All personnel mustered safely with no injuries. Contributing factors included incomplete Management of Change (MOC) process, lack of pre-startup review, unavailable operating procedures, and installation not performed as recommended by the manufacturer.

incident_summary: On 04-JUN-2021 at 0930 hours, an ROV Deckman inadvertently placed his left hand in a pinch point between the two cantilever support areas (A-Frame and lower pedestal) of the ROV lifting frame during ROV launch operations on the Seadrill West Neptune. The Deckman immediately contacted the Winch Operator by radio to stop and retract the A-Frame. The Deckman was medivaced to shore for evaluation. Investigation found that the TBRA did not identify the pinch point hazard, the red zone was unidentified, and the Deckman had become complacent during a routine operation.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Crane equipment maintenance and bearing replacement program
Role: Prevent bearing degradation and sheave failure
LOD Basis: Preventive maintenance to eliminate root cause
supporting_text: ["The lessee could not provide documentation indicating the roller bearing had been replaced over a span of 17 years.", "The roller bearing had exterior corrosion and main lubrication seals were not present"]
incident_summary: On September 16, 2019, at approximately 1700 hours, a sheave failure occurred during a critical lift of Mud Tank #1 module (92.5k pounds, approximately 96% of crane capacity) using the port side crane main hoist at Green Canyon Block 338 Platform A. The main line tip Nylatron idler sheave shattered, resulting in dropped objects that struck two injured persons (IPs) attending tag lines. One IP was struck on the right forearm and right boot tip; the other on left middle and ring fingers. Both received first aid and returned to full duty. Investigation revealed corrosion of the outer bearing race and mild side loading during the heavy lift as probable causes. The roller bearing had not been replaced in 17 years, had exterior corrosion, missing lubrication seals, and surface corrosion on the outer race.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Chemical Injection Quills Installation
Role: Prevent concentrated chemical attack on pipe wall
LOD Basis: Primary engineering control to ensure proper chemical distribution
supporting_text: ["no quills were installed into the fittings to enable the injected chemicals to be released midstream into the pipe", "By injecting the chemicals into the piping without the use of the quills, the chemicals concentrated on the interior pipe wall and down the length of the pipe, causing severe deterioration and metal loss"]
incident_summary: On 16-OCT-2015 at 2015 hours, an 18-inch LP pipe ruptured on the Front Runner spar at GC 338A, releasing approximately 25 barrels of oil and undetermined amount of gas. The rupture occurred at the chemical injection point where the line penetrated the production deck. Root cause was severe internal corrosion (metal loss up to 50.4%) caused by acidic chemical injection without proper distribution via quills. Contributing factors included failure to use quills for chemical distribution and inadequate NDT testing that did not cover injection point areas. One employee was injured. Total estimated damage: $1.5 million.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Dynamic Positioning System
Role: Maintain rig position during adverse weather
LOD Basis: Primary control to prevent rig drift
supporting_text: ["the drillship's DP position was compromised into a red alert status due to high winds and a misaligned thruster", "Thruster-2 is located on the port bow and was found to be 180 degrees out of alignment", "When the DP system increased the thrust on Thruster-2, it increased but in the opposite direction, thus going in the same direction as the wind"]
incident_summary: On January 15, 2022, at approximately 16:10 hours, the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse experienced a Dynamic Position (DP) system failure due to misalignment of Thruster-2 (180 degrees out of alignment) during completion operations at Green Canyon Block 389. High winds and sudden wind direction changes caused the misaligned thruster to push the drillship in the same direction as the wind, compromising DP position into red alert status. The Emergency Disconnect Sequence was initiated, unlatching the Lower Marine Riser Package (LMRP) from the Blow Out Preventers (BOP). This resulted in an unauthorized discharge of approximately 420-558 barrels of 15.1 PPG Zinc Bromide (ZnBr2) into the Gulf of Mexico. The incident was contributed to by the operator's failure to complete the submitted Well Suspension Procedure in a safe and timely manner despite knowing inclement weather was approaching.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Over-temperature control module (6050)
Role: Safety shut-off to prevent heater overheating
LOD Basis: Automatic temperature monitoring and shut-off at 250°F
supporting_text: ["the 6050 over temperature module read the real time temperature of the heating elements inside the heater, and the safety shut-off was set to 250 degrees Fahrenheit", "the 6050 control module should have been reading 'open' and not reading any temperature", "the twisted wires mimicked a thermocouple and both controllers detected a safe temperature (97 degrees F), which allowed them to operate under a false reading and prevented the heater from shutting down"]
incident_summary: On May 10, 2018, a fire occurred in the mud pit room of the drillship Transocean Deepwater Asgard while commissioning a Precision Fluid Injection (PFI) System. The fire was caused by incorrect wiring of temperature control modules to the wrong junction box, resulting in false temperature readings that prevented the heater safety shut-off from activating. The heater continued to heat the drilling fluid beyond safe limits, causing the discharge hose to ignite. The fire was detected by flame, heat, and smoke detectors and extinguished with portable fire extinguishers. No injuries were reported.

incident_summary: On May 10, 2018, a fire occurred in the mud pit room of the drillship Transocean Deepwater Asgard while commissioning a Precision Fluid Injection (PFI) System. The fire was caused by incorrect wiring of temperature control modules to the wrong junction box, resulting in false temperature readings that prevented the heater safety shut-off from activating. The heater continued to heat the drilling fluid beyond safe limits, causing the discharge hose to ignite. The fire was detected by flame, heat, and smoke detectors and extinguished with portable fire extinguishers. No injuries were reported.

barrier_role_match_text:

supporting_text: ["A leaking hydraulic seal on the aft pipe adapter cylinder", "As hydraulic pressure was lost, the gripper slowly lost its closing pressure and eventually allowed the casing to slip out of the gripper", "The leaking seal allowed the pipe stop on the gripper to override its setting for picking up the selected size of casing, thereby deactivating the initial setting"]
incident_summary: On April 7, 2017, a joint of 9 7/8" casing was dropped by the Pipe Deck Pipe Handler (PDPH) crane on the Seadrill Sevan Louisiana while being transferred between pipe bays. A leaking hydraulic seal on the aft pipe adapter cylinder caused the gripper to lose closing pressure, allowing the casing to slip during the lift. The crane operator had to periodically re-initialize the casing settings due to the hydraulic leak but did not identify this as a system malfunction. All personnel were in safe zones and no injuries or equipment damage resulted.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Liquid dump valve on 1st Stage Suction Scrubber MBF-1180-B
Role: Prevent condensate accumulation and uncontrolled drainage
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["the operators on duty by-passed the liquid dump valve on the 1st stage suction scrubber MBF-1180-B", "The unattended open by-pass valve was dumping condensate into the skid in order to bring the high liquid level in the scrubber down in order to restart the gas compressor"]
incident_summary: At approximately 2350 hours on May 7, 2014, a fire occurred in the skid of the Flash Gas Compressor-B on the Marco Polo TLP. Two Platform Operators drained condensate from the 1st Stage Suction Scrubber into the compressor skid in an attempt to restart the compressor. Hydrocarbons were released and vapors reached an unknown ignition source, possibly static charge, causing a fire that spread to CLX cable trays throughout the facility. The fire was eventually controlled with AFFF and BC fire suppressants after the firewater system proved ineffective on the secondary fire.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Clamp Type Elevators (CTE) with mechanical slips
Role: Primary load retention mechanism
LOD Basis: Mechanical load-holding device designed to grip casing
supporting_text: ["the CTE opened and released the 42 foot long joint of casing", "the hook load went from 6,000 pounds to 0 pounds and suddenly the CTE released the joint of casing"]
incident_summary: On December 20, 2020, a 4,725-pound joint of 14-inch casing fell across the rig floor of the Transocean Deepwater Invictus drillship after the clamp type elevators (CTE) unlatched when the joint was lowered to the rig floor during troubleshooting of casing slips. The RBC panel operator prematurely functioned the CTE to 'slips up' position during troubleshooting, which allowed the elevators to open and release the casing when weight was removed. No injuries occurred, though an unauthorized Red Zone entry was made by a casing crew member who escaped unharmed.

The sudden stop of the drill string created immense torque that was transferred to the MDDM (Modular Derrick Drilling Machine), causing bolts on two of the I-BOP's actuator plates to be sheared. One plate fell approximately 30 feet to the bridge-racker walkway, and the other fell approximately 130 feet to the rig floor. Each plate weighed 19 pounds. No personnel were in the impact areas and no injuries occurred. The root cause was a combination of poor weather conditions that prevented rig repositioning and equipment failure where downward force from the drill string overcame the hydraulic pressure holding the slips open.

incident_summary: On April 11, 2017, while picking up riser to begin well 003 in Green Canyon Block 627, the riser gantry crane (RGC) on the Diamond Ocean Blackhornet dropped a joint of riser approximately 19 feet in the riser bay. The RGC operator received a false 'Green Light' signal indicating proper capture when the hooks were not fully engaged. When the remote console was powered down for reboot, the aft crane hook lost hydraulics and released the riser joint, which fell approximately 15 feet and struck additional riser joints before falling an additional 4 feet to the bottom of the riser bay. No personnel were injured due to barricading of the work area.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Smoke alarm system in electrical room
Role: Detection and alarm of fire hazard
LOD Basis: Automatic detection system triggered before catastrophic failure
supporting_text: ["On the morning of May 26, 2017, five smoke alarms activated simultaneously in the port 690 V switchboard room, and the general alarm was sounded for personnel to muster.", "While other personnel mustered, the fire team assembled in order to enter the port 690 V switchboard room."]
incident_summary: On May 26, 2017, smoke alarms in the port 690 V switchboard room of the Diamond Ocean Blackhornet drillship triggered a required muster. An arc flash occurred in Dynamic Braking Unit (DBU) 7 due to blown fuses that had not been replaced since May 10, 2017. Carbon buildup in the fuses created a path for electrical current, enabling arcing through an IGBT switch. The arcing destroyed the fuse housing and jumped to the cabinet frame, causing an arc flash that destroyed the cabinet and activated smoke alarms. Estimated damage was $50,000.

The improper connection, combined with 3.3 knot currents, exceeded the load capacity of the connection, causing failure.

incident_summary: On June 27, 2022, at approximately 1755 hours, a fire occurred inside a Pressurized Welding Enclosure (PWE) on the Northeast side of Green Canyon Block 645 Platform A (Holstein). The fire was caused by improperly wrapped Panther felt on Zone 4 deluge wiring, which allowed slag from hot work to smolder inside the wrapping. The fire was discovered when the firewater pump activated and deluge Zone 4 was triggered by the burning wires that controlled the deluge system. Contributing factors included only one Firewatch being present during the 30-minute cool-down period instead of the required two, and fire and smoke detection systems being disabled when the hot work control panel was turned off.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Job Safety Analysis (JSA) Review
Role: Administrative control to ensure crew awareness of hazards and procedures
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["At 18:00 hours a tour change took place and a Job Safety Analysis (JSA) on 'Picking Up Pipe with Knuckle Boom Crane' was reviewed and signed by all participating drill crew members", "Crane Operator was unaware he should hold the gripper closed for 5 seconds as per manufacture's recommendation"]
incident_summary: On February 3, 2021, at 02:00 hours, a Nabors Offshore night Crane Operator operating the West knuckle boom crane on the Holstein Spar Rig was transferring a single joint of 5.5-inch drill pipe (31.5 feet long, 810 pounds) from the pipe conveyor to the pipe rack. The Crane Operator gripped the pipe after only 2 seconds instead of the manufacturer-required 5 seconds, and the gripper feet were not in full contact with the pipe before gripping. When the operator abruptly stopped the boom swing, the pipe gripper shook and bounced erratically, causing the drill pipe to slip out and fall approximately 13 feet onto the pipe rack. The pipe then bounced and slid over the handrail into the Gulf of Mexico. No injuries were reported. Investigation determined the cause was operator error: failure to follow manufacturer's operating instructions regarding grip time and proper gripper foot contact.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Pipe Racking System Indicator Panel
Role: Provide feedback on gripper status to operator
LOD Basis: Primary control to indicate proper gripper engagement
supporting_text: ["The current PRS panel is not equipped with a positive latch indicator that informs the operator that the pipe is completely latched", "The PRS indicator panel displayed that the gripper had been initiated to the closed position"]
incident_summary: On February 14, 2021 at 0330 hours, a stand of 5.5-inch drill pipe fell approximately 4 feet from the Pipe Racking System (PRS) gripper to the rotary table during drilling operations on the Holstein Spar Rig. The PRS indicator panel displayed that the gripper was initiated to the closed position, but the pipe was not actually properly latched. No injuries were reported. The incident was caused by lack of a positive latch indicator on the PRS panel and failure of the rig crew to visually verify that the drill pipe was properly gripped, despite JSA recommendation to do so.

incident_summary: On May 19, 2018, while rigging down blow out preventers (BOPs) and high pressure riser on the A-8 well aboard the Holstein Spar Rig, a lateral support guide frame (LSG) fell approximately 17 feet after two employees removed bolts securing the frame to the riser. The crew removed all nuts from sixteen studs contrary to the planned procedure and the Driller's instructions. The Toolpusher, newly promoted and unfamiliar with the procedure, instructed the crew to remove the bolts before the LSG frame was properly supported. Both employees were injured and evacuated by medivac.

incident_summary: On November 7, 2015, an Engine Room Operator sustained first and second degree burns on approximately 40% of his body when an inspection glass on a Marine Sanitation Device (MSD) vacuum pump ruptured, ejecting hot fluids and fractured glass. incident_summary: On January 15, 2021, at 15:26 hours during drilling operations on the Seadrill West Vela in 6,534 feet of water, an 8-pound leveling cone and a 104-pound bell guide insert fell approximately 90 feet from Frank's International spider type casing elevators to the rig floor. The incident occurred when the casing became wedged between the VersaFlo tool and the bell guide due to slight misalignment. The elevators tilted on the Top Drive System bails, causing the leveling cone to shear off and the bell guide insert to dislodge. No injuries were reported. The incident was caused by misalignment between the VersaFlo tool and bell guide, combined with inadequate secondary retention devices and excessive weight on the elevators.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Verification of correct valve positions before fluid transfer

incident_summary: On September 13, 2017, the Mad Dog Spar Rig unintentionally discharged 71 barrels of Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SBM) into the Gulf of Mexico due to improper valve alignment while cleaning the mud pill pit. The Derrickman and Floorhand used a valve line-up checklist to verify valve positions, but an error occurred. When the overboard discharge valve was opened and the pump activated, SBM was pumped overboard for approximately eight minutes before the pump was turned off and the valve closed. A leaking splitter valve may have been a contributing factor but was not the primary cause.

supporting_text: ["The Diamond Offshore Transfer Permitted Fluid Overboard procedure states to double check the line up by walking the line to confirm appropriate valves are open and others are closed prior to opening the overboard discharge valve.", "The crew 1 Derrickhands failed to properly check the mud system and valve alignments prior to opening the overboard discharge valve."]
incident_summary: On February 27, 2021, approximately 13 barrels of synthetic oil-based mud (SOBM) containing 6.24 barrels of synthetic oil were inadvertently discharged into the Gulf of Mexico during a wash water overboard transfer operation on the Diamond Ocean Black Hornet drilling rig. The discharge resulted from improper valve alignment at the mud mix manifold combined with inadequate communication during a tour change. The #1 mud mixing pump was left running while circulating mud in the #11 and #13 active mud pits, but this critical information was not properly communicated to the incoming crew. When the overboard discharge valve was opened, mud was discharged instead of wash water. The incident was detected when the Driller noticed volume loss in the active mud pits and immediately notified the Derrickhands, who shut down the pump and closed the valve.

The discharge resulted from improper valve alignment at the mud mix manifold combined with inadequate communication during a tour change. The #1 mud mixing pump was left running while circulating mud in the #11 and #13 active mud pits, but this critical information was not properly communicated to the incoming crew. When the overboard discharge valve was opened, mud was discharged instead of wash water. The incident was detected when the Driller noticed volume loss in the active mud pits and immediately notified the Derrickhands, who shut down the pump and closed the valve.

incident_summary: On February 10, 2017 at 0223 hours, a Rowan Floorhand was struck in the back by manual tongs while assisting with pipe tripping operations on the drillship Rowan Resolute in Green Canyon Block 859. A hydraulic hose on the hydra-tong had ruptured, requiring the crew to switch to manual tongs. The manual tongs were suspended by a hoist line and secured by a half-inch tie back rope. As the driller lowered the top drive to latch onto pipe, a clamp on the kelly hose caught the hoist line, hoisting the tongs higher. When the driller stopped the top drive, the tie back rope broke and the tongs swung toward the rotary table, striking the Floorhand who was standing in the permanent Red Zone facing away from the suspended load. The Floorhand sustained a bruise but no broken bones or internal injuries.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Emergency Disconnect System (EDS)
Role: Rapid disconnection from wellhead to allow vessel evacuation
LOD Basis: Automated safety system for emergency well disconnection
supporting_text: ["At 09:40 hours the Asgard reached the red watch circle which was 159 feet off location. The EDS function was activated from the control panel at the Driller's chair, taking approximately 24 seconds to function.", "the Emergency Disconnect System (EDS) was executed successfully"]
incident_summary: On October 28, 2020, the Transocean Deepwater Asgard drillship was forced to evacuate location due to Hurricane Zeta. Despite initial forecasts indicating the storm would pass to the east, the hurricane's actual path brought it directly over the vessel. The decision to remain connected to the well until the last moment, combined with inaccurate weather forecasting, resulted in the drillship being pushed off location by extreme winds (peak gust 152 mph). During evacuation, the marine riser impacted the hull and the LMRP struck the seafloor twice, causing approximately $5.7 million in damage to the riser, LMRP, telescopic joint, mux cables, and subsea pods.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Primary control to prevent uncontrolled descent
LOD Basis: Automated system to prevent brake override and control descent rate
supporting_text: ["The original equipment manufacture's (OEM's) Programing and Control Logic (PCL) was altered at some point", "The OEM programing changed from 'Default' to 'Warning' settings. This allowed three separate system faults: Slack line, Upper / Lower Travel Limit, and Encoder performance / speed resolution to be overridden", "This also prevented the control logic from engaging either of the two load brakes, stopping the initiation of the lift cycle, or stopping the lifting / lowering operation once it was underway"]
incident_summary: On February 17, 2025, an Expro Americas Inc. employee (AP) was lifted approximately 100 feet on the forward man rider to tighten a leaking hydraulic hose on the Coil Tubing Lift Frame (CTLF) aboard the Noble Globetrotter drillship. After completing the task and signaling for descent, the man rider began descending at an accelerated rate. The hoist operator attempted to control the descent by moving the control to hoist position and attempting to activate the emergency stop, but AP reached the rig floor before intervention could be effective. Investigation revealed that the Original Equipment Manufacturer's Programming and Control Logic (PCL) had been altered from "Default" to "Warning" settings, allowing system faults to be overridden and preventing brake engagement. Contributing factors included lack of preventive maintenance, environmental degradation of equipment, inadequate pre-use inspections, and administrative deficiencies in the Control of Work permit.

supporting_text: ["The BSEE Houma District office was notified orally and a written report was submitted within 15 days", "The BSEE investigation found the magnetic heavy lifting device was certified on March 19, 2024, by a 3rd party company, as part of an annual lifting gear inspection", "at the time of that inspection, the equipment was documented to be in good working order", "the magnetic heavy lifting device was damaged and could not lift to its full capacity"]
incident_summary: On December 25, 2024, a 340 lbs steel plate disengaged from a magnetic heavy lifting device on the Noble Drillship Blackhornet while being lowered to the deck. The plate fell 47 inches and struck both a Noble welder (IP1) and a Noble roustabout (IP2) on the top of the left foot. Both personnel sustained swelling and contusions and were evacuated to University Hospital. Investigation determined the magnetic heavy lifting device was defective with damaged pole shoes causing air gaps that reduced lifting capacity. Contributing factors included inadequate job safety analysis, lack of timeout for safety (TOFS), failure to review hazards, and work environment constraints that created time pressure.

incident_summary: On 08-MAY-2025 at 0430 hours, two Transocean employees (2nd Engineer and 3rd Engineer/IP) were transferring nine sections of Hawse piping from deck to a three-tier storage rack using chain falls on the Deepwater Invictus drillship. After placing the eighth pipe section on the top tier, the 2E left to retrieve a U-bolt for securing the pipe while the IP knelt to rig the next section. The unsecured eighth pipe section rolled off the rack, fell approximately 3 feet, and struck the IP's right middle finger, resulting in amputation of the fingertip at the DIP joint. The IP was evacuated by medivac helicopter and treated at University Hospital. Root causes identified include flawed rack design, inadequate hazard analysis in the HIPC, lack of written job procedure, and failure to secure pipe before removing chain falls.

supporting_text: ["The HIPC identified safe lifting practices as part of the plan", "It did not identify chocking or securing the piping before removing the chain falls", "Neither 2E nor the IP were aware of the immediate danger"]
incident_summary: On 08-MAY-2025 at 0430 hours, two Transocean employees (2nd Engineer and 3rd Engineer/IP) were transferring nine sections of Hawse piping from deck to a three-tier storage rack using chain falls on the Deepwater Invictus drillship. After placing the eighth pipe section on the top tier, the 2E left to retrieve a U-bolt for securing the pipe while the IP knelt to rig the next section. The unsecured eighth pipe section rolled off the rack, fell approximately 3 feet, and struck the IP's right middle finger, resulting in amputation of the fingertip at the DIP joint. The IP was evacuated by medivac helicopter and treated at University Hospital. Root causes identified include flawed rack design, inadequate hazard analysis in the HIPC, lack of written job procedure, and failure to secure pipe before removing chain falls.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Riser tensioner system with predetermined set down weight
Role: Maintain adequate tension on riser to prevent BOP stack tilt during wellhead connector unlatch
LOD Basis: Primary control to prevent excessive compression and tilting
supporting_text: ["The Subsea Supervisor adjusted the tensioners from the electrical technician's office located on the drill floor", "The top tension was reduced to approximately 440 KIPS instead of 650 KIPS as had been agreed", "With the top tension reduced to 440 KIPS, the FE models documented that tilting of the BOP stack was the likely outcome"]
incident_summary: On January 14, 2019, during temporary abandonment operations on well SS002 aboard the Seadrill Sevan Louisiana, the riser adapter failed and allowed the BOP stack to fall to the seafloor. The incident occurred when the Subsea Supervisor reduced riser tension to 440 KIPS instead of the agreed 650 KIPS while unlatching the wellhead connector. This reduced tension caused the BOP stack to tilt approximately 45 degrees, placing excessive bending loads on the riser adapter. The riser adapter partially ruptured immediately and fully failed 17 hours later, causing the BOP stack to fall. The well was already secured with multiple plugs and cement, preventing pollution.

Barrier: High Gas Alarm System
Role: Detection and alarm for gas release
LOD Basis: Detects gas release and triggers platform shutdown
supporting_text: ["a high gas alarm on the main deck gas compression skid tripped", "the gas detection system initiating a platform shut-in"]
incident_summary: At approximately 2040 hours on 06-SEP-2018, a high gas alarm on the main deck gas compression skid tripped on the Stampede floating production platform (OCS-G 26313, Green Canyon 468-A). Investigation revealed the gas release was caused by four loose fastening bolts on the sight glass of the Flash Gas Compressor 2-A Gas Filter Coalescer (MAJ-1950A). The loose bolts allowed the gasket to cut out under normal operating pressure (~300 psi), resulting in gas release and platform emergency shutdown. No injuries were reported.

One is a 'push to operate' and the other is the button to actually fire the system.", "The SDPO turned to the EDS panel, and activated the EDS sequence."]
incident_summary: On September 11, 2016, at 0826 hours, the Senior Dynamic Positioning Officer (SDPO) aboard the Diamond Ocean Blackhornet mistakenly activated the Emergency Disconnect Sequence (EDS) while the Blow Out Preventer (BOP) stack was suspended approximately 1,550 feet below the water line during a planned pull to surface. The EDS activation caused the BOP stack to fall approximately 2,330 feet to the sea floor. The incident resulted from human error triggered by confusion between similar acronyms (EDS vs ESD), similar personnel names, lack of pre-job planning, and informal EDS testing practices. The BOP stack landed safely in the designated safe zone approximately 484 feet from the wellhead with no damage to the rig, no personnel injury, and no environmental impact.

Role: Detection and automatic shutdown of compressor
LOD Basis: Automatic safety instrumentation

incident_summary: On March 21, 2017, at approximately 10:00 a.m., a fire occurred on the Green Canyon 680-A Constitution spar platform. The Flash Gas Compressor No.1 shut in on a Level Safety High High alarm on the 1st Stage Scrubber. During restart attempts, the operator discovered a fire between the engine block and starter. The fire was caused by fuel gas escaping from a cracked starter signal return line, which ignited upon contact with a spark possibly caused by the Bendix on the starter engaging the fly-wheel. The operator shut in the platform, activated the general alarm, and extinguished the fire with a fire hose. No reportable injuries occurred. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Protective coating on air fill-up line field welds
Role: Prevent corrosion initiation
LOD Basis: Primary barrier to prevent corrosion of critical buoyancy system components

incident_summary: On December 27, 2017, the Well A-1 riser on the Constitution Spar (GC 680-A) dropped 3-4 feet onto the workover deck grating. On December 31, 2017, during air-up operations on Buoyancy Can Chamber #2, an air leak was detected. The Well A-1 dry tree subsequently descended 28 feet onto the spar deck. Root cause investigation revealed severe corrosion of the air fill-up lines in Chambers #2 and #3, allowing nitrogen to escape and seawater to enter the chambers. Loss of buoyancy in these chambers caused the weight of the dry tree and riser to exceed the buoyancy capacity of the remaining chambers, resulting in structural collapse of the workover deck grating and descent of the wellhead assembly. Estimated damage: $4,700,000.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Protective heat blankets on exhaust piping
Role: Thermal barrier to prevent ignition
LOD Basis: Secondary barrier to prevent ignition of coolant
supporting_text: ["The piping was covered with protective heat blankets. Once the blankets became saturated with the coolant, the hot exhaust from the engine ignited the coolant and caused the resulting fire."]
incident_summary: On April 29, 2017, at approximately 1:12 a.m., a fire occurred on the Green Canyon 680-A Constitution spar platform in the Gulf of Mexico. Vibration from the Flash Gas Compressor No. 2 engine caused stainless steel coolant tubing to rub against a retaining bolt head, creating a hole. Coolant sprayed onto the engine exhaust piping, which was covered with protective heat blankets. Once the blankets became saturated with coolant, the hot exhaust ignited the coolant, causing a fire. Operators responded immediately, shut in the platform, activated the general alarm, and extinguished the fire with a handheld portable fire extinguisher. No reportable injuries occurred.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Flagger communication with Crane Operator
Role: Maintain safe communication during lifting operations
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["The Deck Coordinator was tasked with being the 'Flagger' for the operation. Through the use of hand signals and a handheld radio, his job was to maintain communication with the Crane Operator throughout the lift.", "The Deck Coordinator, for reasons unknown, failed to acknowledge the Roustabouts warning and continued flagging the Crane Operator to come down with the load."]
incident_summary: On 29-OCT-2015 at 1740 hours, the Deck Coordinator sustained severe crush injuries to his left hand while attempting to relocate two joints of damaged production tubing on the Diamond Ocean Blackhornet. The tubing was being set on an uneven bundle of 6 5/8 inch piping in the pipe bay area. Despite warnings from two Roustabouts that the surface was not level and the tubing would roll, the Deck Coordinator continued the operation. When the tubing began to roll, the Deck Coordinator positioned himself between the tubing and a cargo box and attempted to wedge angle iron under the tubing. The weight of the tubing caused the angle iron to flip, crushing his left hand. The tubing continued to roll, pinning him between the cargo box and tubing until the crane lifted the tubing to free him.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Specification of correct seal materials (Viton O-rings)
Role: Prevent installation of incompatible seals
LOD Basis: Material specification control
supporting_text: ["The technician was unaware of a BP policy that only Viton O-rings (Green Tweed 938 elastomer) should be used instead of the Teflon seals", "The correct seals were not installed due to personnel not having knowledge of the importance of the seals"]
incident_summary: On May 8, 2018 at approximately 2115 hours, a high pressure gas release occurred on sales gas meter #3 at the Atlantis platform in Green Canyon Block 787-A. The release was discovered by third party personnel and lasted approximately 30 minutes. The root cause was the installation of incorrect Teflon seals instead of required Viton O-rings (Green Tweed 938 elastomer) during a transmitter replacement 18 days prior. The seal failed at approximately 2100 psi. This was the fourth gas release incident on BP facilities since 2008 and the third on Atlantis, all due to failed Teflon seals. Contributing factors included lack of knowledge by the technician, poor oversight, inadequate training, bypassed gas detectors, insufficient detector placement, and deficient emergency response procedures.

incident_summary: On December 10, 2016, at 05:40 a.m., a flash fire occurred inside the waste oil incinerator tank on the Pacific Drilling Sharav drillship operating for Chevron in Green Canyon block 807. The fire was caused by failure of the waste oil level switch to shut down power to the internal heating element when the waste oil level became too low. The exposed heating element ignited fumes inside the tank, causing a flash fire that distorted and ballooned the tank. One isolation valve was damaged and approximately 1 gallon of waste oil was released to secondary containment. No injuries or environmental threat occurred.

incident_summary: On December 3, 2017 at 2215 hours, a fire occurred at the Fieldwood Energy LLC Grand Isle 116-A platform. During normal operations, operators observed a flame coming from the #2 seawater lift pump electric motor. The fire was caused by a bearing failure in the pump motor that resulted in excessive heat buildup, igniting lube oil in the motor housing. The fire was contained using a purple K fire extinguisher and the platform fire water system. Root cause was identified as inadequate preventive maintenance and monitoring of the seawater pump motors, combined with a lube oil leak that caused the reservoir level to drop.

incident_summary: On 16 July 2019 at 0700 hours, approximately 4.8 barrels of oil leaked from a 3/4" broken tubing connection (Swagelok fitting) on the gas lift bleed ring of the casing valve at Well R-22 on the unmanned GI 22-R platform. The leak was discovered when an operator on nearby GI 22-L observed a sheen north of the platform. The platform had been shut-in since 28 June 2019 for maintenance, but pressure remained on the gas lift line and well casing. High seas from Hurricane Barry (14-16 ft with occasional 21 ft seas) caused well movement that sheared the tubing. Contributing factors included inadequate tubing support, improperly functioning check valves in gas lift mandrels, incorrect installation of a Flow Safety Valve upstream rather than downstream of the tubing connection, and cleanup procedures that exacerbated pollution to a wildlife refuge.

Role: Detection of abnormal liquid levels
LOD Basis: Primary detection system for process control

incident_summary: On January 26, 2018, at the GI 43 AP Platform in the Gulf of Mexico, a loss of containment of hydrocarbons and gas releases occurred. Control Room Operators experienced abnormal operating conditions due to sand and sludge buildup and chemical flocking affecting level sensors. Safety devices were placed in bypass to prevent equipment shutdown while operators flushed level bridles. A hole was discovered in the Gas Vent Header Piping at 1:18 am leaking hydrocarbons. The leak was temporarily sealed with duct tape and absorbent pad. Due to communication failures and personnel falling asleep, the platform continued flowing until 6:45 am when a sheen was observed and a field-wide shut-in was initiated. The incident resulted in a 10-mile sheen and discharge from 5 locations on the platform. Root causes included sludge buildup, collapsed internal packing, failed sump pumps, safety device bypass, and failure to use Stop Work Authority.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Pressure Safety Low (PSL) sensor and automatic shutdown
Role: Detection and isolation of leak
LOD Basis: Automatic detection and response system
supporting_text: ["The pipeline pressure sensor detected the leak, shutting in the platform", "a Pressure Safety Low (PSL) trip on the departing 12\" pipeline, KAH-101 segment #8217"]
incident_summary: On 25 August 2018 at 0730 hours, a 3/8-inch hole developed in a 12-inch departing pipeline (KAH-101, segment #8217) at Fieldwood Energy's Grand Isle 76-A platform, discharging approximately 8.35 barrels (352.27 gallons) of hydrocarbons into the Gulf of Mexico. The Pressure Safety Low sensor detected the leak and shut in the platform. Investigation revealed the pipeline had been converted from gas sales/condensate service to bulk oil service without BSEE approval. Contributing factors included improper Pressure Safety High (PSH) set point at 1200 psi (too close to MAOP of 1203 psi) and improper Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) set point at 1323 psi, both failing to provide adequate overpressure protection. The pipeline pumps' Maximum Discharge Pressure of 1800 psi exceeded the pipeline's MAOP, creating overpressure risk.

supporting_text: ["The IP verified that the 6-inch gas line's analog gauge indicated that there was zero pressure in the line", "SCADA data taken from the Daniel's Meter on the 6-inch gas line upstream of the control valve indicated that there was 143 psi on the 6-inch gas line at the time of the gas release"]
incident_summary: On 25 April 2025 at approximately 1330 hours, a Louisiana Safety Systems, Inc. Valve Technician was performing maintenance on a control valve located on a 6-inch gas line at GI 47 AP platform. While disassembling the valve, a sudden pressurized gas release occurred, causing the valve bonnet flange assembly (approximately 50 pounds) to strike the technician in the left chest and abdomen area. The technician was knocked backwards onto an elevated scaffolding platform and possibly lost consciousness. The technician sustained a laceration behind the ear and an anterior rib fracture of the 8th left rib. The incident resulted from multiple failures including inadequate communication between the operator and contractor, failure to obtain required work permits and PIC approval, failure to verify zero pressure on the gas line through proper energy isolation procedures, use of unapproved scaffolding without fall protection, and non-adherence to manufacturer recommendations for valve disassembly.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Job Safety Analysis (JSA) for crane engine starting system
Role: Identify hazards and control measures for work activities
LOD Basis: Administrative control to identify hazards before work begins
supporting_text: ["The crane engine starting system utilizing natural gas was not covered in the Job Safety Analysis (JSA)"]
incident_summary: On 11-April-2018 at 17:15 hours, a flash fire occurred at the crane engine air intake blower during fuel system bleed-down operations at platform HI 22 A. Two Shamrock Energy Solution Mechanics were performing crane engine overhaul. After exhausting stored air pressure, facility Operators opened a valve sending natural gas to the fuel gas system to supply additional pressure for the starter. On the third cranking of the starter, a flash fire occurred at the engine air intake blower. One Mechanic sustained 1st and 2nd degree burns to hands, arms, face, and ears. The root cause was failure to vent the crane engine starter discharge to a safe area, combined with lack of communication that the starting system utilized natural gas instead of compressed air, and absence of a Management of Change procedure when the facility's air system was modified to a gas system.

incident_summary: On April 5, 2018, at approximately 14:50 hours, a floor hand on the Ensco 75 drilling rig sustained a crush injury to his left ring finger when it became caught between a five-inch drill pipe stand and a pup joint during a spacing out operation. The injured person was using a short piece of manila rope to stabilize the drill pipe instead of available hands-free tools such as push poles or a drill pipe connection guide. The final diagnosis was a fracture to the tip of the left ring finger. The incident resulted in evacuation to shore and loss of time accident (>3 days).

incident_summary: On 20 April 2015 at approximately 1230 hours, a minor fire occurred at W&T Offshore's HI A379 'B' facility in the Gulf of Mexico. Production operators noticed platform lights flickering and discovered smoke and flames emanating from the electrical winding end of the #2 diesel generator. The generator was immediately shut down and extinguished with dry chemical. No injuries or pollution events occurred.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Air intake filter maintenance and inspection
Role: Prevent air filter obstruction
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["Aggreko believes the right bank air filter collapsed due to being clogged"]
incident_summary: On 3-Jan-2014 at 05:15, a fire was detected in the exhaust piping of a rental 1000 KW generator located on the drill deck. The Contract Night Operator spotted flames, announced the fire, and pulled the engine ESD, which shut down the engine and shut in the platform. A second operator used dry chemical extinguishers and water to suppress the fire. Root cause was determined to be a collapsed/clogged right bank air intake filter that caused incomplete fuel combustion in the cylinders, leading to fuel burning in the exhaust and overheating of the turbo. No injuries or pollution resulted.

incident_summary: On October 1, 2015 at approximately 1240 hours, a contract employee sustained a minor injury at Black Elk Energy's High Island A442 A facility. The injured party had finished applying silicone caulk around a newly installed window air conditioning unit and was descending a 4' step ladder when he lost his footing (slipped). When the injured party fell, he landed on a lifting pad eye on the potable water skid which was adjacent to the 4' step ladder, striking the left side of his ribs. X-rays determined the ribs were bruised but not broken. The injured party did not return to work for the remainder of his 14 day work schedule, resulting in a lost time accident (LTA) of 8 days.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Visual verification of complete pipe joint separation
Role: Prevent incomplete separation from being lifted
LOD Basis: Primary control to prevent the threat from occurring
supporting_text: ["The crew failed to verify complete separation of the joint of pipe before signaling the Crane Operator to lift the pipe.", "personnel were distracted while discussing other equipment and failed to visually verify the complete separation of the tool joint as it was being lifted"]
incident_summary: During Plug and Abandonment operations at High Island A 446 A on 4-DEC-2019 at 0704 hours, a crew member unscrewed the top joint of a 3-1/2 inch work string. When the crane operator lifted the unscrewed joint, it became caught on the last thread section, causing the bowls and slips to rise and unseat. Upon release, the remaining work string fell back down at an angle. The safety clamp broke loose during impact, allowing the bowls to open and the remaining work string (approximately 346 feet) to fall down the hole. The work string impacted the cement retainer 72 feet below mudline. Root cause was failure to verify complete separation of the joint before signaling the crane operator to lift. Contributing factors included failure to document blind lifts in the JSA, failure to secure bowls to the Pacman plate, failure to secure slip handles, crew distraction during the lift, and inadequate procedures addressing blind lifts.

supporting_text: ["the previous lifts were blind lifts and were not discussed or addressed in the Job Safety Analysis(JSA)prior to conducting the job task", "The crew failed to document on the Job Safety Analysis (JSA) they would be conducting blind lifts"]
incident_summary: During Plug and Abandonment operations at High Island A 446 A on 4-DEC-2019 at 0704 hours, a crew member unscrewed the top joint of a 3-1/2 inch work string. When the crane operator lifted the unscrewed joint, it became caught on the last thread section, causing the bowls and slips to rise and unseat. Upon release, the remaining work string fell back down at an angle. The safety clamp broke loose during impact, allowing the bowls to open and the remaining work string (approximately 346 feet) to fall down the hole. The work string impacted the cement retainer 72 feet below mudline. Root cause was failure to verify complete separation of the joint before signaling the crane operator to lift. Contributing factors included failure to document blind lifts in the JSA, failure to secure bowls to the Pacman plate, failure to secure slip handles, crew distraction during the lift, and inadequate procedures addressing blind lifts.

incident_summary: On June 12, 2015 at 20:00 hours at platform HI A 537 B, during evacuation due to approaching tropical storm, a crane operator picked up a Billy Pugh personnel basket carrying four personnel. While swinging the basket over the helideck and lowering it toward a boat, the crane boom continued to swing northward despite the operator attempting to stop it with the swing brake. The crane was operating at 75 percent power instead of full throttle as designed. The operator raised the basket to place personnel back on deck, but the basket struck the crane boom cradle and became entangled. One person fell approximately 10-12 feet onto a Marine Portable Tank, landing on face and stomach and losing consciousness. A second person fell on the boom cradle sustaining minor injuries. Two remaining personnel stayed on basket and climbed down using boom cradle.

incident_summary: On 24-MAR-2014 at 1630 hours, during stringing down block operations on Platform D (Blake 1505 rig), a 1 3/8 inch drill line cable failed to remain in the snake grip and fell, striking a worker manning the hydraulic drill line spool winch control on the left shoulder and right hip. An 'all stop' was called but insufficient time remained to prevent the incident.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Before Job Safety Analysis (BJSA)
Role: Administrative control to identify hazards and appropriate tools before work begins
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["The use of a grinder was not discussed in the Before Job Safety Analysis (BJSA)."]
incident_summary: On 06-Jun-2014, a contract employee of Infinity Valve working for McMoRan was tasked with swapping out a Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) by removing mounting bolts. A grinder with a cutting wheel was used in a small work space with limited visibility instead of a reciprocating saw. The grinder was not discussed in the Before Job Safety Analysis (BJSA). The employee was not comfortable using the grinder but did not invoke Stop Work Authority to complete the job quicker. The cutting wheel became bound in the confined space, causing the grinder to kick back and shatter the wheel. The blade contacted the back of the employee's left hand below the ring finger, causing a 4-inch laceration. The employee received 30 stitches and is expected to make a full recovery.

Root causes included inadequate Job Safety Analysis, lack of established safe standing zones, inefficient crew communication, absence of required supervision, and equipment failure of the Top Drive grabber dies.

Barrier: Combustion Analyzer monitoring of O2 levels

supporting_text: ["hooked up a Combustion Analyzer to the exhaust stack to monitor Oxygen (O2) levels", "The SCADA Technician was monitoring the O2 level. The SCADA Technician suspected something was wrong and signaled the Operator to shut down the AH"]
incident_summary: On 16 March 2015 at approximately 14:48 hours, a backfire/explosion occurred at Chevron USA's High Island A582 'C' facility during platform startup after monthly Emergency Shut Down testing. Production Operators were bringing the platform back on line when the purge timer on Ambitrol Heater EAL-1510 was observed not counting down. Investigation revealed an inadequate purge of air within the Ambitrol Heater Cabin before ignition, allowing an explosive fuel mixture to accumulate. The air/fuel ratio valve linkage was initially disconnected, and the Programmable Logic Controller allowed excess fuel introduction during ignition. The unit backfired and dislodged the heater cover. No personnel injury, environmental incident, or fire resulted.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Tri-Gas Testing and Continuous Monitoring
Role: Atmospheric hazard detection
LOD Basis: Initial atmospheric assessment and continuous monitoring to detect oxygen deficiency, hydrogen sulfide, and lower explosive limit
supporting_text: ["No Tri-gas survey was conducted when the Heater Treater vessel hatch was initially opened, and to continuous monitoring for Tri-gas readings", "No Tri-Gas (O2, H2S, LEL) initial and continuous monitoring conducted"]
incident_summary: On September 4, 2023, at approximately 09:15 hours, an injured person (IP) entered the NBK-1070 Heater Treater vessel on platform HIA 379 B without a Supplied Air Respirator (SAR), wearing only an Air Purifying Respirator (APR). The IP was overcome by toxic fumes and passed out face down in the vessel fluids and sludge. A pump operator noticed the IP missing and alerted the cleaning crew supervisor. The supervisor entered without a respirator, was also overcome, and had to exit. A construction supervisor then entered without respiratory protection, extracted the IP, and CPR was administered for approximately 20 minutes before the IP began breathing. The IP was evacuated by helicopter and treated in intensive care for three days. The incident resulted from failure to follow the confined space entry plan, including no tri-gas testing, no hole watch, improper PPE, and no rescue equipment setup.

incident_summary: On June 2, 2023, at 12:30 hours, a crane boom contacted the boom stop at High Island A 382 F facility during crane operations. The boom was raised to too high an angle, causing the load block to swing into the crane boom and damage the heel section cords. The estimated replacement cost was $50,000. No injuries or pollution were reported. The incident was attributed to crane operator inattentiveness regarding boom angle and failure of the high angle kick out safety system.

incident_summary: At 0955 hours on 2 October 2019, a Pipeline Technician sustained a bicep tendon tear while manually lifting a one-inch high pressure hose from a suspended 4'x4'x3' metal basket on the High Island A 474 A facility. The basket was suspended by crane approximately two to three feet off the deck and positioned half on another basket and half over the deck. The work area was congested with other baskets, bottle racks, and equipment. The injured person felt a 'pop' in his upper right arm while reaching over the top edge of the basket to retrieve items. The technician required surgical intervention.

Barrier: Mechanical Integrity Program
Role: Prevent pipe corrosion and perforation through inspection and maintenance

incident_summary: On May 26, 2022, BSEE inspectors observed a sheen approximately 30 yards by 200 yards in Gulf of Mexico waters originating from Cox Operating's High Island A 582 C facility. Investigation determined hydrocarbons were discharging from the incoming KAQ 2700 high pressure 8-inch wet gas pipeline segment number 13380 at the plus 10 deck level. The source was identified as a one-quarter inch diameter hole in the pipe due to corrosion. An estimated total of 129.145 gallons of oil was discharged during the bleed down process. The pipeline was repaired with a clamp and returned to service on June 17, 2022.

incident_summary: On 14-OCT-2014 at 01:05 hours, during preventive maintenance on the Aft Pipe Racker System, a Short Service Employee was hoisted in a Billy Pugh Derrick Basket to a height of approximately 110 feet. The basket hung up on an unknown part of the derrick/PRS causing it to tilt. Despite radio communication of an 'all stop' command, the basket continued to climb 3-4 inches before stopping. A loud 'pop' was heard and the rider discovered major damage to the single main support arm and two lower arm braces had separated. The rider used the anchor line for support and called for emergency descent. When the basket reached the rig floor, the hanging arm completely separated from the basket.

incident_summary: On 14-OCT-2014 at 01:05 hours, during preventive maintenance on the Aft Pipe Racker System, a Short Service Employee was hoisted in a Billy Pugh Derrick Basket to a height of approximately 110 feet. The basket hung up on an unknown part of the derrick/PRS causing it to tilt. Despite radio communication of an 'all stop' command, the basket continued to climb 3-4 inches before stopping. A loud 'pop' was heard and the rider discovered major damage to the single main support arm and two lower arm braces had separated. The rider used the anchor line for support and called for emergency descent. When the basket reached the rig floor, the hanging arm completely separated from the basket.

incident_summary: On 1 April 2015, the SeaDrill West Vela drill ship under contract to BP reported a dark area identified by ROV approximately 400-500 feet from the wellhead at Well 001 (Tiber #3) in Keathley Canyon Block 147. Samples from the seafloor showed gelatinous material resembling filter cake with 2% oil content, consistent with Synthetic Based Drilling Mud used during the 16.5 inch hole section drilling. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Work Risk Assessment Procedure
Role: Identify hazards and control measures before work begins
LOD Basis: Established Transocean procedure for evaluating task hazards

incident_summary: At approximately 08:10 hours on 6 January 2016, a joint of drill pipe fell from the aft pipe transfer conveyor onto the aft pipe deck of the Transocean Discoverer India drill ship during drilling operations for Chevron U.S.A. in Keathley Canyon Block 770. The drill pipe became trapped under a utility cart during loading arm operations, causing it to roll and fall over handrails, creating a 5-inch diameter hole in the aft deck. No personnel injuries occurred due to area barricading. The incident resulted from failure to follow work risk assessment procedures, inadequate safety time-outs, and lack of hazard assessment for utility cart positioning.

barrier_role_match_text:

incident_summary: At 20:20 hours on 30 August 2014, the Transocean Discoverer Clear Leader drill ship under contract to Chevron was conducting drilling operations at Keathley Canyon Block 829 when a loss of synthetic base mud was observed from a telescopic slip joint packer failure. Approximately 12 barrels of 14.5 ppg SBM (6.12 gallons synthetic oil) was spilled into the Gulf of Mexico but was restricted to the moon pool area. Root cause was a leaking stainless steel JIC connection for the air hose at the bottom of the J-plate, with contributing factors including improper hose support, ocean currents causing vibration, and submersion of hose bundle below water line.

incident_summary: On 7 August 2019 at 04:30 hours, a Seadrill subsea technician sustained an eye injury while working on the hands-free gooseneck (HFGN) during well operations. incident_summary: On 7 August 2019 at 04:30 hours, a Seadrill subsea technician sustained an eye injury while working on the hands-free gooseneck (HFGN) during well operations. While attempting to retract the kill line HFGN locking pins, an indicator rod was ejected by trapped air and water pressure from within the operator housing, striking the technician in the right eye. The technician was diagnosed with orbital fractures and bruising. The incident resulted from operation of out-of-compliance equipment with known defects, failure to follow OEM guidelines, creation of unsafe workaround procedures, and failure to account for trapped hydrostatic pressure.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Smoke detection system in switchboard room
Role: Early detection of fire
LOD Basis: Detection system activated before visible fire
supporting_text: ["At approximately 21:00 hours on 28 May 2019, two smoke detectors sounded off when smoke was detected in the DO Ocean Blackhornet's Starboard 690V Drilling Switchboard Room"]
incident_summary: On 28 May 2019 at 21:00 hours, smoke detectors activated in the Starboard 690V Drilling Switchboard Room aboard the Diamond Offshore Ocean Blackhornet drillship. An electrical fire occurred in the Aux Drawworks B (ADWB) motor drive cabinet, causing severe damage to two Inverter Modules and two Variable Frequency Drives. The general alarm was activated and all personnel reported to muster stations. No injuries were reported. The assessed damage was approximately $180,000. The root cause could not be definitively determined, but possible causes included a faulty Inverter Module, cooling water leakage, or improperly torqued DC fuses.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Vented enclosure design for rechargeable batteries
Role: Prevent hydrogen accumulation in enclosed spaces
LOD Basis: API RP 14F and API RP 500 requirements for battery installation
supporting_text: ["The installation of rechargeable batteries inside a non-vented panel creates a Class 1 Division 1 location inside of the panel according to API RP 500", "All rechargeable battery systems should be installed such that hydrogen cannot collect in sufficient quantity to create a hazard"]
incident_summary: An explosion occurred on 14 February 2019 at 0115 on the Mississippi Canyon 109 A (Amberjack) Platform. The explosion originated from a 12 Volt battery located inside a Remote Terminal Unit (RTU) Panel used for gas sales measurement and communication. A rechargeable battery had been overcharged due to improper replacement and installation in a non-vented enclosure, causing hydrogen gas accumulation and ignition of electrical components. The panel door was blown off and debris landed 45 feet away. No injuries or pollution occurred. The root cause was flawed equipment design and installation of non-rated electrical components in a hazardous area, compounded by inadequate personnel training on battery hydrogen venting and lack of hazard analysis during design changes.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Double check valve alignment before pumping
Role: Prevent incorrect valve alignment leading to unintended discharge
LOD Basis: Job Risk Assessment requirement
supporting_text: ["without double checking valve alignment as stated in the Job Risk Assessment, the overboard discharge valve was unlocked", "Failure of the Derrick Man and Assistant Derrick Man to verify valve alignment prior to pumping the displacement sea water overboard"]
incident_summary: On 02 July 2015 at 0400 hours, the Rowan Relentless drill ship inadvertently discharged 368 barrels of Synthetic Based Mud (SBM) containing 67 percent synthetic oil into Gulf of Mexico waters. The discharge occurred when the Derrick Man, without verifying valve alignment, pumped displacement seawater overboard from pits #12 and #14. A manually operated splitter valve separating reserve pit #1 from these pits was left halfway open, allowing SBM to flow into the overboard discharge line. Root causes included poor communication between parties, failure to verify valve alignment prior to pumping, and lack of awareness of company policy regarding sensitive valve lockout procedures.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Master dump valve for Trip Tanks
Role: Primary containment barrier to prevent overboard discharge
LOD Basis: Physical barrier designed to isolate discharge line when closed and locked
supporting_text: ["the master dump valve for the Trip Tanks was in the locked and open position, allowing 61 bbls of SBM flow into the overboard discharge line", "This valve is a barrier in preventing drilling fluids from discharging fluids into the GOM when in the closed position", "The pit cleaning crew may have locked open the master dump valve and never returned it to its normally closed and locked position"]
incident_summary: At approximately 0055 hrs on 1 April 2019, LLOG Exploration Offshore LLC experienced a spill of 61 barrels of Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SBM) on the Seadrill West Neptune Drillship during drilling operations at Mississippi Canyon Block 199. The spill resulted from a misalignment of trip tank valve positions. While the Derrickman was transferring mud between trip tanks per driller request, the master dump valve for the Trip Tanks was found to be locked in the open position, allowing 61 bbls of SBM to flow into the overboard discharge line into the Gulf of Mexico. The pit cleaning crew had possibly locked open the master dump valve during cleaning operations and never returned it to its normally closed and locked position. Total pollution was 37.8 bbls (61 bbls at 62% synthetic make-up).

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Fail-Safe Shutdown Sequence
Role: Automatic shutdown of wells upon loss of communication
LOD Basis: Automatic response to loss of control signal
supporting_text: ["The loss of communication initiated a fail-safe shutdown sequence that successfully shut in associated wells."]
incident_summary: On 12 November 2019 at 1200 hours, a loss of communication to MC 208/209 SS001 well occurred at Murphy Exploration & Production Company's MC 254 A Delta House platform. This resulted in catastrophic failure of the satellite umbilical cobra head termination with rupture of one HP and two LP hydraulic supply lines. The root cause was heavy weight and center of gravity offset from the UTH assembly resulting in high shear and bending moment on the MQC plate beyond its reported capacity, with collapse of bolts on the MQC bracket. Contributing factors included temporary buoyancy modules left on cobra heads that were not designed for long-term subsea use, and EFL grounding to the UTH. Approximately 1,000 gallons of hydraulic fluid was released, below EPA reportable threshold. The fail-safe shutdown sequence successfully shut in associated wells.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: High angle kick-out (boom hoist high limiter)
Role: Prevents crane boom from exceeding maximum angle and striking boom pad
LOD Basis: Primary mechanical safety device designed to stop boom at maximum angle
supporting_text: ["the high angle kick-out or boom hoist high limiter on the crane body was found to be adjusted all the way in, rendering this safety device non-operational", "With the high angle kick-out adjusted in all the way, there were no other safety measures in place to stop the crane boom from striking the boom pad and damaging the boom heel"]
incident_summary: On 9-JAN-2015 at approximately 0600 hours, during a routine lift operation on the NABORS MODS 200 crane at Platform B SIMBA in MC Block 21, the crane boom struck the boom pad causing structural damage to the boom heel. Investigation revealed that the high angle kick-out (boom hoist high limiter) safety device was adjusted all the way in, rendering it non-operational. This device is designed to prevent the crane boom from striking the boom pad. Both day and night crane operators had initialed Daily/Pre-Use Crane Inspection forms from 7-JAN-15 through 13-JAN-15 verifying the high angle boom kick-out was checked for proper operation, despite the device being inoperable.

incident_summary: On 18 October 2020 at 0820 hours at MC 243 A (Matterhorn), a crane mechanic's finger was crushed while transporting a 55-gallon drum of hydraulic oil across the platform deck using a hand dolly. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Uninterrupted Power Supply (UPS-3451)
Role: Backup power supply to maintain critical systems during primary power loss
LOD Basis: Secondary power system to prevent loss of critical safety functions
supporting_text: ["the Uninterrupted Power Supply (UPS) failed to supply backup power", "Crew members found that UPS-3451 malfunctioned", "The UPS system failed due to shorted capacitors and undersized fuses on the inverter capacitor bank"]
incident_summary: On 6 May 2019 at 0748 hours, Shell Offshore Inc. MC 437-A (Appomattox) platform experienced a partial loss of primary generator power during a fuel swap operation from diesel to natural gas. The Uninterrupted Power Supply (UPS-3451) failed to provide backup power due to shorted capacitors and undersized fuses on the inverter capacitor bank. This caused shutdown of the fire and gas processor, preventing emergency generator startup. The UPS was bypassed and power restored. A second power loss occurred at 2147 hours but the emergency generator restored power within 30 seconds with the UPS in bypass. Root cause was identified as turbine generator trip combined with UPS-3451 failure. No injuries or pollution resulted. The facility had not commenced first oil production at the time of the incident.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Flame detector in ZAN-700 turbine generator enclosure
Role: Detection of fire initiation
LOD Basis: Early warning system enabling rapid response
supporting_text: ["Flame detectors installed in the ZAN-700 turbine generator enclosure alerted the control room", "the control room received an alarm from a flame detector located inside the ZAN-700 turbine generator enclosure"]
incident_summary: On 30 November 2021 at 1917 hours, a fire occurred in the ZAN-700 turbine generator enclosure at the Appomattox semi-submersible production platform. Flame detectors alerted the control room, and a deck operator confirmed smoke and sparks from the generator housing. The generator ESD was activated, platform ESD initiated, and full muster sounded. Fire teams extinguished the fire with water. Investigation determined that vibration caused loss of fastener preload in the exhaust hatch bolts, allowing hot exhaust to escape and ignite Visqueen (plastic tarp) left by painters, which then melted cables. No injuries, explosions, or environmental harm occurred.

No injuries, explosions, or environmental harm occurred.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Visual verification of chain lock position
Role: Procedural check to confirm mooring chain is secured
LOD Basis: Primary human-based control to prevent unplanned payout
supporting_text: ["The MGP maintenance crew assumed the mooring chain was in the lock position when work was completed. The MGP maintenance crew did not visually observe the chain in a lock position.", "the MGP's maintenance crew failed to check if the RRW roller assembly upper chain stopper pawls were engaged"]
incident_summary: On 29 January 2020 at 1400 hours, a Roller Ram Winch (RRW) mooring chain incident occurred at Shell Offshore Inc's Appomattox platform in Mississippi Canyon Block 437. During maintenance activities on mooring line L-11, the chain links suddenly began paying out from the chain locker. The guide roller broke free from its bearing cradle, and 22 chain links paid out outboard. The chain stopper cylinders, angle transmitter, and Slack Chain Hang-off Unit (SCHU) stopper were forced out of frame. The root cause was identified as human performance error: the maintenance crew assumed the mooring chain was in lock position without visually confirming it. Contributing factors included flawed equipment design lacking proximity switches and programmed interlocks to confirm SCHU stopper extension. Total repair and recovery costs were $5,750,000.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Isolation valve M4220-77H1
Role: Prevent pressurized chemical release
LOD Basis: Primary containment barrier for pressurized line
supporting_text: ["Upon opening isolation valve M4220-77H1, asphaltene inhibitor sprayed out of the flow element's weep hole", "A loose tubing fitting between the valve and the flow element"]
incident_summary: On 17 April 2021 at 1415 hours, a stack operator was contacted with asphaltene inhibitor on the arms and face while attempting to open chemical injection valves to Well AC-006 on the Appomattox platform. The incident was caused by a loose tubing fitting between an isolation valve and flow element FE 4220-H1, which resulted in chemical spray from the weep hole. The operator was wearing inadequate PPE (no chemical resistant gloves or face shield). The injured person was initially released to full duty by the medic but experienced eye irritation the next day and was evacuated. An onshore physician diagnosed chemical conjunctivitis and ordered 7 days off work, resulting in an LTA > 3 days classification.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Spider Dog Safety Door Retention Chain
Role: Secondary retention to prevent grating from falling
LOD Basis: Secondary mechanical restraint
supporting_text: ["Inadequate secondary retention chain for securing the grating on the riser spider", "The chain was attached to the bracket (which secured the safety grating spider dog) and spider dog by one of the 4 bolts. The chain rapped around the spider dog and is designed to prevent the spider dog safety grating from falling away from the well center to the work platform while the dogs are in the open/vertical position. Since the chain was bolted to the bracket and the bolts were sheared, this allowed the grating to fall into the well center", "Inadequate secondary safety for securing the spider dog safety door to the spider dog; the chain prevents the spider dog safety door from falling into the work platform area but does not retain the spider dog safety door from falling towards well center and to the moon pool"]
incident_summary: On October 31, 2020 at approximately 17:58 hours, a spider dog safety door (grating) fell through the rotary table to the top of the Blow Out Preventers (BOP) on the SeaDrill West Neptune drillship at Mississippi Canyon 503, Well #007, injuring two floor hands. The safety door was dislodged when spider dogs were functioned to the open position while back plates remained in the down position, causing bolts securing the grating to shear. The inadequate secondary retention chain failed to prevent the grating from falling into the well center. One injured person sustained facial lacerations and the other a fractured rib.
