barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Isolation valve design and installation
Role: Prevent hydrocarbon release from pressurized pipeline
LOD Basis: Primary containment barrier
supporting_text: ["A 1/2\" isolation valve on a pressurized pipeline pump's discharge piping developed a crack", "BSEE and LLOG conclude the isolation valve was not installed with proper support", "The lack of support and high vibration service caused fatigue damage"]
incident_summary: On 14 July 2019 at 21:50, a fire occurred on LLOG's MC 547 A platform when a 1/2" isolation valve on Pipeline Pump A's discharge piping developed a crack. Hydrocarbons sprayed onto the hot turbo exhaust system and ignited. The TSE activated the ESD system, all production shut in, and firewater pumps activated. A 3-man fire team extinguished the fire in 16 minutes. No injuries occurred, but pollution was detected on deck and structural supports. The root cause was improper installation of the isolation valve without adequate support, causing fatigue damage from high vibration service.

incident_summary: On 13 September 2019 at 1530 hours, an injured person sustained a laceration and fracture to the left index finger while assisting with installation of a flow assurance pump on Deck 7 of the Gulfstar 1 platform. The injury occurred when an air-operated chain hoist jerked upward while the IP was using an alignment tool to position piping flanges, causing the IP's hand to be pinched between the piping and alignment bar. The incident was attributed to inadequate hazard analysis in the Job Safety Analysis, congested workspace, poor hand placement, and the jerking motion of the air hoist.

incident_summary: On 13 February 2015 at 1115 hours, a 260 barrel discharge of 15 ppg Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SOBM) occurred at MC 762 on the Stena Forth. The Derrickman intended to transfer salt water from active pit #2 to active pit #4 but was unaware that SOBM from equalized active pits #1 and #3 had flowed into active pit #2 due to failure of the air actuator for active pit #2 and the suction valve for active pit #3. Poor communication between the Derrickman and Mud Loggers, combined with equipment failure and lack of continuous pit monitoring, resulted in the unauthorized discharge.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Risk Assessment During Planning and Review Phases
Role: Identify and mitigate risks associated with fluid loss control device shearing
LOD Basis: Procedural development and line-by-line review should identify risks before execution
supporting_text: ["The risk, consequences, and mitigations associated with the inadvertent shearing and opening of the fluid loss control devices were not adequately assessed during the planning and review phases of the completion.", "Within the Kosmos Management System, there are opportunities for this risk to be addressed during the procedural development phase and the line-by-line procedural review, as well as during the Complete Well on Paper (CWOP)."]
incident_summary: On January 9, 2021, at approximately 06:00 hours, Kosmos Energy experienced a well control incident on Seadrill West Neptune during completion operations on the SS003 well. While displacing wellbore completion fluid to packer fluid, the downhole Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD) unintentionally sheared the upper and lower Fluid Loss Control Devices (FLCD), resulting in cross flow between completed intervals and fluid losses of 600 barrels per hour. The well was subsequently shut in and killed over a multi-day operation. The incident was attributed to inadequate risk recognition, poor communication, insufficient execution and supervision, and incomplete procedures.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Engineering design specifications for lifting equipment placement
Role: Prevent load deviation from planned lift path
LOD Basis: Primary control to ensure safe load positioning
supporting_text: ["the specific placement of the lifting equipment was not followed as required by engineering designs", "this resulted in the hoisting and traveling of the load to veer from the planned lift path and elevation"]
incident_summary: On 6 March 2015 at 1615 hrs, during installation of the West Gas Lift Mezzanine Deck (6,500 pounds) on Murphy's MC 736 A Platform, the load shifted and jammed between high pressure (1300 psi) gas lift piping and a 4" isolation valve. The incident resulted from failure to follow engineering design specifications for lifting equipment placement, non-use of hold back hoist and tag lines, and non-installation of stabbing guides. Attempts to unjam the load caused the gas lift piping to bow and create a 5/16" gouge in the isolation valve body. No injuries or hydrocarbon releases occurred. Property damage exceeded $50,000.

incident_summary: On 6 March 2015 at 1615 hrs, during installation of the West Gas Lift Mezzanine Deck (6,500 pounds) on Murphy's MC 736 A Platform, the load shifted and jammed between high pressure (1300 psi) gas lift piping and a 4" isolation valve. The incident resulted from failure to follow engineering design specifications for lifting equipment placement, non-use of hold back hoist and tag lines, and non-installation of stabbing guides. incident_summary: On 7 July 2014 at 1940 hours during workover operations on the Seadrill West Vela platform in MC Block 776, a tubing hanger handling tool (THHT) separated from a tubing hanger at approximately 35 degrees angle while being transported. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Workover Fluid Cleanup Verification Procedure
Role: Prevent mixing of workover and process fluids
LOD Basis: Primary control to prevent hazardous fluid mixture
supporting_text: ["BP decided to place the well into production before verifying that the workover fluids were cleaned up", "BP placed the MC 822 TD003 S45 subsea well into production before verifying that the workover fluids were cleaned up"]
incident_summary: On 13 July 2020 at 1055 hours, a pollution event occurred at BP's Thunder Horse platform (MC 778-A) in the Gulf of Mexico. While ramping up production following pigging and flowback operations, production operators observed a sheen covering 90% of a 100-yard area. BP's failure to adequately flush workover fluids from subsea well MC 822 TD003 S45 before placing it into production resulted in abnormal emulsion and foaming in process vessels. This caused an estimated 3 barrels of oil to be discharged into the Gulf of Mexico through the overboard produced water handling system. The incident was not reported to the National Response Center or BSEE until 23 July 2020, 10 days after occurrence, violating reporting requirements.

While ramping up production following pigging and flowback operations, production operators observed a sheen covering 90% of a 100-yard area. BP's failure to adequately flush workover fluids from subsea well MC 822 TD003 S45 before placing it into production resulted in abnormal emulsion and foaming in process vessels. This caused an estimated 3 barrels of oil to be discharged into the Gulf of Mexico through the overboard produced water handling system. The incident was not reported to the National Response Center or BSEE until 23 July 2020, 10 days after occurrence, violating reporting requirements.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Job Safety Analysis (JSA) Preparation
Role: Administrative control to identify hazards before work
LOD Basis: Pre-job planning and hazard identification
supporting_text: ["the BP production operators prepared a Job Safety Analysis (JSA) to load a pigging device into the oil export pig launcher"]
incident_summary: On 2 August 2020 at 1925 hours, BP production operators were preparing to load a pigging device into the oil export pig launcher KAH-4190 at Thunder Horse platform in the Gulf of Mexico. When operators began cracking open the launcher door, a gas cloud was observed emanating from the top of the launcher. The operators immediately shut the door. The combustible gas detector ASH 962-102, located 3 feet above the launcher, activated and reached 88% of the Low Explosive Limits (LEL). The gas cloud dissipated to 0% LEL within 2 minutes. The incident was caused by oil residue in the pig trap generating a hazardous atmosphere after the launcher had been isolated for 5 days. The launcher was not purged with nitrogen immediately before opening the door, contrary to standard operating procedures. All safety systems responded as designed and no injuries occurred.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Produced water treatment system - Float Cell design
Role: Prevent excess clarifier chemical from entering produced water discharge

incident_summary: A pollution event occurred on 4 May 2021 at Thunder Horse platform (MC 778 A) when approximately 5 barrels of hydrocarbons were released into the Gulf of Mexico from the produced water outfall. The incident was caused by operator error leaving the water clarifier injection quill to Float Cell #2 open, allowing 286 gallons of water clarifier to gravity drain into the vessel. This excess clarifier caused a lack of oil/water separation. The issue was prolonged because a faulty level control valve in Float Cell #1 prevented switching to the backup produced water train. The sheen was first detected via satellite imagery on 4 May 2021 and the discharge continued through 9 May 2021.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Job Safety Analysis (JSA) procedure for pipe tripping operations
Role: Administrative control to ensure safe sequencing of operations
LOD Basis: Procedural control to prevent unsafe operational sequences
supporting_text: ["Operators did not follow JSA procedure for tripping pipe out of the hole"]
incident_summary: On August 7, 2014 at 0525 hours during pipe tripping operations, the Assistant Driller retracted the Hydra-Racker with elevators still closed and top drive extended. Without communicating with the Auxiliary Driller, the Assistant Driller lowered the top drive with drill pipe still connected to elevators and block. The top drive struck the drill pipe, causing it to bow and damage multiple top drive components. The drill pipe broke out of the Hydra-Racker heads, shearing mounting bolts and causing a 500 lb lower guide arm to fall 12 feet to the rig floor. The incident occurred in the last 30 minutes of the last shift prior to crew change when employees were not focused on the task.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: T-Time & Evacuation Report calculations
Role: decision support for operational planning
LOD Basis: operational planning and weather monitoring
supporting_text: ["The Operator and Contractor monitored storm forecasts waiting on notification of a direct path rather than following operational T-times already calculated relative to current well operations and potential Extreme Weather Event.", "Noble's T-Time & Evacuation Report for August 27th 2021 at approximately 05:00 am showed 52 hours to safely secure the well and 12 hours to evacuate/evade... -49 hours Decision Time. This meant the operation was hours behind in the attempt to successfully evacuate/evade.", "The Noble Globetrotter II continued well work throughout the night even though the T-time calculations put them within the potential cone of an extreme weather event"]
incident_summary: The Noble Globetrotter II Drillship was conducting Temporary Abandonment operations on well P012 in MC Block 809 when Hurricane Ida approached. Despite T-time calculations indicating insufficient time to safely secure the well and evacuate, the operator and contractor proceeded with operations. A crew change delayed the temporary abandonment operation by several hours. The drillship unlatched the LMRP and began pulling riser but encountered over-torqued bolts and hydraulic torque wrench breakdowns. At approximately 7:00 pm on August 28th, the Captain initiated Stop Work with 12 riser joints and LMRP still hanging from the rotary. The drillship attempted to evade the hurricane at speeds of 1-3.5 knots but was unsuccessful against Category 2+ hurricane force winds and high seas. The riser broke below the rotary, sending approximately 11 joints and the LMRP to the seafloor. The incident resulted in approximately 3,700 gallons of miscellaneous oil spilled, one loss-time injury, over $1 million in vessel and equipment damage, and 115 personnel exposed to extreme weather conditions.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Gas detection system in RGC enclosure
Role: Detection of gas release
LOD Basis: Detects hazardous condition and triggers alarm response

incident_summary: On 9 December 2020 at approximately 1806 hours, a gas detector inside the Recycle Gas Compressor (RGC) enclosure detected a gas release from a valve flange on a 1-inch methanol injection line. A nut had backed off one of four bolts holding the valve flange, allowing gas to leak through the O-ring. The gas migrated into the RGC enclosure because the enclosure is not sealed and the flange was within 3 feet of the building. The alarm triggered an automatic shutdown of the RGC unit and platform. Emergency response crews successfully located, isolated, and secured the leak. No injuries, equipment damage, or pollution occurred.

incident_summary: On 4 July 2015 at 2120 hours, an Assistant Driller was struck in the left shoulder by the end cap of a pressure relief valve from mud pump #4 on the Noble Globetrotter Drillship. The incident occurred when the Driller failed to verify proper alignment of mud pump #4 to the boost line. The pump was lined up on the #1 standpipe instead, creating a closed system. When the pump was staged to 40 strokes per minute, pressure rapidly increased to 7,919 psi, exceeding the relief valve setting of 7,100 psi. The relief valve actuator housing failed, projecting an 8.5-inch diameter, 1-inch thick, 14-pound end cap that struck the injured person. Investigation revealed no scheduled maintenance had been performed on the pressure relief valves as recommended by the manufacturer.

With no abnormal pressure observed, the Driller then increased the pump to 40 strokes per minute", "Without checking for proper alignment, the Driller initially staged up mud pump #4", "The Driller failed to verify the proper alignment of the #4 mud pump to the boost line"]
incident_summary: On 4 July 2015 at 2120 hours, an Assistant Driller was struck in the left shoulder by the end cap of a pressure relief valve from mud pump #4 on the Noble Globetrotter Drillship. The incident occurred when the Driller failed to verify proper alignment of mud pump #4 to the boost line. The pump was lined up on the #1 standpipe instead, creating a closed system. When the pump was staged to 40 strokes per minute, pressure rapidly increased to 7,919 psi, exceeding the relief valve setting of 7,100 psi. The relief valve actuator housing failed, projecting an 8.5-inch diameter, 1-inch thick, 14-pound end cap that struck the injured person. Investigation revealed no scheduled maintenance had been performed on the pressure relief valves as recommended by the manufacturer.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: BOP Wet Verification Procedure (CG1-OPS-CSP-01-59, Rev. 15)
Role: Procedural control to ensure correct weight application during LMRP disconnect
LOD Basis: Primary procedural control for stack hopping operations
supporting_text: ["The team seemed to have misunderstood Step No. 21 on the BOP Wet Verification Procedure", "The procedure states to adjust tension to 100,000 lbs of LMRP weight on the LMRP connector. Instead, the team applied 100,000 lbs weight to the BOP connector (not the LMRP connector)", "This step was misinterpreted and as a result, the weight was incorrect when the LMRP connector was unlatched"]
incident_summary: On June 7, 2019, during BOP wet verification (stack hopping) operations on the Vito VA001 well, the drill team misinterpreted Step 21 of procedure CG1-OPS-CSP-01-59 Rev. 15. The team applied 100,000 lbs weight to the BOP connector instead of the LMRP connector, resulting in insufficient weight on the LMRP connector. When the Riser Connector was unlatched via ROV flying lead, the LMRP lifted off the BOP while pod receptacles remained energized, causing approximately $500,000 in equipment damage to pod stingers, receptacles, and related tubing/hoses. No spill was recorded.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Manufacturer Installation, Operations & Maintenance Procedures for Aceco Valve
Role: Procedural control to ensure correct valve installation
LOD Basis: Primary control to prevent improper installation
supporting_text: ["the technician did not verify the correct positioning of the face seals and the end flange bolts were not torqued properly according to the Operations & Maintenance Manual", "Technician did not follow the (ACECO VALVE) Installation, Operations & Maintenance procedures when installing the valve in the system"]
incident_summary: On 16-FEB-2015 at 12:40 hours, the Titan Platform (OCS-G 16661) experienced an O-Ring failure on a 1" 15,000 psig Aceco compact valve on the Telemark (AT 63 No. 4) incoming flowline. The valve was being used during methanol flow-back operations following hydrate remediation. Approximately 438 barrels of methanol with trace amounts of crude oil leaked into the Gulf of Mexico, creating a barely-visible sheen over a 1,320' by 10' area. Root causes included improper installation by a Shamrock technician: flange cap screws were not properly torqued, an incorrect outlet flange was installed, and the face seal was not properly verified. No injuries or fatalities occurred.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Emergency Shutdown System
Role: Automatic safety system to isolate hazardous conditions
LOD Basis: Automatic response to loss of pressure in TSE loop
supporting_text: ["At 1455 hours, an ESD was initiated in Zone 14 of the platform due to the Temperature Safety Element (TSE) loop losing pressure", "the platform muster alarm sound and the platform's flare ignited"]
incident_summary: On 09 May 2025 at 1530 hours, an Emergency Shutdown event occurred at MC 127 A SPAR platform when EnerMech technicians accidentally initiated a pressure loss in the Temperature Safety Element loop while troubleshooting an air compressor. During recovery from the ESD, multiple procedural failures and equipment issues led to air entering the firewater system. When the Fire Water Pumps were restarted, a water hammer effect ruptured multiple sections of FiberBond firewater piping, causing extensive damage. A metal support clamp weighing 1.5 pounds fell 30 feet from the ruptured piping and landed 5-6 feet from a person near Lifeboat 4. No injuries occurred, but the firewater system was damaged and unable to provide adequate firefighting capability.

incident_summary: On 09 May 2025 at 1530 hours, an Emergency Shutdown event occurred at MC 127 A SPAR platform when EnerMech technicians accidentally initiated a pressure loss in the Temperature Safety Element loop while troubleshooting an air compressor. During recovery from the ESD, multiple procedural failures and equipment issues led to air entering the firewater system. When the Fire Water Pumps were restarted, a water hammer effect ruptured multiple sections of FiberBond firewater piping, causing extensive damage. A metal support clamp weighing 1.5 pounds fell 30 feet from the ruptured piping and landed 5-6 feet from a person near Lifeboat 4. No injuries occurred, but the firewater system was damaged and unable to provide adequate firefighting capability.

incident_summary: On 21 October 2023 at 13:04 hours at Mississippi Canyon 127 A, Horn Mountain platform, personnel attempted a heavy lift of 8,300 pounds using the platform's West Crane. When the lift was 15 feet above the platform deck, two nylon choker slings failed due to contact with a sharp edge on the hydraulic chain jack cylinder, causing the load to fall onto two cargo baskets. No personnel injuries occurred. The root cause was the decision to use nylon choker slings wrapped around the cylinder body instead of the manufacturer-recommended lifting pad-eyes, combined with inadequate hazard analysis that failed to identify the sharp edge as a hazard.

incident_summary: On 28 MAR 2025 at 1643 hours, an Able Body Seaman 1 (AB1) conducting routine accommodation fire dampener testing alone mistakenly operated the wrong switch panel in the Forward Emergency Gear Locker, inadvertently actuating emergency fuel shut off valves. This caused sequential diesel generator failures, loss of dynamic positioning capability, and drillship drift off station. The rig floor manually activated EDS, shearing the drill pipe and detaching the LMRP, resulting in discharge of 2436 barrels of brine into the Gulf of America and $2.6M in equipment damage.

incident_summary: On May 29, 2025, at approximately 10:46 a.m., the Noble Stanley Lafosse drillship experienced an unexpected and severe weather event during cement preparation operations. Forecasts had predicted winds not exceeding 10 knots; however, a thunderstorm developed producing a sudden 60-knot wind gust from the north that shifted to the west. The vessel experienced a 14-meter excursion while hanging off 5-7/8" drill pipe, triggering escalation from yellow alert to red alert. The crew initiated an Emergency Disconnect Sequence, successfully closing blind shear rams and securing the well. Approximately 417 barrels of 10.9 ppg calcium chloride were released to the sea. No injuries were reported. The incident was caused by rapid and variable wind conditions that exceeded the rig's station-keeping capacity.

incident_summary: On March 5, 2024, at 04:15 hrs, the Transocean Deepwater Poseidon drillship experienced extreme unexpected changes in wind speed and direction due to strong thunderstorms while drilling the AW003 well in MC 391. Wind speeds escalated from 30 kts to 83 kts with rapid directional shifts that were not forecasted. The vessel excursion triggered an Emergency Disconnect Sequence when the vessel approached the red watch circle limit (90.3 ft). During the EDS, three joints of 6-5/8 in drill pipe and an MPD bearing assembly (13,200 lbs) fell from the derrick and struck the drill shack protective barrier. Approximately 2,875 bbls of synthetic based mud (1,667 bbls base oil) was released to the seafloor. Total estimated damages: $1,000,000.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Emulsion breaker chemical trial approval process
Role: Management approval gate for chemical trials
LOD Basis: Pre-incident approval by BSEE
supporting_text: ["Although the chemical trial was approved by BSEE, the trial chemical proved to be non-compatible in the produced water system"]
incident_summary: On 08 February 2025, BP personnel at Na Kika platform began injecting a trial emulsion breaker chemical into the produced water treatment system. The new chemical caused frothing and foaming within production vessels, leading to faulty level readings and liquid carryover. This resulted in approximately 89 barrels of oil and grease discharging from the produced water overboard system into the Gulf of America from 08-15 February 2025. The discharge went undetected for 7 days due to the discharge point being located 80 feet below the water surface. Once BP identified the chemical trial as the cause, they discontinued the trial and returned to the original formula, successfully ending the pollution event.

incident_summary: On 07-JUN-2023 at 11:00 hours, a 43-foot cleanout assembly fell approximately 40 feet onto the pipe skate during drilling operations on the Diamond Ocean BlackHornet Drillship at MC 520. incident_summary: On 24 December 2023 at 0743 hours, a fire occurred on a rental air compressor being used for painting and blasting operations on the FPU 'Who Dat' in MC 547 A, approximately 39 miles offshore in 3280 feet of water. The platform's fire detection system and Emergency Shut Down system functioned as designed. The fire was safely extinguished by the platform's Fire Team using water. Cost of repairs/replacement: $31,969.09.

incident_summary: On 24 December 2023 at 0743 hours, a fire occurred on a rental air compressor being used for painting and blasting operations on the FPU 'Who Dat' in MC 547 A, approximately 39 miles offshore in 3280 feet of water. The fire was caused by failure of a 90° elbow on the oil separator piping, which allowed oil to spray into the enclosure and ignite on hot surfaces. The platform's fire detection system and Emergency Shut Down system functioned as designed. The fire was safely extinguished by the platform's Fire Team using water. No injuries, environmental impact, or platform damage occurred. Damage was limited to the rental air compressor and minor charring of adjacent generator enclosure wiring. Cost of repairs/replacement: $31,969.09.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Identify equipment defects and corrosion
LOD Basis: Preventive maintenance inspection program
supporting_text: ["Upon review of the East crane annual inspection performed on 05 October 2022, it was noted that the boom had major corrosion on two of the boom mid-sections and major corrosion on the boom tip section along with storm damage. The inspection also identified that the cable rollers on the boom had major corrosion and were unable to move as designed. The crane inspector recommended that the cable rollers be replaced or repaired within one to three months on the annual crane inspection deficiency report. However, the parts were never ordered by Murphy to repair or replace the cable rollers."]
incident_summary: On 16 April 2025 at approximately 1500 hours, a 15-pound cable roller measuring 66 inches long and one inch in diameter broke off from the boom of an East Crane (700L-140) on the Medusa SPAR platform and fell 50 feet to the deck below. The cable roller struck a hydraulic hose on the deck, damaging it beyond use. No injuries occurred. The root causes were inadequate preventive maintenance (failure to repair corroded cable rollers identified in October 2022 inspection) and human error (failure to barricade the area beneath the crane to prevent personnel access to the drop zone).

incident_summary: On March 19, 2024, at 13:30 hrs, during BOP deployment/riser running operations on the Valaris DS-18 drillship in Mississippi Canyon Block 607, riser joint #31 was dropped onto the forward conveyor skate. The incident occurred when the riser cart shuttle brake valve wire was loose, causing a fuse to blow and preventing the cart from freewheeling. As the driller continued to hoist the riser joint, one end came out of the conveyor bucket and the Cameron Hydraulic Riser Running Tool (HRRT) mandrel parted, allowing the riser joint to fall approximately 28 inches onto the conveyor. The incident resulted in damage to the riser skate tailing arms, skate lift bucket, HRRT, and riser joint floatation, with estimated property damage of $418,000. No injuries occurred.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: DOT special permit regulations requiring 10-year hydrostatic or ultrasonic examination
Role: Inspection and certification of cylinder integrity
LOD Basis: Regulatory compliance inspection
supporting_text: ["the nitrogen cylinder rack adhered to Department of Transportation (DOT) special permit regulations, which mandate a 10-year hydrostatic or ultrasonic examination. However, these inspections failed to identify critical issues such as excessive corrosion with the internal cylinders"]
incident_summary: On June 5, 2024, at approximately 15:38 hours, a nitrogen cylinder ruptured inside a twelve-cylinder bottle rack aboard the Helix Q5000 during workover operations. The rupture caused damage to other bottles in the rack and scattered them across the main deck. Three personnel sustained minor injuries from shrapnel. The root cause was corrosion of the cylinder wall due to inadequate paint coating protection in the marine environment, combined with design deficiencies including lack of protective barriers between cylinders and rack, and inadequate drainage. Visual pre-fill inspections prior to offshore deployment did not identify the corrosion because the rack was not disassembled for thorough inspection of individual cylinders.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Ensure correct tools are identified and gathered before work begins
LOD Basis: Administrative control to prevent tool modification by identifying proper tools upfront

incident_summary: On 24 February 2023 at approximately 1115 hours, a Mechanic employed by Helmerich & Payne was attempting to install a hydraulic line on the Pipe Handling Machine (PHM) at MC 807A (Mars TLP). When the combination wrench being used was determined to be too long to access the fitting, the Mechanic decided to modify the wrench by cutting it shorter using a manual hack saw. The wrench was placed in a vise to stabilize it during cutting. As the final cut was made, the Mechanic's weight shifted, causing his hand to come down onto the freshly cut sharp edge of the wrench, resulting in a laceration between the ring and little finger. The Mechanic received first aid onsite and was evacuated to an onshore medical facility where sutures were applied. The Mechanic returned to the platform on 26 February 2023.

incident_summary: On 23 February 2025 at 0830 hours, a SafeZone worker slipped while securing felt and cleaning inside a Pressurized Welding Enclosure (PWE) on the Ursa platform at MC 809. The worker lost his footing on an elevated working surface, fell 12-18 inches, and his right leg contacted a jagged edge of an I-beam, resulting in a 3-to-4-inch laceration. The worker was medically evacuated and received 11 staples and 4 stitches. Investigation found that jagged edges and uneven surfaces were not identified in the initial JSA hazard assessment, and supervision was inadequate during the pre-job hazard identification process.

incident_summary: On February 13, 2024, at approximately 22:35 hours, two Tetra filtration crew members were injured on the Transocean Deepwater Proteus drillship during completion operations on the SS005 well. While attempting to repair a small leak in a 6-inch camlock connection, the Tetra employee released the camlock levers. Pressurized fluid in the hose caused the hose to blow off the deck flange and strike two personnel. One worker received facial impact and a fractured tooth; the second worker was struck and fell against a pipe, sustaining bruising and chemical exposure.

incident_summary: On 10 May 2023 at approximately 0930 hours, two workers employed by Grand Isle Shipyard were removing bolts from a flange on a 2-inch Well Equalization Header line during a scheduled shut-in at MC 941 Platform A (Titan). As the workers loosened the last bolt, trapped pressure within the line escaped and sprayed the two workers in the face with a small amount of hydrocarbon fluid. The release activated a nearby gas detector, sounding the platform alarm. No injuries, pollution, or equipment damage occurred. The incident was caused by oversight of residual fluids and trapped pressure in a dog-leg bend above the flange during the isolation process.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: In-Service Inspection Plan (ISIP) with American Bureau of Shipping (ABS) Underwater Inspection in lieu of Drydocking (UWILD)
Role: Detection of mooring line degradation
LOD Basis: Scheduled inspection program to identify defects before failure
supporting_text: ["On 15 June 2023, in accordance with the In-Service Inspection Plan (ISIP), Titan was conducting a scheduled American Bureau of Shipping (ABS) Underwater Inspection in lieu of Drydocking (UWILD) when an abnormal amount of slack was observed on the ML #9"]
incident_summary: On 15 June 2023, during a scheduled underwater inspection of the Titan SPAR platform mooring system, abnormal slack was observed on Mooring Line #9 from the Port Column. Investigation revealed a significant length of the line was resting on the seabed. The 5-inch Grade RQ4 chain link subsequently failed due to fatigue initiating at the crown where stresses are high, with corrosion as a contributing factor. The failure was likely initiated by surface cracks on the crown. The incident resulted in production shutdown and a replacement cost estimate of $5,000,000.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Automatic Operated Valve (AOV)
Role: Isolation barrier to prevent MEG flow through abandoned well circuit
LOD Basis: Primary isolation device in MEG injection line
supporting_text: ["The Automatic Operated Valve (AOV) and Shut Down Valve (SDV) were leaking, causing MEG to flow through the valves into the EA2 umbilical circuit", "The investigation revealed the leaking AOV and SDV to have caused the pollution event"]
incident_summary: On July 11, 2018, at 1200 hours, an estimated 17,098 gallons of Mono Ethylene Glycol (MEG) was discharged subsea from the Na Kika Platform (MC 474-A) following repair work on PZZ 2591/92 MEG pumps. The leak occurred through leaking Automatic Operated Valve (AOV) and Shut Down Valve (SDV) into the EA2 umbilical circuit. The leak went undetected for approximately 4 days (July 11-15, 2018) due to subsea location and pollutant dissipation before reaching the surface. The incident resulted in an unauthorized discharge violation and failure to notify BSEE.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Roof inspection and repair program
Role: Prevent water ingress into safety-critical equipment enclosures
LOD Basis: Preventive maintenance and asset integrity management
supporting_text: ["BP failed to include the roof in an inspection or repair program", "The condition of the roof deteriorated after several years"]
incident_summary: On May 4, 2019, at 1351 hours, a partial loss of power occurred at BP Nakika Platform (MC 474-A) in the Gulf of Mexico. The root cause was rainwater ingress into the ESS-2 safety panel due to an unprotected warehouse roof under repair. The rainwater disrupted the ESS-2 processor, which handles critical safety functions including electrical power management. The emergency generator failed to start automatically due to a faulty lube oil temperature switch. Crew bypassed the switch and manually started the generator. All production safety systems functioned as designed. No evacuations, injuries, or pollution occurred. Power was restored at 1431 hours.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Management of Change (MOC) procedures
Role: Administrative control to ensure operational changes are reviewed and procedures updated
LOD Basis: Preventive control to identify need for procedure updates when operational conditions change
supporting_text: ["From the end of 2017 to the time of the incident, water production increased by approximately 6,000 barrels per day (BPD). However, the increase in water production did not prompt a review of the produced water handling system and well start up procedures.", "Management Systems- Inadequate Management of Change (MOC) procedures: The change in water production did not prompt a review of the produced water handling system."]
incident_summary: On May 16, 2018, a pollution event occurred at Noble Energy's Mississippi Canyon 736 A (Thunder Hawk) Platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The incident involved the discharge of approximately 14.77 barrels of oil from the facility's overboard water line. The pollution event was preceded by three separate shut-in and start-up events between May 13-16, 2018. During production ramp-ups, operators pumped methanol and hydrate inhibitor into flowlines, which increased demand on the overboard water treatment system. Level control issues in process vessels, combined with inadequate operational procedures for handling increased water production (6,000 BPD increase since end of 2017), resulted in oily water discharge. The root cause was identified as inadequate Management of Change procedures and inadequate operational procedures for well start-up sequences.

incident_summary: On July 10, 2021, at approximately 18:15 hours, during sidetrack operations on the A005 well at MC Block 773, a dropped object incident occurred when a stinger hook struck a walkway handrail while lifting a crossover sub using 35-foot slings. The broken handrail (5.5 ft by 1.5 in square tubing, 13.6 lbs) fell approximately 75 feet to the deck below. No injuries occurred, though one person was 6 feet from the impact location. Root cause was determined to be use of incorrect (too long) crane slings and lack of communication between driller and crane operator.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: UPS-B System with Inverter and Static Bypass Mode
Role: Provide backup power to Emergency Motor Control Centers
LOD Basis: Primary backup power system for critical facility loads
supporting_text: ["On September 17, 2017, at 2000 hours, the MC 778-A (Thunder Horse) UPS-B inverter failed but normal power continued to supply the Emergency Motor Control Center(s) (EMCC) when the UPS switched to Static Bypass Mode.", "The following morning on September 18, 2017, at approximately 0700 hours, a BP electrician discovered the alarms on UPS-B during normal rounds."]
incident_summary: On September 18, 2017, at 1100 hours, the Thunder Horse Platform (MC 778-A) experienced a complete loss of electrical power including emergency power. The incident was triggered by a UPS-B inverter failure on September 17, followed by a static bypass switch opening due to frequency synchronization tolerance mismatch between the Emergency Generator and Turbine Generators. The facility's Vessel Management System incorrectly maintained an abandon platform alarm state when rebooted, causing repeated power losses. A carbon monoxide gas release was detected in the UPS-B battery room. The incident resulted in the evacuation of 265 personnel and took approximately 24 hours to restore emergency power and several days to fully restore facility operations.

incident_summary: On September 18, 2017, at 1100 hours, the Thunder Horse Platform (MC 778-A) experienced a complete loss of electrical power including emergency power. The incident was triggered by a UPS-B inverter failure on September 17, followed by a static bypass switch opening due to frequency synchronization tolerance mismatch between the Emergency Generator and Turbine Generators. The facility's Vessel Management System incorrectly maintained an abandon platform alarm state when rebooted, causing repeated power losses. A carbon monoxide gas release was detected in the UPS-B battery room. The incident resulted in the evacuation of 265 personnel and took approximately 24 hours to restore emergency power and several days to fully restore facility operations.

incident_summary: On 22 October 2020 at 13:59, MC 778 A (Thunder Horse) experienced an unplanned power outage when line-of-sight gas detectors near the air compressor header activated, triggering a Process Shut Down and subsequent Emergency Platform Shut Down. Upon restart and troubleshooting, loss of hydraulic fluid was discovered at Subsea Well MC 776 TG002, caused by leaking SCSSV rod piston seals. An estimated 3 to 50 barrels of Transaqua Hydraulic Control fluid and/or hydrocarbon entered the Gulf of Mexico. The SCSSV was re-opened and hydraulic control lines were re-pressurized to prevent further loss of containment. ROV inspection confirmed no fluid flowing from the SCSSV vent. A function test on 21 November 2020 confirmed the SCSSV failed to function open. The well is currently secure with subsea tree valves in the closed position.

incident_summary: On June 2, 2019 at 14:30 hours, the Seadrill West Capricorn Semi-Submersible Drilling Rig encountered a well control event while drilling the Taggert #3 Well in Mississippi Canyon Block 816. The drill crew observed increased return flow with increased gas at the surface (max gas=1386 units). After closing the BOP upper annular and diverter bag, approximately 4 barrels of Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SBM) were diverted to the Gulf of Mexico waters. No injuries occurred. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Orifice Plate Removal Procedure
Role: Procedural control to ensure safe disassembly of pressurized equipment
LOD Basis: Written procedure should prevent hazardous conditions before they develop
supporting_text: ["The IP failed to close the equalizer valve between the upper (top) and lower (bottom) chamber on the SOF prior to loosening the clamp bar bolts on the upper chamber", "When the IP found the SOF in an abnormal condition, all steps in OP removal procedure should have been reevaluated"]
incident_summary: On 24-Nov-2014 at approximately 0830, an operator at Fieldwood Energy's Matagorda Island 622 'C' platform was injured when investigating a flow rate indication problem on the #4 Production Separator. While attempting to verify orifice plate installation, the operator failed to close the equalizer valve between the upper and lower chambers of the Senior Orifice Fitting prior to loosening clamp bar bolts. A plugged blow-down valve created a false impression of zero pressure. When the operator loosened the second clamp bar bolt, approximately 915 PSIG of natural gas and debris was released, striking the operator's left eye and face. The operator was transported by helicopter to hospital where corneal surgeries were performed. Full recovery is expected.

LOD Basis: N/A

incident_summary: On March 14, 2017 at approximately 2330 hours, a diesel-driven cement pump on the Lift Boat Jamie G Eymard experienced engine over-speed during decommissioning operations, causing fire to erupt from exhaust stacks. The right bank turbo charger had failed, allowing engine oil to be injected into the air intake. Despite activation of the Emergency Shutdown Device, the engine continued to run due to a flapper seal failure that allowed continued air feed. Crew successfully extinguished the fire using portable extinguishers and the vessel's fire water system. No injuries or pollution resulted. Property damage estimated at $100,000.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Initial combustible gas check prior to cutting operations
Role: Detection of flammable gas before hot work
LOD Basis: Pre-work hazard assessment
supporting_text: ["An initial combustible gas check was completed prior to commencing cutting operations", "an Altair 5x gas detector was utilized at the cellar deck level, resulting in a gas detection of 0% Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)"]
incident_summary: On October 2, 2015, at approximately 20:25 hours, a flash fire occurred during decommissioning activities on Matagorda Island 685, Platform A, OCS-G-04548. Tetra Offshore welding personnel were cutting slots in the B-1 conductor for installation of lifting plates when an Oxylance burning bar ignited flammable gas trapped inside the 13 3/8 inch x 9 5/8 inch casing annulus at the plus ten deck. Initial combustible gas checks at the cellar deck level and wellhead detected 0% LEL, but failed to detect trapped gas in the annulus. The flash fire burned for approximately 30 seconds before self-extinguishing. No injuries, pollution, or property damage were reported.

Tetra Offshore welding personnel were cutting slots in the B-1 conductor for installation of lifting plates when an Oxylance burning bar ignited flammable gas trapped inside the 13 3/8 inch x 9 5/8 inch casing annulus at the plus ten deck. Initial combustible gas checks at the cellar deck level and wellhead detected 0% LEL, but failed to detect trapped gas in the annulus. The flash fire burned for approximately 30 seconds before self-extinguishing. No injuries, pollution, or property damage were reported.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Sling Proof Load Testing

incident_summary: On May 5, 2018, at 0745 hours, a crane incident occurred at Cox Operating LLC's MO Block 872 A platform. During the second lift, a 4000-pound tote tank containing 550 gallons of diesel was being offloaded from M/V Diamond to the platform. While lowering the tote tank into a containment skid, one of the four sling legs came loose at the swage ferrule due to a manufacturing defect. The wire rope parted, causing shock loading on the remaining three sling legs, which also failed. The tote tank fell to the deck, spilling a small amount of diesel into the containment skid. No injuries or pollution to the Gulf of Mexico occurred. Investigation revealed the sling had a manufacturing defect: a 3/8 inch ferrule was installed on 5/16 inch wire rope when 3/8 inch wire rope was specified.

incident_summary: On 25 December 2020 at approximately 18:00, a backfire occurred while attempting to start the compressor engine at MP Block 153 B platform. A sticking fuel valve relay in the pneumatic panel allowed unburnt fuel to saturate the engine, causing a backfire upon the second start attempt. The backfire caused the muffler to separate and become a projectile, damaging wall siding, compressor building lights, crane window, and air filters. No injuries were reported. The platform remained shut in as a result.

incident_summary: On 8 May 2021 at 1100 hours at MP 283 A, two personnel (mechanic and electrician) were moving a 100-pound crate 30 feet across deck grating using a hand dolly to load it into a cargo box. Due to the crate's rectangular shape and size, it could not be balanced equally on the dolly. As personnel pulled the crate across the deck and encountered a 4-inch step-up 2 feet from the cargo box opening, the crate slid off the dolly. The injured person (mechanic) lost grip on the dolly handle and it impacted his right foot just behind the steel toe, causing a contusion. The worker was evacuated by helicopter and placed on light duty for greater than 3 days.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Kimray Temperature Controller
Role: Primary control to maintain safe operating temperature
LOD Basis: First line of defense to prevent excessive temperature
supporting_text: ["The Operators later discovered that the 'Kimray Temperature Controller' was not working properly and caused the temperature to rise above the normal setting of 150 degrees.", "Kimray Temperature Controller malfunctioned."]
incident_summary: On 2-FEB-2015 at 0230 hours, a flash fire occurred on Stone Energy's OCS-G 01665 Main Pass 288-A Platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The Kimray Temperature Controller on the BAP-1500 Line Heater malfunctioned, causing excessive temperature rise above the normal 150 degrees setting. The Temperature Safety High (TSH) sensor failed to shut off fuel supply and inflow of combustible fluids. This caused Glycol and Antifreeze mixture to boil over and spill onto the lower deck, dripping onto the Compressor exhaust and igniting. The fire was extinguished by operators using a fire extinguisher and water hose. Contributing factors included a metal plate blocking the burner sight glass port, preventing visual inspection for abnormal conditions, and the Pressure Safety High (PSH) sensor also failing to function.

incident_summary: On September 22, 2019, at 06:55 hours, an Electric Line Operator (ELO) at McMoRan's Main Pass 299 A facility sustained puncture wounds to his right-hand fingers, left forearm, and left torso when a Green Detonator blasting cap exploded during continuity testing. The ELO used an incorrect testing device (megohmmeter) instead of the proper galvanometer, and did not place the blasting cap inside the safety loading tube prior to testing. The incident resulted in the ELO being transported by helicopter for medical evaluation, which determined that only first aid was required.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Wet seal system with labyrinth seals
Role: Prevent gas from entering lube oil system
LOD Basis: Primary barrier to maintain separation between gas and lube oil
supporting_text: ["The wet seals keep gas from entering the lube oil system by pumping oil through labyrinth seals at a pressure greater than the gas pressure", "Gas migrated into the lube oil tank from a failed wet seal"]
incident_summary: On 21 September 2021 at approximately 1550 hours, an Emergency Shut Down occurred at MP 42 D/L/M facility. A leak in the ESD/TSE loop caused loss of main power and rendered both primary and secondary seal oil pumps inoperable. Combined with a liquid carryover event in the first stage suction scrubber, produced gas bypassed the wet seals and entered the lube oil tank. The overpressurized tank lid blew off, spraying lube oil and natural gas onto the hot turbine engine, causing ignition and rapid fire spread. All personnel were safely evacuated with no injuries or pollution.

The overpressurized tank lid blew off, spraying lube oil and natural gas onto the hot turbine engine, causing ignition and rapid fire spread. All personnel were safely evacuated with no injuries or pollution.

incident_summary: On 9 October 2021 at 0910 hours, the reciprocating gas compressor (CBA-0900) at MP 64 A #1 experienced an explosion in the 3rd stage discharge piping between the discharge bottle and heat exchanger during startup after shutdown. The explosion was caused by oxygen intrusion into the compressor inlet suction stream through two leaking valve cover gaskets on the 1st stage compression suction cylinder. The oxygen mixed with process gas and was auto-ignited by high temperature discharge gas from the 3rd stage cylinder. The operator mistakenly interpreted an initial ignition noise as an engine backfire and restarted the compressor rather than shutting down for mechanical diagnosis. External damage was limited to a 3-inch piping section and insulation. No injuries occurred and no personnel mustered.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Identify hazards and communicate risks to workers before work begins
LOD Basis: Administrative control to prevent workers from entering hazardous situation unaware
supporting_text: ["The failure to perform a pre-job hazard walkthrough was confirmed in the GOM Shelf's internal investigative report", "they were told by the PIC, 'go figure it out, do your job'", "The LSS Technicians were unaware of the hazard (extremely high gas pressure, 4330 psi) upstream of the 2 inch production casing valve prior to starting their job"]
incident_summary: On 12 March 2023 at 1633 hours, two Louisiana Safety Systems technicians were injured on MP 311 B platform while performing valve maintenance on well QAY B-18ST. A 1-inch stainless steel gas lift injection tubing, rated at approximately 3100 psi, ruptured due to 4330 psi of unexpected pressure trapped in the tubing. One technician suffered a serious injury resulting in lost time greater than 3 days; the other sustained minor injuries. The incident was caused by: (1) failure to monitor casing pressure, (2) failure to perform pre-job hazard walkthrough, (3) failure to perform required casing diagnostic, and (4) discrepancies between approved drawings and actual field installation. Root cause was downhole well integrity issues that resulted in casing pressure buildup following a grease zerk repair in July 2021.

A 1-inch stainless steel gas lift injection tubing, rated at approximately 3100 psi, ruptured due to 4330 psi of unexpected pressure trapped in the tubing. One technician suffered a serious injury resulting in lost time greater than 3 days; the other sustained minor injuries. The incident was caused by: (1) failure to monitor casing pressure, (2) failure to perform pre-job hazard walkthrough, (3) failure to perform required casing diagnostic, and (4) discrepancies between approved drawings and actual field installation. Root cause was downhole well integrity issues that resulted in casing pressure buildup following a grease zerk repair in July 2021.

incident_summary: On 27 April 2025 at 1520 hours, a 12-bottle nitrogen rack on the top deck of MP 42 Delta platform experienced an explosion. One nitrogen bottle with corrosion at its base ruptured, causing the 'daisy-chained' tubing and isolation valves to be ripped from the remaining bottles, launching them across the platform. Four bottles went overboard into the Gulf; seven were scattered on deck. One bottle remained severely damaged in the aluminum rack. No personnel were injured. Damage included the aluminum rack, welding machine door, and section of solid decking. The bottles were at least 1 year overdue for inspection.

At this point, the mechanical brake had no load holding capability to stop the boom from falling", "The Lead Mechanic quickly tried to stop the boom from falling by turning the bolt counterclockwise. incident_summary: On 15 October 2019 at 0755 hours, a crane boom dropped from a 45-degree angle onto the boom rest at Talos Petroleum's Main Pass 288 A platform during unscheduled maintenance. The boom was being lowered using an unapproved emergency load lowering procedure with an improper brake release tool (a 1/4" threaded bolt instead of the required fixed-head nut tool). The spiral lock ring in the brake actuator failed, causing loss of load control and the boom to fall approximately 30 feet, resulting in severe structural damage to the outermost 20-foot boom mid-section. No injuries or damage to process equipment or platform structure occurred. Root causes included inadequate communication between operator and contractor, lack of proper documentation and approval for the procedure used, and mechanical failures in the brake assembly.

Role: Thermal barrier to prevent ignition of combustible materials

incident_summary: On 25-JUN-2015 at 2100 hours, lighting failed at platform MU A 111 A due to a tripped electrical breaker. Upon investigation, the P&A Supervisor discovered a small flash fire on the generator engine exhaust manifold insulation. The fire was caused by vibration of metal rings used to adjoin heat wrap pieces together, which created a pinhole in the underlying blow tubing. Oil from the blow tube was blown onto the heat wrap by the engine fan, igniting the insulation. The fire was immediately extinguished and the generator shut down. Damage was limited to a small burned area of heat wrap insulation, with estimated property damage of $500.

incident_summary: On 25-JUN-2015 at 2100 hours, lighting failed at platform MU A 111 A due to a tripped electrical breaker. Upon investigation, the P&A Supervisor discovered a small flash fire on the generator engine exhaust manifold insulation. The fire was caused by vibration of metal rings used to adjoin heat wrap pieces together, which created a pinhole in the underlying blow tubing. Oil from the blow tube was blown onto the heat wrap by the engine fan, igniting the insulation. The fire was immediately extinguished and the generator shut down. Damage was limited to a small burned area of heat wrap insulation, with estimated property damage of $500.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Flow Safety Valve (FSV)
Role: Prevent pressurized gas release
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["Natural Gas was observed to be leaking from the upstream flange of the Flow Safety Valve (FSV) on the FA-2 flow line segment", "gasket failure on the upstream flange of the FSV"]
incident_summary: On July 4, 2014 at 1300 hours, construction workers on the main deck heard an unusual noise from the cellar deck. Natural gas was observed leaking from the upstream flange of the Flow Safety Valve (FSV) on the FA-2 flow line segment of the B-001 well. The leak was caused by multiple valve deficiencies: the Manual Master Valve, Surface Safety Valve, and Wing Valve were all leaking, allowing shut-in tubing pressure of 3200 PSIG to reach the out-of-service production header, causing gasket failure on the FSV upstream flange. These valve deficiencies had been previously identified on August 16, 2013 but were not mitigated by management. The facility was evacuated and the well was isolated.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Permit to Work (PTW) system for lifting operations
Role: Administrative control to ensure proper authorization and planning
LOD Basis: Primary control to prevent unauthorized or improperly planned lifts
supporting_text: ["the Noble Crane Operator and his crew failed to complete a new Permit To Work (PTW) and a Lift Plan for this operation", "The previous PTW and Lift Plan was for lifting chrome tubing for the tubing operation. This permit for lifting chrome tubing had expired"]
incident_summary: On July 22, 2022, at 2203 hours, a spanner joint assembly being lifted by tandem lift (topdrive and crane) on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse experienced failure of the nylon sling tail line. The assembly dropped approximately 6 feet onto the riser skate deck. Investigation revealed the crane operator failed to create a new Permit to Work and Lift Plan, instead reusing an expired permit for chrome tubing. The nylon sling used was rated for only 5,100 pounds in choker arrangement but was required to lift approximately 15,000 pounds. No injuries occurred due to Red Zone Management and personnel positioning.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Hazard Assessment and Stored Energy Evaluation
Role: Identify and quantify stored energy in pipeline prior to cutting operations
LOD Basis: Pre-work planning and risk assessment
supporting_text: ["Personnel were not aware of the amount of stored energy within the pipeline", "operations did not account for or expect the amount of stored energy within the pipeline"]
incident_summary: On 30 June 2022 at 2346 hrs, while conducting subsea pipeline modification operations on PSN 11015 in the Gulf of Mexico (205 ft water depth, 41 miles offshore), a diamond wire saw cut through an 18-inch diameter pipeline. At approximately 65% completion of the cut, the pipeline suddenly separated due to unknown stored energy, causing each end to move in opposite directions. This unexpected torsion/tension caused the temporary 8-inch bypass piping to shear at a flange connection, releasing approximately 90 barrels of crude oil into the Gulf of Mexico over a 3-day period. The incident was caused by personnel not being aware of the amount of stored energy within the pipeline, and was contributed to by lack of communication with a pipeline contributor prior to performing the cut.

incident_summary: On 30 June 2022 at 2346 hrs, while conducting subsea pipeline modification operations on PSN 11015 in the Gulf of Mexico (205 ft water depth, 41 miles offshore), a diamond wire saw cut through an 18-inch diameter pipeline. At approximately 65% completion of the cut, the pipeline suddenly separated due to unknown stored energy, causing each end to move in opposite directions. This unexpected torsion/tension caused the temporary 8-inch bypass piping to shear at a flange connection, releasing approximately 90 barrels of crude oil into the Gulf of Mexico over a 3-day period. The incident was caused by personnel not being aware of the amount of stored energy within the pipeline, and was contributed to by lack of communication with a pipeline contributor prior to performing the cut.

incident_summary: At approximately 1000 hours on 8/01/2021, a fire occurred on Talos Energy South Pelto Block 23 Platform D in the Heater Treater (NBK-2900). A Production Operator noticed black smoke from the stack and investigated, discovering a small fire. The operator and PIC shut in the heater treater, turned off gas, opened the front cover hatch, and used a dry chemical extinguisher to extinguish the incipient fire. Upon inspection, a pinhole leak was found in the fire tube. Investigation determined the fire tube was operating at higher temperatures (110-120°F) than designed (maximum 105°F), and paraffin solids had accumulated on the fire tube due to insufficient heat transfer, contributing to the failure. A pattern of similar fires had occurred in 2016 and 2018. Post-incident modifications included installation of a new fire tube, burner, heat shroud, and updated operating procedures.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Burner Safety Low alarm system
Role: Detection of unsafe burner conditions

incident_summary: On September 23, 2021 at 1010 hours, a Burner Safety Low alarm occurred on the NBK-2900 Heater Treater at Talos Energy's South Pelto Platform. Two operators responded: one at the burner location and one at the master panel. After resetting the fuel shutdown valve relay and relighting the burners, a pressure release occurred when the West side burner was lit. The root cause was identified as restricted airflow in the stack arrestor from a previous fire tube fire on August 1, 2021, combined with insufficient purge time of residual gas before reignition. A second incident occurred on September 30, 2021 during restart, revealing charred wiring on the igniter and ground wires. The burner section was permanently taken out of service.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Production alarm system
Role: Detection of abnormal conditions
LOD Basis: Alarm detection of burner safety low condition
supporting_text: ["a production alarm sounded at 1930 hours", "Personnel went to the panel to investigate and found the burner safety low (BSL) indicator for the Heater Treater (NBK-2900) activated"]
incident_summary: On 29-FEB-2016 at 1930 hours, a fire occurred inside the 24-inch fire tube of the Heater Treater (NBK-2900) on the South Pelto PL 23-D platform. The fire was caused by thermo-galvanic corrosion of the A-106 Grade B schedule 20 fire tube material. Multiple corrosion-accelerating parameters were present: low pH produced water (2.95), high chloride concentration (25,028 mg/l), temperature gradient on the metal surface, and scale deposition. The phosphoric acid in the emulsion breaker contributed to the low pH. The calculated pitting corrosion rate was 450 mils/year, an extremely high rate requiring thermo-galvanic corrosion as the driving force. The fire was quickly extinguished with no injuries or evacuation. The platform remained shut in for repairs until 18-MAR-2016.

incident_summary: On 29-FEB-2016 at 1930 hours, a fire occurred inside the 24-inch fire tube of the Heater Treater (NBK-2900) on the South Pelto PL 23-D platform. The fire was caused by thermo-galvanic corrosion of the A-106 Grade B schedule 20 fire tube material. Multiple corrosion-accelerating parameters were present: low pH produced water (2.95), high chloride concentration (25,028 mg/l), temperature gradient on the metal surface, and scale deposition. The phosphoric acid in the emulsion breaker contributed to the low pH. The calculated pitting corrosion rate was 450 mils/year, an extremely high rate requiring thermo-galvanic corrosion as the driving force. The fire was quickly extinguished with no injuries or evacuation. The platform remained shut in for repairs until 18-MAR-2016.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Detection of H2S gas concentration

incident_summary: At approximately 1730 hours on 25-June-2018, an H2S release occurred on the South Pelto 5-B platform during abandonment operations involving flushing of eight platforms and fifteen pipelines. A worker detected an odor of H2S at an open top flow back tank. An H2S detector recorded 100 ppm of gas. All operations were stopped and personnel mustered for safety. H2S readings steadily decreased from 100 ppm to 0 ppm over nine hours. No personnel were injured. Investigation determined that water left in pipelines for over two years prior to abandonment operations allowed high concentrations of bacteria growth, which led to small pockets of H2S gas formation.

incident_summary: On 26-Nov-2015 at approximately 1030 hours, a fire was observed on the unmanned, shut-in Padre North 975 A platform during an overflight. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Close torch bottle valves when not in use
Role: Prevent pressurized flammable gas release
LOD Basis: Primary control to prevent acetylene release
supporting_text: ["torch bottle valves were not closed when finished with use", "construction personnel decided to use a plasma cutter in lieu of the cutting torch. Reports stated that the cutting torch and hoses were then rolled up and stored on a bracket hung on the wall"]
incident_summary: On 20 August 2022 at 1007 hours, a worker discovered a small flame emanating from an unattended oxygen/acetylene cutting torch hanging from a bulkhead in a Safe Welding Area on the Ursa TLP. The worker immediately extinguished the flame using a nearby portable fire extinguisher and notified the Control Room. A general alarm was sounded, full muster conducted, and Fire Team dispatched. No flame, smoke, or damage was observed by the Fire Team. The incident was caused by construction personnel failing to close torch bottle valves, depressurize lines, and fully tighten the torch head valve after switching to a plasma cutter. Sparks from the plasma cutter subsequently ignited acetylene gas leaking from the improperly secured torch. No injuries or pollution resulted.

The worker immediately extinguished the flame using a nearby portable fire extinguisher and notified the Control Room. A general alarm was sounded, full muster conducted, and Fire Team dispatched. No flame, smoke, or damage was observed by the Fire Team. The incident was caused by construction personnel failing to close torch bottle valves, depressurize lines, and fully tighten the torch head valve after switching to a plasma cutter. Sparks from the plasma cutter subsequently ignited acetylene gas leaking from the improperly secured torch. No injuries or pollution resulted.

incident_summary: On 8 May 2022 at 1130 hours, a sheen of unknown origin was observed on the water at Mississippi Canyon Block 940, OCS-G 31534. The leak originated from Subsea Well MC 940 (Vito) VA007, a completed well not yet tied back via pipeline to Shell's Vito facility. The source was identified as a tubing string parting at a coupling in the 5.5" tubing at approximately 5,122 feet below mudline. The leak was stopped within 4 days by closing the SV2 needle valve on the subsea tree. An estimated 7 barrels of oil were released into the Gulf of Mexico between 8 May and 12 May 2022. Shell's response and reporting were found to be in compliance with federal regulations.

incident_summary: On February 25, 2014 at approximately 1500 hours, a contract employee fell approximately 90 feet from the rear of a crane into the Gulf of Mexico. The employee was cleaning oil from a skid when he lost his footing on a slippery beam due to humid and foggy conditions. During the fall, he struck a safety chain that was meant to barricade an opening to a ladder. The u-bolt connecting the safety chain to a stainless steel fixture broke under the impact, allowing the employee to fall the full 90 feet. The employee was rescued after grabbing an emergency escape rope on the north side of the facility and suffered severe injuries including a forehead laceration, broken nose, and broken tailbone.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: High-angle boom kick-out safety device
Role: Prevents boom from exceeding safe angle and contacting boom stops
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["The high angle boom kick out switch was inoperable due to its mounted position", "The crane mechanic had to readjust the position of the high-angle boom kick-out to initiate at 78 degrees", "the high angle boom-kickout failed to function allowing the boom to make contact with the boom stops"]
incident_summary: On 7 September 2022, damage to the boom heel section of the SM 192A platform crane was discovered during a monthly facility inspection. The damage consisted of bent vertical lacings and uniform bends in both upper chords of the boom heel section. Investigation revealed that the high-angle boom kick-out safety device had failed to function, allowing the boom to contact the boom stops and sustain damage. The root causes were identified as: (1) inoperable safety device due to incorrect mounted position, and (2) human performance error where crane operators failed to function test the high-angle kick-out despite checking it off on pre-use inspection forms on multiple occasions. The crane was de-rated and placed out of service.

incident_summary: On December 3, 2014, a sheen was reported in the vicinity of South Marsh Island Area Block 27. Pipeline Segment Number 3334 operated by Sea Robin Pipeline Company was identified as the source. A hole measuring 1/2" by 3/4" was located at the 6 o'clock position at the bottom of the line at 93 feet water depth. The spill volume was initially reported as 15 barrels but later revised to 0.7 barrels of condensate. The leak was secured on December 5, 2014, and repairs were completed by December 12, 2014. Investigation findings indicated no corrosion, no third-party damage, and normal cathodic protection readings, suggesting the probable cause was a manufacturing defect or isolated pit in the pipe.

incident_summary: On July 7, 2018 at approximately 1115 hours, a contract crane mechanic fell approximately 81 feet from a crane skid into the water while attempting to replace an anti-two block control valve at Fieldwood Energy's South Marsh Island 105 A platform. The mechanic had removed hydraulic hoses the previous day without properly tightening them, causing hydraulic oil to spill onto the crane skid. When returning to replace the valve, the mechanic climbed a 10-foot ladder placed between the crane and handrail, placed both feet on the crane skid (over 7 feet above deck), and fell backwards over the handrail when attempting to reposition his foot on the ladder. The mechanic may have slipped due to hydraulic oil. He was rescued approximately 45 minutes later and suffered fractures to the C-7 and lower lumbar vertebrae.

incident_summary: On 25-FEB-2024 at 0930 hours, a contract construction crew attempted to raise the elevation of an I-beam support by approximately 1 inch on the Talos OCS-G02280 SM 130 B production facility. After unsuccessful attempts with come-along winches, the crew attached the crane's main line to the fixed I-beam and applied tension. The crane operator exceeded the maximum load capacity of 21,500 lbs at the 55-degree boom angle by applying approximately 30,000 lbs of tension, resulting in damage to the crane boom heel section and lattice. Estimated repair cost: $400,000. No injuries occurred.

incident_summary: On May 12, 2023, at approximately 1605 hours, a crane boom collapse occurred on the Talos ERT LLC OCS-G02280 South Marsh Island 130 B Facility. While attempting to lower an 11,000 lb grating rack to the top deck, the boom cable broke causing the boom and load to collapse onto the deck. The boom cable failed due to fatigue from numerous cycles through the bridle sheaves, exacerbated by frequent operation at 60-to-75-degree boom angles and inadequate lubrication penetration. Total damage was $730,000 ($330,000 to deck and production equipment, $370,000 to crane). No injuries occurred.

incident_summary: On May 12, 2023, at approximately 1605 hours, a crane boom collapse occurred on the Talos ERT LLC OCS-G02280 South Marsh Island 130 B Facility. While attempting to lower an 11,000 lb grating rack to the top deck, the boom cable broke causing the boom and load to collapse onto the deck. The boom cable failed due to fatigue from numerous cycles through the bridle sheaves, exacerbated by frequent operation at 60-to-75-degree boom angles and inadequate lubrication penetration. Total damage was $730,000 ($330,000 to deck and production equipment, $370,000 to crane). No injuries occurred.

incident_summary: On 17 February 2020 at 07:55 hours, a Warrior Energy Services Electric Line Helper sustained a foot injury when a 1.25-inch tubular pipe slipped through single joint elevators during hoisting operations on a Fieldwood Energy platform at South Marsh Island Block 149D. The tubular fell and struck the worker's right foot, causing bruising. The root cause was determined to be improper equipment: the elevators were pre-dressed with dies sized for 1.5-inch pipe instead of 1.25-inch pipe. The elevator C-plate was installed upside down, obscuring the size stamp that would have allowed crew verification.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Compressor piston and rod maintenance inspection
Role: Detect early signs of component degradation
LOD Basis: Preventive maintenance to identify scarring and wear before failure
supporting_text: ["scarring was found on the piston. As per the compressor mechanic, the scarring that was discovered is generally a sign of poor maintenance"]
incident_summary: On January 16, 2024, at 0252 hours, the C1 compressor at Arena Offshore's SM-128B facility experienced a piston failure. The piston rod nut loosened due to component fault or wear, causing the two bottom connecting rod bolts to break. This allowed the piston rod nut to contact the head end of the cylinder and separate from the frame. As the housing broke, gas ignited. The vibration switch detected the abnormal vibration and tripped, but failed to initiate shutdown at the panel due to moisture in the output line that froze in the 17°F ambient temperature. The night operator discovered the fire, activated the fire alarm and ESD, alerted personnel, and the fire was extinguished without injuries.

incident_summary: On December 15, 2023, at 0520 hours, an 8-inch bi-directional gas pipeline (Segment 5114) containing 1220 psi ruptured on the Talos ERT LLC SM 130 B facility in the Gulf of Mexico. The rupture resulted in a fire that traveled approximately 80 feet from below the +10 level to the production deck. A 3-foot section of pipeline from the +4 to +7 elevation was missing. The fire lasted 5-7 minutes before gas pressure bled down. Life saving equipment, wiring, and minor structural damage occurred near the +10 level. No injuries were reported. Favorable wind conditions (28 mph blowing away from platform) prevented more severe damage. Root cause was determined to be severe external corrosion of the pipeline flange driven by high chlorine concentrations (12,000 ppm) in an oxygen-containing environment, combined with inadequate maintenance of protective coating and failure to complete required mechanical integrity inspection.

Role: Primary braking control to prevent uncontrolled load descent
LOD Basis: Primary mechanical brake system designed to control load descent
supporting_text: ["the driller's-side drawworks brake became non-responsive", "the Enterprise Driller was able to use the off-side drawworks brake to slow and control the descent", "the driller's-side retention roll pin located on the drawworks drum brake band assembly was broken"]
incident_summary: On 11 September 2019 at approximately 13:00 hours, while making up for a casing run on the Enterprise 263 jack-up rig, the driller's-side drawworks brake became non-responsive. The topdrive and traveling block began to descend uncontrollably. The driller used the off-side drawworks brake to slow the descent. The topdrive struck the top of the 7.625-inch casing in the hole and the traveling block fell against the casing stabbing board. Investigation revealed a non-OEM stainless steel retention roll pin had broken, causing brake band separation. Post-incident investigation found incorrect retaining roll pins installed in both drawworks brake band assemblies instead of required OEM cotter pins. No injuries occurred.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Grating clips securing deck grating
Role: Prevent grating from shifting or falling
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["The section of grating where the mechanic fell through was secured with grating clips and was not welded in place", "Cox did not properly maintain the clips that held the decking in place", "When the contract mechanic stepped on the grating, the grating shifted causing it to slip off the beam and fall to the water below"]
incident_summary: On 08 September 2020 at 1330 hours, a contract mechanic fell through corroded steel bar deck grating on Cox Operating LLC's South Pass 49 Platform A while walking near the fire water pump to retrieve a serial number. The grating, secured only with clips and not welded, gave way beneath the mechanic, creating an open hole. The mechanic fell chest deep but caught himself and pulled to safety. Initial injury assessment reported bruises to the upper left rib area, but subsequent medical evaluation revealed a meniscus tear in the left knee requiring surgery, resulting in a lost time accident greater than 3 days. Investigation revealed extensive corrosion of deck grating throughout the platform, with prior BSEE inspections having documented corrosion issues that had not been adequately addressed by the operator.

incident_summary: During personnel transfer from M/V Patience Lab to SP 93 B Platform using a 4-Man Billy Pugh basket, a sudden wind gust caused the crane boom to swing. The crane operator attempted to lock the swing brake but it failed to hold. The basket struck the vessel railing and platform structure, injuring three personnel. The fourth rider was suspended in the basket for approximately 15 minutes before being lowered back to the vessel.

incident_summary: On 09-OCT-2015 at 0015 hours, a fire ignited inside the galley/diner on Platform SP 93 B in the Gulf of Mexico. The fire burned for over 8 hours, with at least 4 hours of contained burning with both doors closed. Two operators on the platform detected the fire via alarm and attempted initial containment using the platform's Fire Water Cooling System. The Person In Charge shut in gas and oil pipelines and made emergency distress calls. A nearby vessel (M/V Canyon Runner) responded and assisted in extinguishing the fire using its water cannon. The fire caused extensive structural damage to the galley/diner. Root cause investigation identified improper installation of an electric stove/oven with insufficient clearance from combustible materials in the walls, allowing heat transfer to ignite wood paneling, insulation, and wiring. No personnel injuries occurred.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Standard Operating Procedures for compressor restart
Role: Administrative control for safe equipment operation
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["The LO restarted the compressor using the Standard Operating Procedures (SOP)"]
incident_summary: On October 20, 2014 at 1000 hours, a fire occurred on the Energy XXI Facility, Lease OCS-G 1619, South Pass 93-A platform. The Lead Operator was loading the #4 Arial Compressors after a shutdown. After restarting the compressor and switching the governor from manual to automatic mode, a loud popping sound was heard from the turbine area followed by a small flash fire approximately 6 inches long. The fire was extinguished using a handheld fire extinguisher with no injuries reported. Investigation revealed that minor metal fragments from previous repairs of the right bank #6 cylinder went unnoticed during installation, causing coolant to enter the cylinder and leading to a cascade failure involving lube oil sealing system damage and combustion.

incident_summary: On 18 July 2025 at 0730 hours, Motor Vessel Marc C allided with South Pass 27 #5 platform during platform surveying operations for decommissioning. The vessel captain, after completing surveys in the East Bay field and setting course to West Delta field, became distracted by computer work and failed to maintain proper lookout. The captain did not use the electronic navigation chart system that clearly identified platform structures. The allision resulted in damage to both the platform and vessel starboard bow, with no injuries or pollution.

incident_summary: On 21 September 2023 at 0745 hours, a loss of well control event occurred on South Pass 28 13(V) jacket. Production operators discovered an external leak on the production casing of Well 0693 #98, with a hydrocarbon sheen observed leaking into Gulf of Mexico waters. The total estimated spill volume was 65.7 gallons of oil. The well continued to leak until 30 September 2023 when pressure bled off to zero. The incident was caused by equipment failure due to inadequate preventive maintenance, corrosion, and erosion in a harsh shallow saltwater environment.
