barrier_role_match_text:

incident_summary: On 30 July 2025 at 13:10 CDT, a Supreme Services crew contracted by W&T Offshore was laying down a 12-inch mechanical casing cutter from Well C005 into a shipping basket on South Pass 49 C platform. While guiding the tool into the basket, the outermost casing bowl (approximately 250 pounds) unexpectedly separated and slid down the tool due to a 'grease lock' created by heavy buildup of grease and debris from prior cutting operations. A Supreme operator's left hand was caught between the bowl and tool, resulting in amputation of one finger and traumatic crush injuries to two others. The operator was evacuated and underwent medical treatment resulting in permanent dismemberment of three fingers.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: API Level 1 Structural Survey
Role: Detection of corrosion defects
LOD Basis: Third-party annual inspection
supporting_text: ["Review of the Level 1 surveys also revealed that the corroded area of the 8-inch gas vent line where the gas release occurred was not identified on the level 1 surveys", "The affected 8-inch gas pipe was not listed on the most recent Level 1 Surveys"]
incident_summary: On 03 November 2025 at 1205 hours, a gas release was detected on SP 62 C platform during a BSEE annual production inspection. A BSEE Inspector observed suspected gas vapors coming from a corroded area on an 8-inch gas vent line. Platform operators confirmed the gas release using a portable gas detector. The platform was manually shut in and the corroded gas line was replaced. The incident resulted in no injuries or pollution. Root causes were identified as inadequate equipment inspection and inadequate equipment maintenance, with contributing factors related to inadequate job procedures for routine corrosion inspections.

barrier_role_match_text:

supporting_text: ["The JSA did not include sequential steps or guidance on how to prevent corrosion from seizing the cap onto the PIG launcher", "The JSA also lacked guidance on how to eliminate or establish safety measures to prevent personnel from coming into contact with sharp edges", "The JSA was not updated once the davit/swing arm was broken, which increased the challenges of the task and risk to personnel", "The JSA was also missing a revision to identify a manual handling approach for lifting, holding, and threading the cap onto the PIG launcher"]
incident_summary: On 21 September 2023 at 1505 hours, an injured person (IP) suffered a laceration to the right hand while assisting two other operators in reinstalling a PIG launcher cap onto gas pipeline KZZ-602 on the cellar deck of unmanned Platform A, SP Block 83. The three-person crew removed the 12-inch cap for pipeline flushing operations. During removal, the lever arm assembly became damaged, forcing manual reinstallation. The crew mistakenly believed the cap was securely threaded after rotating it, and when they lessened their hold, the cap fell. The sharp edge of the lever arm shaft penetrated the IP's cut-resistant gloves, causing a laceration. The IP was evacuated by helicopter and treated at an onshore medical facility with eleven sutures.

incident_summary: On 5 June 2019 at 0915 hours, a construction employee partially fell through a corroded section of deck grating on the Fieldwood Energy Offshore LLC South Pass 60-D platform while retrieving a wrench for scaffold installation. The employee caught himself with his arms and elbows, sustaining minor scratches. The hole measured 16 inches long by 20 inches wide. Investigation revealed severe grating corrosion caused by inadequate preventive maintenance, lack of operator training on corrosion hazard identification, and possible accelerated corrosion from a nearby gas flowline producing condensation.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Prevent pressurized gas from being present during hot work

supporting_text: ["The JSEA identified that well A5 was flowing and needed to be shut-in prior to hot work commencing", "The primary cause of this incident was the failure to shut-in well A5 prior to commencing hot work", "The Production Operator was the only personnel on the facility with the knowledge to ensure the well was shut-in, and he was not present when the hot work started nor was he consulted with to ensure the well was shut-in"]
incident_summary: On August 2, 2017, a fire occurred on Ship Shoal Block 177 Platform A during decommissioning operations of well A4. A cutting crew was removing a casing valve using a cutting torch when a fire ignited at a weep hole on well A5's Surface Safety Valve (SSV). The fire was extinguished in approximately 3 seconds by the Fire Watch using a portable fire extinguisher. Investigation revealed that well A5 had a leaking diaphragm in its SSV which allowed gas to escape and be ignited by slag from the cutting torch. The primary cause was the failure to shut-in well A5 prior to commencing hot work, compounded by lack of communication between the cutting crew and the Production Operator.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Prevent formation of flammable mixture by removing air from process
LOD Basis: Primary control to prevent hazardous condition
supporting_text: ["the compressor was restarted without being purged or loaded", "air from the bleed points [was] not properly purged[,] which allowed a continuous source of air into the process and components", "purging was not even recognized as a key step in the job safety analysis (JSA)", "At the time of the incident, Renaissance had no general purging procedure in place"]
incident_summary: On July 16, 2017, at approximately 4:30 pm, a Waukesha Reciprocating Compressor on the Ship Shoal 198-J platform experienced an internal explosion during start-up operations. The compressor had undergone maintenance on July 12-15, 2017, during which internal components were exposed to atmosphere. During restart, the compressor was not purged or loaded before running idle for approximately ten minutes with the blowdown valve open. An LEL mixture of air and natural gas ignited within the process piping, causing the explosion and damaging the 3rd stage cooler and associated piping. The compressor mechanic experienced migraines and dizziness five days later. Probable causes were identified as failure to purge and inadequate job safety analysis.

incident_summary: On February 23, 2018, operators discovered oil discharging from the overboard water discharge line of the Sump Tank (ABH-1000) on SS Block 224-D platform. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Cargo manifest weight labeling and communication
Role: Ensure accurate load weight information is available to crane operator
LOD Basis: Administrative control to prevent overload condition at source
supporting_text: ["The operator's shorebase cargo manifest dated 6/3/2015, shows the total (gross) weight of several loads rather than the actual weights of each individual load.", "As shown on the manifest, the operators divided the 40,000-pound total weight by 3 to get the assumed (mistaken) weight of 13,300 pounds per lift.", "The information on the cargo manifest dated 6/3/2015, should have indicated actual weights per lift and not total weights."]
incident_summary: On June 18, 2015 at approximately 1300 hours, the platform crane boom on Ship Shoal 229 A suffered major structural failure while offloading equipment onto an offshore vessel. The crane operator was lowering a load approximately 10-20 feet above the vessel deck when boom swing control became inactive. The operator heard what sounded like failure of boom hydraulic pistons and the boom and load began lowering uncontrollably. When the operator attempted to raise the boom, it collapsed in the middle, dropping the load onto the starboard side and deck of the vessel. The root cause was that the crane operator used an assumed load weight of 12,000-14,000 pounds rather than verifying the actual weight of 20,300 pounds, which exceeded the maximum rated capacity of 15,000 pounds at a 70-degree boom angle.

LOD Basis: Engineering control to maintain crane structural integrity
supporting_text: ["The crane inspector noted on his report that he increased the hydraulic relief pressure from 2000 psi to 2500 psi.", "Both men noticed what sounded to them like failure of the boom hydraulic pistons and then realized that the boom and load were lowering uncontrollably."]
incident_summary: On June 18, 2015 at approximately 1300 hours, the platform crane boom on Ship Shoal 229 A suffered major structural failure while offloading equipment onto an offshore vessel. The crane operator was lowering a load approximately 10-20 feet above the vessel deck when boom swing control became inactive. The operator heard what sounded like failure of boom hydraulic pistons and the boom and load began lowering uncontrollably. When the operator attempted to raise the boom, it collapsed in the middle, dropping the load onto the starboard side and deck of the vessel. The root cause was that the crane operator used an assumed load weight of 12,000-14,000 pounds rather than verifying the actual weight of 20,300 pounds, which exceeded the maximum rated capacity of 15,000 pounds at a 70-degree boom angle.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Design specification for Complete Joint Penetration welds
Role: Prevent defective equipment design and manufacture
LOD Basis: Design control to prevent manufacture of defective equipment
supporting_text: ["Design Documentation failed to specify that Complete Joint Penetration (CJP) welds were required for the 'Pipe Stop Arm.'"]
incident_summary: On September 25, 2014, at 1350 hours, an employee working on the deck of Weatherford's Hydraulic Pulling Unit #4 was struck on the right arm by a Pipe Stop Arm (PSA) that fell 24 feet from the derrick. The PSA, weighing approximately 165 pounds and measuring 8 feet in length, broke off during the process of lowering it while racking back pipe from the A004 well. The injured person sustained no fractures or broken bones and was released to full duty the following day. Root cause analysis identified incomplete weld joint penetration and shallow/thin welding along the fractured weld, combined with overload conditions (tension, bending, and/or torsional).

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Ball check assemblies in gauge cock valves (top and bottom of sight glass)
Role: Prevent or mitigate oil discharge from sight glass failure
LOD Basis: Primary mechanical barrier to isolate pressurized vessel contents
supporting_text: ["the gauge cock valves located on the top and bottom of the Heater Treater's interface sight glass were not fully functional because there were no ball checks in either valve", "If the ball checks were in place, they could have prevented the flow and/or mitigated the flow of oil due to interface sight glass breakage"]
incident_summary: On January 5, 2017, at approximately 2:00 a.m., a fire occurred on Ship Shoal 266-A platform when oil discharged from a failed interface sight glass on the Heater Treater (NBK-1700) saturated protective insulation and contacted the hot exhaust manifold of Pipeline Pump #1 (PAX-4100), located 14 feet away. The sight glass failure was exacerbated by missing ball checks in the gauge cock valves, which allowed continuous oil discharge. All four crew members were evacuated and rescued. The fire was extinguished at approximately 5:50 a.m. Total property damage was estimated at $2.7 million.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Casing valve monitoring by drill crew
Role: Detection of well flow
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["A member of the drill crew had been positioned at the casing valve to monitor the well for flow", "At approximately 10:15am, the employee noticed fluid start to flow out of the casing valve at a rate of approximately 15 barrels per hour"]
incident_summary: On October 23, 2014, while performing drilling operations on the C-22 well, the Ensco 87 drilling rig experienced a well control incident during cementing operations. A gas bubble from the formation entered the well during the cement job and migrated up the well before the cement had time to cure. At approximately 10:15am, fluid began flowing from the casing valve at 15 barrels per hour. The crew cleared the well and closed the Diverter Annular at 11:00am, after which fluid discharged to a height of approximately 20 feet above the rig floor. For approximately 30 minutes, the well intermittently discharged a mixture of gas, mud, and cement from the Diverter line. The Emergency Shut Down was initiated and 62 personnel were evacuated. No injuries, equipment damage, or pollution were sustained.

A gas bubble from the formation entered the well during the cement job and migrated up the well before the cement had time to cure. At approximately 10:15am, fluid began flowing from the casing valve at 15 barrels per hour. The crew cleared the well and closed the Diverter Annular at 11:00am, after which fluid discharged to a height of approximately 20 feet above the rig floor. For approximately 30 minutes, the well intermittently discharged a mixture of gas, mud, and cement from the Diverter line. The Emergency Shut Down was initiated and 62 personnel were evacuated. No injuries, equipment damage, or pollution were sustained.

incident_summary: On March 28, 2022, at approximately 0830 hours, a D-Operator employed by Wood PLC was performing a general overview inspection of W&T Offshore's Ship Shoal Block 300 Platform B. The operator noticed a leak from a ¾ inch thermowell plug on a pipeline pump discharge line and attempted to tighten it. The plug released unexpectedly and struck the operator in the lower abdomen, causing significant injury requiring surgery. The operator had approximately 8 months of offshore experience. Investigation determined the incident was caused by operator error and lack of job safety analysis for routine operations.

incident_summary: On February 22, 2020, at approximately 15:30 hours, a Seatrax S100P temporary crane operator was transferring a 6-by-6-by-6-foot Conex box containing drinking water (approximately 3,000 pounds) from supply vessel John Harville to the platform at Ship Shoal Block 358. During the boom-down operation, the right side pendant line fell outside the boom frame and snagged on a bolt in the flange connecting the heel and mid-section of the boom. As the crane operator continued to boom down, the pendant line twisted the boom, causing significant structural damage. The crane was tagged out of service pending boom replacement. No injuries were reported.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)
Role: Prevent overpressure by relieving excess pressure
LOD Basis: Primary pressure relief device
supporting_text: ["The PSV likely became detached because of improper sizing. Poor threading alignment as evidenced by visible threads justifies this assumption.", "PSV not properly sized on reboiler, not taking into account the pressure build up by a ruptured line inside the reboiler."]
incident_summary: A 2-inch line inside a reboiler ruptured, causing vessel overpressure. The pressure safety valve (PSV) was forcibly dislodged due to improper sizing and poor threading alignment, releasing liquids into the atmosphere. These liquids contacted the heater treater, igniting a flash fire. The ESD was activated and the source isolated. No injuries occurred. Two Incidents of Noncompliance were issued for equipment maintenance and PSV sizing deficiencies.

incident_summary: On December 23, 2016, at approximately 1550 hours, personnel on the E-Auxiliary platform at Ship Shoal Block 154 noticed smoke coming from the exhaust stack of the Heater Treater (NBK-1800). After shutting off fuel gas, a small fire was observed inside the fire tube. The fire was extinguished with a handheld fire extinguisher. Investigation revealed corrosion and pitting in the welds of the fire tube, likely caused by caustic chemicals introduced into the production system that damaged the metal over time.

incident_summary: On December 13, 2021 at 11:15 hours, the auxiliary wire rope of the SS 72 OF crane failed during offloading operations at Sanare Energy Partners' Ship Shoal Block 72 Platform. A 4,000 lbs rental air compressor being offloaded from motor vessel Leader fell approximately 20-30 feet into offshore waters when the aux cable parted above the hook. The compressor was recovered five days later. Root cause was determined to be failure to lubricate the wire rope, allowing corrosion and disintegration. Contributing factors included inadequate maintenance, failure to follow manufacturer recommendations, and failure to take the crane out of service despite identified deficiencies.

incident_summary: On March 7, 2025, at 08:15 hours, a flash fire occurred at W&T Offshore's production facility at Ship Shoal Block 80, Platform A while attempting to bring Pipeline Pump #2 (PAX-360) online. A W&T Mechanic and Contractor Electrician sustained burn injuries to head and hands. The fire resulted from a loose fitting on the fuel gas starter exhaust piping that allowed gas to accumulate near the pump. When the engine backfired during startup, the accumulated gas ignited. Both injured personnel were evacuated and treated; the Electrician was released to return to work on April 9, 2025, while the Mechanic continued treatment. The incident was exacerbated by personnel not wearing required PPE (gloves) and wind direction that pushed flames toward one worker.

incident_summary: On August 25, 2018 at 0345 hours, a Floorhand employed by Helmerich & Payne was severely injured while being hoisted in the derrick of rig H&P 107 to inspect the top drive assembly following a jarring operation. The Floorhand's secondary safety static line became snagged on a monkey board finger while he was being hoisted upward. The opposing forces created by the moving hoist line and the snagged safety line caused the Floorhand to be pulled in opposite directions, resulting in severe injury. The Toolpusher operating the man rider winch had obstructed line of sight due to racked drill pipe and was not using a flagger as required by the JSA and Pre-Job Checklist. The Floorhand was evacuated via helicopter and transported to hospital for emergency medical treatment.

incident_summary: On August 25, 2018 at 0345 hours, a Floorhand employed by Helmerich & Payne was severely injured while being hoisted in the derrick of rig H&P 107 to inspect the top drive assembly following a jarring operation. The Floorhand's secondary safety static line became snagged on a monkey board finger while he was being hoisted upward. The opposing forces created by the moving hoist line and the snagged safety line caused the Floorhand to be pulled in opposite directions, resulting in severe injury. The Toolpusher operating the man rider winch had obstructed line of sight due to racked drill pipe and was not using a flagger as required by the JSA and Pre-Job Checklist. The Floorhand was evacuated via helicopter and transported to hospital for emergency medical treatment.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Prevent weld corrosion from caustic chemicals
LOD Basis: Primary barrier to prevent chemical attack on welds

incident_summary: On March 22, 2018, a fire occurred in the Heater Treater skid underneath the flame arrestor on the Ship Shoal 72 J platform. Operators discovered the fire during routine rounds and successfully extinguished it using a CO2 fire extinguisher while activating the fire suppression system and shutting off fuel gas supply. Investigation revealed corrosion on fire tube welds caused by caustic chemicals with low pH used in the production system. Combustible liquids entered the fire tube through corroded weld holes, causing hydrocarbon ignition. No injuries or major damages were reported.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Automatic boom angle limiter to prevent over-extension
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["The High Angle Kick Out (HAKO) system failed", "The HAKO was found to have a bent actuation valve and heavy corrosion", "Annual inspection report shows that the HAKO did not work during inspection and was replaced on October 22, 2020"]
incident_summary: On May 7, 2021 at 17:00 hours at South Timbalier Block 100, the crane's boom control lever became stuck in the upright position during backloading operations. The boom pulled up into the boom stops causing significant structural damage. The crane operator attempted to reverse the control lever without success, then kicked it forward to stop the boom. Investigation revealed multiple failures: corroded control valve and linkage, failed High Angle Kick Out (HAKO) system with bent actuation valve and heavy corrosion, and failed Emergency Shut Down (ESD) system. The crane was permanently removed from service.

barrier_role_match_text:

LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["The normal procedure for running drill pipe called for the top drive to be raised and to stop and wait for the floor hands to be ready to 'tail-in' the drill pipe into place.", "The AD took his eyes and attention off of the operation, and during this lapse in attention to the job, he broke from normal procedure by continuing to raise the top drive when he should have stopped and waited for the Floor Hands to move the drill pipe."]
incident_summary: On November 26, 2015 at 01:31 hours, a Floor Hand on the Rowan Gorilla IV suffered a laceration to his left hand and wrist when a stand of 4" drill pipe swung uncontrolled and crushed his hand between the pipe and an iron roughneck. The Assistant Driller (AD) was raising the top drive to pick up drill pipe when he noticed the throttle pedal felt different, looked down briefly, and failed to stop the operation. During this lapse in attention, the drill pipe swung freely and struck the Floor Hand. The incident was caused by lack of focus by the AD and failure of the crew to follow the Red Zone Policy.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Communications Tower lightning protection
Role: Prevent lightning strike damage
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["Damage to the Communications Tower, including charring and discoloration to the supporting structure in multiple areas were indicative of lightning damage", "the cause of the fire was lightning striking the Communications Tower and Communications Building"]
incident_summary: On May 2, 2021, at approximately 10:11 hours, a fire occurred on the unmanned, out-of-service ST-26 A/A-QTR fixed platform in South Timbalier Block 26, Lease OCS-G 01361, operated by Cox Operating, L.L.C. The fire was caused by lightning striking the Communications Tower and Communications Building during severe thunderstorms. The fire was intensified by hydrocarbons released from a nearby Dry Oil Tank that was damaged by intense heat, causing process piping to separate at welds. Multiple response vessels conducted water deluge operations for approximately 18 hours. The fire was completely extinguished by May 3, 2021. No injuries were reported. Total property damage was estimated at $2,000,000.

incident_summary: On June 10, 2017, at approximately 3:15 pm during normal startup operations on South Timbalier 26-A Platform, a Cooper gas compressor backfired after two failed restart attempts and adjustments to the magnetic pickup and flywheel. The backfire sent the scavenger box and debris airborne, igniting paper filters in the filter housing. A mechanic standing nearby was injured by flying debris to the chin and upper body. The probable cause was determined to be poor or improper gas purging techniques during compressor startup operations.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Fusible plug in platform fire loop system
Role: Detection and automatic shutdown trigger
LOD Basis: Automatic detection and response to fire
supporting_text: ["The fire had melted a fusible plug in the platform fire loop system, initiating a process shut-in which alerted the operating personnel."]
incident_summary: On October 11, 2017, at 0015 hours, a fire occurred on top of Gas Compressor No. 1 on the South Timbalier 28 PRD Platform. The fire was caused by a failure/rupture of the third stage cooler tube resulting in a gas release, with static charge as the probable ignition source. The fire melted a fusible plug in the platform fire loop system, initiating a process shut-in alarm that alerted operating personnel. The fire was extinguished immediately and the compressor was secured.

incident_summary: On May 17, 2014, at 0830 hours, while performing abandonment operations on Walter Oil & Gas's #001 well on the Rowan Gorilla IV, three employees were burned by a flash fire. The crew was welding pad eyes to the inside of a 36-inch casing to enable removal. Burning slag fell inside the wellbore, igniting gas that had migrated from the reservoir after the casing was cut. The three employees suffered first and second degree burns to their upper bodies. Investigation revealed gas was present at approximately 40 feet depth inside the casing but not at the surface where employees were working.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Ball valve self-locking mechanism
Role: Prevent inadvertent opening of drain valve
LOD Basis: Primary mechanical control to prevent valve opening
supporting_text: ["operator inadvertently opened a ball valve connected to the bottom of the LSH bridle with his foot/shin while exiting the area", "It is possible the self-locking mechanism on the ball valve handle was stuck in the 'up' position at the time the incident occurred"]
incident_summary: At approximately 1200 hours on January 23, 2020, at South Timbalier Block 311 Platform A, an operator inadvertently opened a ball valve connected to the bottom of an in-service LP Gas Compressor 3rd stage scrubber LSH with his foot/shin while exiting the area. This released approximately 1100 psi through a 3/8" stainless steel tubing drain line approximately 7 feet in length. The pressure release caused a whipping action in the drain tubing striking the operator on the side of the face/hardhat. Hydrocarbons from the 3rd stage suction scrubber vented toward the in-service LP gas compressor engine and ignited. Personnel activated the manual ESD station, signaled the fire alarm, and all personnel mustered. An attempt to use a nearby 125 lb. wheeled fire extinguisher unit failed to discharge. Fire water was used to extinguish the fire. Personnel reported first aid injuries (scraps, bumps, and skin redness). The fire caused damage to the compressor engine electrical components/wiring and the 3rd stage discharge PSV.

incident_summary: During the second transfer of two air hoisting work baskets from the boat landing of Platform A to the deck of the liftboat Raimy Eymard, a paint crew member's right hand was caught between a guardrail and a tag line, causing fractures to his index, middle, and pinky fingers. The injured person attempted to control the swing using a tag line wrapped around the guardrail, but his hand was caught during the lift.

incident_summary: On May 9, 2025, at approximately 0900 hours, a crane load block failure occurred during offloading operations at Platform A in Block 229, South Timbalier Area, approximately 50 miles offshore in the Gulf of America. While lowering a Job Safety Analysis document in a trash bag attached to the load block hook, the crane cable lost tension and fell approximately 65 feet onto the aft deck of a motor vessel. The load block sheave was ejected upward and fell onto the vessel deck, striking a grocery box. A deckhand sustained a lumbar muscle strain while evading the falling components. Root cause analysis revealed inadequate lubrication of the main block sheave bearings due to internal debris obstruction in the lubrication passage, leading to bearing failure, sheared dowel pin, and sheave pin dislodgement. Contributing factors included operator inattention to distress indicators, inadequate training on early mechanical failure signs, and unsafe work practices involving maintaining the suspended load at excessive height above the vessel.

incident_summary: On May 9, 2025, at approximately 0900 hours, a crane load block failure occurred during offloading operations at Platform A in Block 229, South Timbalier Area, approximately 50 miles offshore in the Gulf of America. incident_summary: On January 29, 2024 at approximately 1745 hours, a fire occurred on the Glycol Reboiler vent line at Cantium LLC facility in South Timbalier Block 23. The facility had shut in due to a Level Safety Low alarm on the Glycol Surge Tank. incident_summary: On September 3, 2018, approximately 517 barrels of synthetic based mud (SBM) were inadvertently discharged into the Gulf of Mexico during a fluid transfer operation from the work boat 'Esplanade' to the H&P 100 platform rig. The discharge was caused by a gasket failure in a 4-inch butterfly valve combined with the absence of a secondary TODO Dry Break valve that had been removed but not reinstalled. incident_summary: On September 15, 2017, during a boom rest operation on the South Timbalier 34B Platform, the crane boom experienced structural failure. The two bottom bolts connecting the midsection and heel section failed, causing excessive stress on the top bolts. The top section of the boom collapsed (jackknifed) to the right and dropped, eventually hanging by the main and auxiliary cables on the west side of the platform. The probable cause was a recent boom/boom stop contact that damaged the lower bolts. Heavy corrosion may have contributed to weakening the bolts. No reportable injuries occurred.

incident_summary: On December 1, 2021, at approximately 19:30 hours, the west crane boom on the H&P 100 rig at Viosca Knoll 989 A experienced a catastrophic failure and free fell. incident_summary: On December 1, 2021, at approximately 19:30 hours, the west crane boom on the H&P 100 rig at Viosca Knoll 989 A experienced a catastrophic failure and free fell. The boom damaged a 45-person life capsule and platform handrails. No personnel were in the crane cab at the time. Investigation revealed that the ratchet and pawl locking assembly was not properly adjusted after boom winch replacement on October 14, 2021, causing failure of the load-holding mechanism. Contributing factors included motor overheating, sprag clutch slipping, and inadequate hydraulic system pressure.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Prevent crack formation in expansion joint

incident_summary: On 22 December 2020 at 15:30 hours, a small fire occurred at Viosca Knoll (VK) 900 Platform A production platform operated by Cox Operating, LLC. A flame was noticed on the pipeline pump's exhaust insulation. Operators extinguished the fire using a handheld fire extinguisher in under a minute. The root cause was a 6 to 8 inch crack in the expansion joint that allowed hot exhaust to contact the engine's left bank air filter housing, causing a plastic dust cover to melt and drip onto the exhaust blanket, resulting in a small flame. No injuries, pollution, or property damage occurred. One pipeline was taken out of service.

incident_summary: On 28 March 2021 at approximately 1115 hours, a fire occurred on Pipeline Pump #2 exhaust piping at Viosca Knoll Block 900 A platform. incident_summary: On 22 July 2023 at approximately 1240 hours, a fire occurred on VK 900 A Platform when diesel from a tote tank spilled onto the intake housing of the #1 turbine compressor. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Gas Detection System
Role: Detection of gas release to alert personnel
LOD Basis: Detects gas release and triggers alarm response
supporting_text: ["the VK-915A platform control room received a gas alarm on the Compressor #1", "An Occidental Petroleum Control Room Operator (CRO) immediately traveled to the Compressor #1 and witnessed the nipple on the sight glass level bridle for the number one suction scrubber releasing gas", "The gas detectors detected the gas release and alarmed personnel who were then able to shut in the platform"]
incident_summary: On 24 November 2024 at 1710 hours, a 3/4 inch pipe nipple on the sight glass level bridle of Compressor #1 Suction Scrubber (MBF P630A-1) cracked and broke, releasing gas from the platform. The gas alarm activated, alerting the Control Room Operator who witnessed the release and initiated platform shutdown via the Emergency Shutdown System. A platform muster was conducted with no injuries reported. The broken nipple was replaced and the facility returned to production within 30 minutes. The incident was caused by fatigue cracking of the nipple due to extended compressor vibrations, over-tightening during original installation, possible loosening causing excessive vibration, and excessive weight from the sight glass level bridle support.

incident_summary: On 04 May 2025 at 0445 hours, motor vessel Adriatic (178-foot supply boat) struck the Marlin Tension Leg Platform at Viosca Knoll Block 915. The vessel's bow contacted the Northwest pontoon and stern contacted the Southwest pontoon, resulting in damage to production risers (Dorado and Crown and Anchor), internal gas lift line, and firewater piping. No injuries, environmental pollution, or stability issues were reported. The allision was caused by the vessel captain's inattention to navigation while performing administrative tasks and the absence of a second watch stander on the bridge.

incident_summary: On 02 December 2024 at approximately 0830 hours, a gas release was detected during pressurization testing of a newly replaced Subsea Control Module (SCM) pod at VK 989 Platform A. A Remote Operated Vehicle (ROV) identified gas bubbles emanating from a 90-degree bend segment in subsea flowline pipeline segment 10688. The flowline was immediately isolated and pressure testing suspended. The incident is suspected to have resulted from corrosion at a previous field repair location where field welds may have had imperfections.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Casing Pressure Monitoring and Bleed-Off Procedures
Role: Detect and relieve sustained casing pressure to prevent buildup
LOD Basis: Routine monitoring and pressure relief are primary controls to prevent pressure accumulation
supporting_text: ["Casing pressure was bled to zero and monitored", "Casing pressure diagnostics were performed on 27-Jan-2015. The pressure at the beginning of the bleed off was 1,250psi and could not be bled to zero psi", "The monthly casing pressure recordings remained at or below 100psi"]
incident_summary: On 19-June-2015 at 0605 hours, a production operator on platform VR-279 discovered gas and oil leaking from well A3/A3D through a hole in the 20 inch drive pipe at the +10 bell guide and from the 20 inch x 10 3/4 inch annulus below the wellhead base plate. Approximately 0.67 gallons of oil leaked into the Gulf of Mexico. Root cause analysis determined that a gas compressor failure on 16-June-2015 caused production shutdown, allowing reservoir shut-in pressure to build. This pressure caused pump through plugs to fail, allowing gas and oil to enter deteriorated short string tubing, then the 7 5/8 inch production casing, and finally the 10 3/4 inch surface casing, which had a leak approximately 10 feet below the wellhead. The surface casing leak allowed pressurized fluids to enter the non-pressure-containing 20 inch drive pipe, creating open leak paths to the atmosphere.

incident_summary: On 13-Nov-2014, the Hercules 300 was completing well A5 when approximately 66 barrels of 16.6 ppg Zinc Bromide completion fluid was discharged overboard. incident_summary: On June 24, 2022, at approximately 12:30 pm during completion operations at VR-369 platform A, the wire rope guard assembly for the main load line idler sheave carrier fell 103 feet to the platform deck. The incident occurred when the auxiliary load line jumped over the main load line idler sheave carrier plate and came to rest on the wire rope guard, causing abrasion damage. The root cause was identified as flawed equipment design of the American Aero OM 450 platform crane, where the auxiliary load line and boom line run in close proximity to the main load line idler sheave carrier, allowing contact during high-speed operation.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Fire tube inspection and maintenance program
Role: Detect and prevent fire tube degradation before failure
LOD Basis: Preventive maintenance to identify corrosion before pin hole develops
supporting_text: ["there were neither any records nor recollection within BEEOO to support if and/or when prior maintenance/inspection had been performed with regards to the fire tube", "There was a lack of documentation onboard the facility showing that the fire tube had ever been removed from the vessel and inspected in the past", "BEEOO in-house Root Cause Analysis (RCA) from December 30, 2013 had identified that due to large quantities of acid being pumped directly to the treater (100 - 150 gallons per week), inspection of the fire tube should be performed; however, this had not taken place prior to the incident"]
incident_summary: On April 18, 2014 at approximately 1900 hours, a fire occurred in the Heater Treater (NBK-2100) on the VR369A platform operated by Black Elk Energy Offshore Operating LLC in the Gulf of Mexico. The fire was caused by a pin hole that developed in the fire tube due to pit corrosion from frequent high-dosage batch treatments with acid-based de-emulsifier (Gyptron TA-21). Produced oil leaked through the pin hole into the fire tube and sprayed near the main burner, providing a steady fuel source. The operator responded to a Burner Safety Low alarm, isolated gas supply manually, activated the ESD station, and alerted personnel. The fire was successfully extinguished using handheld fire extinguishers. The incident resulted in $15,000 in property damage to the fire tube.

incident_summary: On April 18, 2014 at approximately 1900 hours, a fire occurred in the Heater Treater (NBK-2100) on the VR369A platform operated by Black Elk Energy Offshore Operating LLC in the Gulf of Mexico. The fire was caused by a pin hole that developed in the fire tube due to pit corrosion from frequent high-dosage batch treatments with acid-based de-emulsifier (Gyptron TA-21). Produced oil leaked through the pin hole into the fire tube and sprayed near the main burner, providing a steady fuel source. The operator responded to a Burner Safety Low alarm, isolated gas supply manually, activated the ESD station, and alerted personnel. The fire was successfully extinguished using handheld fire extinguishers. The incident resulted in $15,000 in property damage to the fire tube.

supporting_text: ["BSEE has determined FWE did not follow API 2D sixth edition during the setting of the packer", "API RP 2D 6th Edition Appendix B.3.2.3 states 'Guidelines for moving the load are as follows: Before starting to lift, the following conditions shall be verified: 2. The load is free to be lifted'", "FWE had no intentions of lifting the packer while setting the packer in the 7 5/8 inch casing"]
incident_summary: On February 10, 2020, at 5:45 am, a shock loading incident occurred with the platform crane at Vermilion Block 371 while setting a tension packer during plug and abandonment operations. The packer's shear ring failed at 26,000 pounds of pull (below its 40,000 pound rating) due to the ring not being changed after previous settings per manufacturer instructions. The sudden load reduction from 26,000 to 9,000 pounds shock loaded the crane, damaging the 9/16 inch diameter Dyform 6 boom cable. Root causes included failure to follow API RP 2D guidelines, use of incorrect Job Safety Analysis, crew misunderstanding of shear ring rating (believed 50,000 pounds instead of 40,000 pounds), and non-compliance with manufacturer instructions to change shear ring after each setting.

incident_summary: On August 11, 2024, at approximately 7:10 p.m., an explosion and fires occurred on the deck of the liftboat Brazos at Vermilion Block 170 A during a well cleanup and test operation. Two Production Technology & Services Inc. (PTS) workers sustained second-degree burns and required evacuation by USCG. The incident resulted from improperly latched hatches on a 500-barrel tank allowing gas to escape, which accumulated near a rental diesel generator. The generator's faulty electronic control module failed to shut down the engine when it overran due to gas ingestion, causing ignition of the gas plume. Contributing factors included lack of written procedures, inadequate hazard analysis, poor maintenance of tank seals, and failure of the generator's automatic emergency shutdown system.

incident_summary: On August 11, 2024, at approximately 7:10 p.m., an explosion and fires occurred on the deck of the liftboat Brazos at Vermilion Block 170 A during a well cleanup and test operation. Two Production Technology & Services Inc. (PTS) workers sustained second-degree burns and required evacuation by USCG. The incident resulted from improperly latched hatches on a 500-barrel tank allowing gas to escape, which accumulated near a rental diesel generator. The generator's faulty electronic control module failed to shut down the engine when it overran due to gas ingestion, causing ignition of the gas plume. Contributing factors included lack of written procedures, inadequate hazard analysis, poor maintenance of tank seals, and failure of the generator's automatic emergency shutdown system.

barrier_role_match_text:

incident_summary: On April 4, 2024, at approximately 10:30 PM, an employee of Cajun Cutters Inc. was struck in the left eye and face by a pressurized gas release from the open top of the tree of well A-15 while reconfiguring a tree cap connection. The injured person had been on duty for 15.5 hours. The incident resulted in evacuation, emergency medical treatment, partial permanent hearing loss, and ongoing evaluation for future eye surgeries. Root causes included failure to implement proper Lockout/Tagout procedures, inadequate written procedures, designation of an inexperienced operator for the overnight shift, and lack of safe working surfaces.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Boom hoist limit device
Role: Automatic mechanical stop to prevent boom from exceeding maximum angle

supporting_text: ["The boom reached the upper boom hoist limit device but it failed to function; most likely because the extension shaft was loose and bent thus allowing the boom to exceed its maximum angle height and come into contact with the boom stops.", "Boom hoist limit device failed to automatically stop the boom hoist winch when the boom reached its maximum predetermined high angle."]
incident_summary: On 16-DEC-2015 at approximately 1845 hours, a crane incident occurred at Fieldwood Energy's Vermillion 261-A-Aux facility during boom positioning activities. The boom hoist limit device failed to function, allowing the boom to exceed its maximum angle height and contact the boom stops, resulting in bent boom chords and bent boom stops. Contributing factors included failure of pre-use inspection to identify a loose and bent extension shaft on the boom hoist limit device, a boom angle indicator obscured by paint overspray and grime, and failure of the job safety analysis to identify hazards associated with close proximity of load to crane's minimum boom radius and low lighting conditions.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Nondestructive testing (Dye Penetrant) of refurbished hydraulic boom cylinders
Role: Detection of weld defects prior to installation
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["Both of the failed HBCs had inferior quality welds that went undetected during a nondestructive test (Dye Penetrant) after refurbishment.", "Metallurgical tests conducted post incident showed the first failed HBC to have 'a poor weld with multiple voids and a lack of penetration'"]
incident_summary: A hydraulic box boom crane at Castex Offshore's Vermilion 252-A facility experienced two consecutive hydraulic boom cylinder (HBC) failures where the cylinder rod separated from the cylinder rod eye. The first failure occurred two days after installation of a refurbished unit, and the second failure occurred twenty-four days after replacement. Investigation revealed that both failed cylinders had inferior quality welds with voids and lack of penetration, and more critically, the refurbished cylinders had a working stroke roughly one inch different from the originals. This mismatch created applied tension instead of compression, causing rod eye failure. Both failures occurred with little or no hook load and resulted in no personnel injury or additional crane damage.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Required Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) for SOBM handling
Role: Primary barrier to prevent skin contact with SOBM
LOD Basis: Direct contact prevention
supporting_text: ["The IP did not have the proper PPE when SOBM splashed on his clothing", "At the time of the incident, the IP was not wearing barrier cream, tyvek suit, nitrile gloves, face shield and goggles or a mono shield", "The SOBM soaked through his clothing contacting his legs causing a Contact Dermatitis rash"]
incident_summary: On July 1, 2019, at 2:00 pm, during pulling of workstring (POOH) operations on the Ensco 102 rig at Vermilion Block 369, a floor hand was splashed with Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SOBM) on his legs. The injured person was not wearing required personal protective equipment (barrier cream, tyvek suit, nitrile gloves, face shield, and goggles). The SOBM soaked through his clothing, causing contact dermatitis with lesions, scabbed blisters, and signs of infection. The injured person continued working and did not report the injury until July 7, when he sought medical attention. He was evacuated by helicopter for onshore medical treatment. Investigation revealed inconsistent completion of Job Safety Analysis (JSA) forms, inadequate hazard identification in pre-tour safety meetings, and failure of supervision to enforce PPE requirements.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Removal of flammable chemicals and hydrocarbons from work area
Role: Primary control to eliminate ignition source proximity
LOD Basis: 30 CFR 250.113(a) and W&T Hot Work Checklist line-item 17
supporting_text: ["The Zealous Job Safety Analysis (JSA) clearly states 'Remove all FLAMMABLE OR COMBUSTABLE ITEMS, Make sure no Hydrocarbons are present.'", "Written procedures in submitted documents clearly states to remove hydrocarbons prior to hot work commencing.", "The construction crew elected to use the third control measure of the W&T Offshore Hot Work Checklist, which was erecting fire blanket barriers between the work area and the chemical storage area, versus removing all flammable or combustible items as per the Zealous JSA."]
incident_summary: On February 21, 2024, at approximately 1349 hours, a fire occurred at the HI A 379 B facility during construction operations. A construction fitter was using an electric grinder to smooth welds on deck plating when sparks migrated through two layers of protective fire blanket barriers into a chemical/methanol storage area located approximately six feet from the grinding area, igniting flammable materials. The fire was extinguished and the facility was shut down. Investigation revealed that written procedures required removal of flammable chemicals from the work area, but the construction crew elected to use fire blanket barriers as an alternative control measure, which proved inadequate.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Sand Hopper Pre Job Check List
Role: Inspection and verification of equipment condition prior to offshore deployment
LOD Basis: Initial equipment inspection before use
supporting_text: ["On July 10, 2014 a 'Sand Hopper Pre Job Check List' was completed prior to sending the equipment offshore; however, this inspection failed to identify the poor condition of the bolt threads."]
incident_summary: On 24-OCT-2014 at approximately 1455 hours during routine sandblast and paint operations on Arena Offshore's West Cameron 544-A facility, the top hatch lid of a sand hopper failed catastrophically due to poor condition of fastening components. The lid dislodged, was deflected by the upper rack assembly, and struck a blast and paint crew member along a nearby walkway, causing significant injuries including head lacerations, finger damage, mandible fracture, broken teeth, and pelvic fracture. Investigation revealed worn bolt threads with inconsistent washers and mismatched nuts, two of which were completely missing. Pre-job inspection procedures and daily maintenance checklists were not properly followed.

incident_summary: On November 27, 2021, at approximately 09:30 am, a flash fire occurred while removing skid pads with a torch on the top deck at West Cameron Block 96 platform B. Slag and sparks from hot work operations fell onto the unsealed 24-inch manway cover of the water skimmer (MBM-0500), which contained undetermined amounts of condensate and combustible gas vapors. The fire self-extinguished immediately. Root causes included incomplete vessel cleaning, loose manway cover securing, lack of gas detection survey, inadequate fire-retardant tarp placement, and failure to conduct jobsite walkthroughs before permit approval.

The fire self-extinguished immediately. Root causes included incomplete vessel cleaning, loose manway cover securing, lack of gas detection survey, inadequate fire-retardant tarp placement, and failure to conduct jobsite walkthroughs before permit approval.

supporting_text: ["In the JSA, the total weight to be lifted was not mentioned nor were the limitations of the slings described.", "The JSA did not have written threats of the dynamic loading of waves during the lift or the lift hanging up in the 48-inch caisson.", "In the JSA, the total weight of the lift was not written."]
incident_summary: On August 10, 2023, at approximately 5:15 pm, during crane operations to remove a water abrasive cutter from a 48-inch caisson at WC-33 #1 platform, a 2.5-ton wire rope sling in the lifting arrangement parted and struck an Offshore Technical Services (OTS) employee on the head and neck. The incident occurred when the abrasive cutter hung up in the caisson simultaneously with a wave surge, introducing dynamic loading that exceeded the safe working limit of the sling. The injured person sustained a forehead laceration requiring 16 stitches and a C6 vertebrae fracture. Root causes included inadequate Job Safety and Environmental Analysis (JSA) that did not address dynamic loading from adverse sea conditions (5-7 foot sea state), failure to call an all-stop after the initial hang-up, and personnel positioned near the suspended load contrary to JSA requirements.

incident_summary: On 13-APR-2019 at 0615 hours, a hammer union pump connection (Type 1502, 40.6 lbs) fell 36.8 feet from a tubing joint being lifted by liftboat crane during workover operations on the A1 well at WC-61A platform. The pump connection struck a DSS worker on the hard hat, upper right arm, right thigh, and right foot, fracturing the humerus and lacerating the big toe. The root cause was improper securing of the pump connection to the tubing joint; during the second attempt to make the connection with the work string, the crane sling unscrewed the pump connection as workers turned the tubing joint clockwise. Contributing factors included poor communication regarding shift progress, inadequate supervision, missing work permits, complacency, inattention to task detail, and failure to follow JSA procedures.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Fuel system gasket integrity and maintenance
Role: Prevent fuel gas leak from cylinder head fuel injection line
LOD Basis: Primary engineering barrier to prevent gas release
supporting_text: ["The No. 6 left bank cylinder head fuel injection line had gas leaking due to a failed gasket where the machined flange bolts to the cylinder head", "The findings indicated that the cause of the gas leak was a cut gasket on the fuel valve jumper line"]
incident_summary: On 29-NOV-2020 at 0657 hours, a flash fire occurred during maintenance troubleshooting of a gas compressor engine (CBA-2030) at the West Delta Block 105 Platform E. A mechanic was troubleshooting a non-firing No. 6 left bank power cylinder. When the mechanic disconnected the secondary spark plug wire from the ignition coil, a spark was introduced into an area containing leaked fuel gas at explosive concentrations. The gas ignited, causing a flash fire. The root cause was a cut gasket on the fuel valve jumper line that allowed gas to leak. The mechanic immediately activated the ESD system, descended the catwalk, and extinguished the fire with a fire extinguisher. No injuries, pollution, or property damage occurred. The platform was shut in until repairs were completed and permission to restart was granted by BSEE on 01-DEC-2020. The probable cause was identified as inadequate hazard analysis in the Job Safety Analysis (JSA), failure to use a gas detector, and failure to utilize a hot work permit.

Role: Automatically shut down equipment and sound alarm upon fire detection
LOD Basis: Automated response to limit escalation of fire incident
supporting_text: ["The mechanic immediately climbed down the catwalk, activated the Emergency Shut Down (ESD) system, and grabbed a fire extinguisher. The ESD automatically sounded the fire alarm"]
incident_summary: On 29-NOV-2020 at 0657 hours, a flash fire occurred during maintenance troubleshooting of a gas compressor engine (CBA-2030) at the West Delta Block 105 Platform E. A mechanic was troubleshooting a non-firing No. 6 left bank power cylinder. When the mechanic disconnected the secondary spark plug wire from the ignition coil, a spark was introduced into an area containing leaked fuel gas at explosive concentrations. The gas ignited, causing a flash fire. The root cause was a cut gasket on the fuel valve jumper line that allowed gas to leak. The mechanic immediately activated the ESD system, descended the catwalk, and extinguished the fire with a fire extinguisher. No injuries, pollution, or property damage occurred. The platform was shut in until repairs were completed and permission to restart was granted by BSEE on 01-DEC-2020. The probable cause was identified as inadequate hazard analysis in the Job Safety Analysis (JSA), failure to use a gas detector, and failure to utilize a hot work permit.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Pipeline Burial/Cover Protection
Role: Physical barrier to prevent trawl net contact with pipeline infrastructure
LOD Basis: Regulatory requirement 30 CFR 250.1003(a)(2) mandates at least 3 feet of cover for pipeline tie-ins and similar obstructive items
supporting_text: ["Neither the application nor the completion report contains any indication that the spool was buried or covered", "30 CFR 250.1003(a)(2) states that pipeline valves, taps, tie-ins, capped lines, and repaired sections that could be obstructive shall be provided with at least 3 feet of cover", "The BSEE GOMR Pipeline Section does not contain any approved departures for this pipeline segment"]
incident_summary: On September 9, 2018, at 1130 hours, a trawling vessel snagged a bi-directional pipeline (Segment No. 15815) in the Gulf of Mexico, causing it to rupture and release approximately 0.8 gallons of hydrocarbon. The pipeline failed due to excessive stresses imposed when the trawler's net snagged on an 8-inch to 6-inch tie-in spool and dragged it until failure. The tie-in spool, installed in 2007 as part of a Right-of-Way modification, was not sufficiently buried or covered as required by regulations. The incident resulted in an estimated $2,225,000 in property damage.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Glycol startup procedure with temperature rate specifications
Role: Prevent rapid temperature increase and overpressure
LOD Basis: Industry standard (Gly-Tech TEG Systems Manual recommends heating to no more than 220°F before startup circulation)
supporting_text: ["The Renaissance Glycol start up procedure did not provide specifics on the rate of temperature increase when starting the reboiler after an extended shut in.", "According to Gly-Tech's TEG Systems Manual (an industry recognized training manual), 'heat the reboiler to no more than 220 degrees F before start up circulation.'", "The Operator increased the temperature from 200 to 250 degrees F without circulating glycol and allowing enough time to boil off water."]
incident_summary: On January 17, 2019, at 0930 hours, a fire occurred in the glycol dehydration unit on Renaissance Offshore's WD 152-A platform. During platform startup following an extended shutin, the Field Foreman started the glycol reboiler and increased temperature from 200°F to 250°F without proper circulation time or adherence to industry guidelines. The #1 compressor shut down due to high pressure, and the Field Foreman left the glycol unit unattended. Rapid temperature rise caused overpressure in the reboiler due to high water content (21% vs. typical 5-10%), forcing Triethylene Glycol (TEG) and liquid contaminates out of the still column onto the hot stack (operating temperature >660°F). The TEG ignited upon contact with the stack, creating a large flame. Personnel responded by activating ESD, fire alarms, and manually fighting the fire with fire reels. No injuries or pollution were reported. Damage included fire-damaged aluminum shell, broken sight glass, melted pressure gauges, burned insulation, heat-damaged motor/belt, damaged electrical wiring, and damaged asbestos panels. Root causes were inadequate startup procedures and lack of operator training on glycol phase vaporization characteristics.

incident_summary: On 24 November 2019 at 0700 hours, a fire occurred on West Delta 27 A Platform involving the cooler fan tower package (HAL 1161/62/63) for gas compressor unit CBA-1101. The fire was caused by ignition of escaping gas from a ruptured cooling tube in the third stage cooler (HAL-1163) when it contacted the compressor's hot exhaust system operating above 900°F. Personnel extinguished the fire within six minutes with no injuries. Equipment damage was limited to $2,040 in charred metal on the cooling tower. The facility's TSE and ESD systems functioned as designed, safely shutting down production and mitigating consequences.

incident_summary: On 09 January 2021 at approximately 0600 hours, a pin hole leak developed in the inlet suction piping of the gas compressor on West Delta 30 Platform P. The leak activated the Pressure Safety Low (PSL) system, which automatically shut down the gas compressor, LP separator, and all nine LP production wells. The leak was caused by excessive corrosion resulting from Cox's failure to maintain protective coatings on the process piping. No loss of containment or pollution was observed. A temporary clamp was installed with BSEE approval and the platform was returned to production within 4 hours.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Wet Oil Transfer Pump (PBA-410E)
Role: Prevent oil accumulation in floatation cell oil chamber by transferring oil to storage
LOD Basis: Primary mechanical control to prevent oil level rise
supporting_text: ["The Wet Oil Transfer Pump (PBA-410E) required to transfer oil from the Floatation Cell's (ABM-410) oil chamber, failed to pump due to a stuck, internal pump shifting/slider valve."]
incident_summary: On 21 September 2016 at 0828 hours, BSEE Inspectors discovered an oil sheen 7 miles long and 1 mile wide originating from WD 32 E Platform. Investigation revealed that the Wet Oil Transfer Pump (PBA-410E) failed due to a stuck internal pump shifting/slider valve, preventing oil transfer from the Floatation Cell (ABM-410). Oil level in the oil chamber rose to the level of the produced water side and flowed back into the overboard discharge chamber. The Fisher 2500 Level Controller maintained preset water discharge level, causing oil to discharge through the overboard water outlet. The Level Safety High (LSH 1) shut down device was not installed in the oil chamber as required, preventing automatic platform shut-in. Approximately 8.05 barrels of crude oil were discharged into Gulf waters. The unmanned facility was in-service and producing at the time of discovery.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: API 2D Paragraph 3.1.5 Operating Practices - Cab cleanliness
Role: Prevent clipboard obstruction of controls
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["CO did not follow API 2D Paragraph 3.1.5 Operating Practices and Energy XXI Crane Policy ensuring the cab was free of operating obstacles and trash", "a clipboard fell between the boom lever and the crane cab window causing the boom to rise up further"]
incident_summary: On July 30, 2014 at 2030 hours at WD 32-E Platform, a crane incident occurred during wireline operation rigging down. A clipboard fell between the boom lever and crane cab window, causing the boom to rise and activate the boom kick-out. The right-side boom kick-out failed to retract due to improper maintenance, allowing the boom to contact the boom stops and sustain structural damage. No injuries occurred. Root causes included crane operator failure to follow API 2D and Energy XXI policies regarding cab cleanliness, improper maintenance of boom kick-outs, and failure to perform pre-use inspection of high angle kick-out.

incident_summary: On 27 August 2025 at 1045 hours, the WD-152 A platform crane failed during a dynamic lift of an 8,000-pound Power Pack from a Motor Vessel. The crane's boom cable separated, causing the crane boom to fall uncontrollably and strike platform handrails and the vessel below. The root cause was a failed bearing in the boom cable bridle sheave assembly that caused excessive wear to the sheave pin, allowing the cable to cut through the sheave and ultimately fail under load. No injuries occurred, but damage was sustained to platform handrails, the crane assembly, and the Motor Vessel. A small amount of hydraulic fluid was released into the Gulf of America.

incident_summary: On 17 February 2023 at approximately 0315 hours, a gas leak was discovered on a 12-inch gas pipeline riser (PSN 759), approximately 5-7 feet above the water line on the WD 73AT platform. The platform's ESD was activated, shutting down the facility and sounding the alarm. All 26 personnel on board were accounted for and sheltered in place in the quarters building upwind from the leak. The pipeline was bled down over several hours. A light sheen was observed during overflight, later determined to originate from the slop oil tank rather than the pipeline. Investigation revealed inadequate preventative maintenance, failed repairs documented in a 2015 survey, and insufficient pipeline inspection methods.

incident_summary: On April 21, 2025, at 19:30 CDT, the WD 90 B platform experienced a loss of well control during Plug and Abandonment operations on well B020. An uncontrolled release of approximately 20 barrels of well fluids occurred due to a leak at the starter head spool. The incident was triggered when the P&A crew pumped fluid down the tubing to assist pressure equalization across a downhole plug. Oil was observed leaking from between the starter head flange and the surface casing annulus. After approximately 5-10 minutes, the fluid flow reduced and stopped, but uncontrolled gas was then observed, prompting full platform evacuation. All 22 personnel were safely evacuated. The well was shut in by closing the crown valve and cutting the wireline. No injuries were reported.

supporting_text: ["Upon observation of this leak, the CES supervisor called an all stop over the radio, and the pump operator shut down the pump.", "After an estimated 5 to 10 minutes the fluid flowing from the well reduced and stopped."]
incident_summary: On April 21, 2025, at 19:30 CDT, the WD 90 B platform experienced a loss of well control during Plug and Abandonment operations on well B020. An uncontrolled release of approximately 20 barrels of well fluids occurred due to a leak at the starter head spool. The incident was triggered when the P&A crew pumped fluid down the tubing to assist pressure equalization across a downhole plug. Oil was observed leaking from between the starter head flange and the surface casing annulus. After approximately 5-10 minutes, the fluid flow reduced and stopped, but uncontrolled gas was then observed, prompting full platform evacuation. All 22 personnel were safely evacuated. The well was shut in by closing the crown valve and cutting the wireline. No injuries were reported.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Managed Pressure Drilling (MPD) System
Role: Primary pressure control system
LOD Basis: Active pressure management during drilling operations
supporting_text: ["First, the MPD system was put into Flow Control mode", "the Surface Back Pressure (SBP) in the MPD system increased to over 1,300 psi exceeding the 1,300 psi PRV setting", "when the PRV's set point is exceeded the MPD system reverts to 'Manual Mode'"]
incident_summary: On November 16, 2022, at 05:11 AM, a saltwater kick occurred during drilling of the 12-1/4 inch hole section at approximately 25,000 feet on the Diamond Offshore West Vela drillship. The kick caused Surface Back Pressure in the MPD system to exceed the 1,300 psi PRV setting. Approximately twelve barrels of Synthetic Based Mud were discharged overboard via the MPD system Pressure Relief Valves. The well was successfully shut in and the kick was circulated out. The incident was attributed to human performance error in the decision to divert drilling fluid overboard rather than to the flowline as specified in the New Technology Application.

incident_summary: On February 14, 2015, bolts on an Inside Blow-Out Preventer (IBOP) actuator plate located on the top drive of the Vantage Titanium Explorer sheared, causing the actuator plate to fall approximately 140 feet to the rig floor. Investigation revealed that tight tolerances between the actuator plates and lever arms allowed contact during drilling operations, generating sufficient force to shear the bolts. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Lock-out/Tag-out (LOTO) procedures and documentation
Role: Administrative control to track valve key custody and prevent unauthorized valve operation
LOD Basis: Primary administrative barrier to prevent loss of control during equipment transitions
supporting_text: ["the keys for Pit #2's overboard dump valves were issued to the on-tour Derrickman by the Assistant Rig Manager, but no entry was made into the rig's Lock-out/Tag-out log book", "During the transition, the keys for Pit #2's dump valves were passed on again to the oncoming Derrickman. Again, no entries were entered into the rig's Lock-out/Tag-out log book"]
incident_summary: On December 11, 2014, the Noble Jim Day unintentionally discharged 142 barrels of Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SBM) into the Gulf of Mexico during mud pit cleaning operations. The discharge resulted from valve misalignment during the transition between crew changes. Keys to the dump valves were passed between personnel without proper Lock-out/Tag-out documentation. The Derrickman, lacking knowledge of proper valve alignment and receiving poor communication and labeling, misaligned the valves and created a pathway for hydrocarbons to discharge overboard. The Pit Volume Totalizer alarm detected the loss after approximately 5 minutes, at which point the crew secured the pits and corrected the valve alignment.

incident_summary: On July 27, 2016, during riser running operations on the Maersk Viking, a riser skate trolley derailed from its track. While attempting to reposition the skate using a 3.2 ton Vital Lever Hoist, the chain hoist malfunctioned when tension was applied. The hoist handle spun and struck an employee on the wrist, causing a bone fracture. The root cause was a defective chain hoist missing a vital retaining pawl latch. Contributing factors included poor job planning, improper pre-use inspection, and inadequate training.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Smoke/Temperature alarm on thruster #2
Role: Detection of abnormal conditions
LOD Basis: Early warning system for equipment failure

incident_summary: On December 23, 2015, at 11:20 hours, the drillship Discoverer Clear Leader experienced an electrical fire on the Motor Side of thruster #2 while performing abandonment operations for Chevron in Walker Ridge Block 758. The fire was caused by capacitor failure, which led to high temperatures, pressure buildup, and release of conductive gases. The incident triggered a muster and resulted in loss of thrusters #2 and #6, though the vessel maintained station-keeping ability. The well was in a secure state and a controlled disconnect was performed. The capacitor failure was attributed to stress from a previous incident on May 15, 2015.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Mud Engineer verification of valve alignment
Role: Prevent incorrect valve alignment during overboard discharge
LOD Basis: Primary control to ensure correct valve configuration before discharge operations
supporting_text: ["The Mud Engineer is also tasked with verifying valve alignments to avoid fluid contamination and accidental discharges of hazardous fluids into the marine environment.", "Without the Mud Engineer to assist in the procedure and verify valve alignment, two valves on the low pressure header were incorrectly opened while pumping water overboard"]
incident_summary: On June 27, 2017 at 2120 hours, the Pacific Sharav discharged 29 barrels of 14.0 PPG Synthetic Oil Based Mud into the Gulf of Mexico. The discharge occurred due to misalignment of two valves on the low pressure header while the Mud System Operator was pumping deck drain water from the muddy water tank overboard. The valves were incorrectly opened, allowing mud from the active mud system to enter the overboard line. The incident was caused by human error and failure to follow approved procedures, specifically the absence of the Mud Engineer who is responsible for verifying valve alignment during the semi-weekly discharge procedure.

The valves were incorrectly opened, allowing mud from the active mud system to enter the overboard line. The incident was caused by human error and failure to follow approved procedures, specifically the absence of the Mud Engineer who is responsible for verifying valve alignment during the semi-weekly discharge procedure.

incident_summary: On 07-JAN-2023 at 1920 hours, a single joint of 6.625" drill pipe (60.78 lbs./ft) being laid down using the Pipe Laydown System (PLS) on the drillship Transocean Deepwater Atlas was released and fell 58 feet onto the pipe skate. barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Low flow and high temperature safety devices on filtration unit
Role: Prevent excessive temperature buildup in unit
LOD Basis: N/A
supporting_text: ["the unit operators were having difficulties keeping the unit running. After contacting the manufacturer, the operators were instructed to disable the low flow and high temperature safety devices to enable the unit to remain running", "The disabled low flow and high temperature safety devices"]
incident_summary: At approximately 0130 hours on July 26, 2018, a fire ignited in the skid of a rental oil filtration unit on the North side of the production deck at the Big Foot facility (OCS-G 16942), located 120 miles from shore in 5185 feet of water. The fire was approximately 10 feet high by 10 feet wide with heavy black smoke. The root cause was that an operator restarted the filtration unit but failed to reopen the inlet and outlet valves after a supper break. The operator then left the unit unattended for approximately 14 minutes. With the valves closed and safety devices disabled, the temperature in the unit rose high enough to degrade the integrity of the hose, causing it to burst and release hot lube oil. The lube oil ignited, causing the fire. The fire was extinguished within a few minutes using AFFF and firewater. No injuries were reported. Property damage totaled $3,275,000.

barrier_role_match_text:

Role: Environmental assessment prior to casing operations
LOD Basis: N/A

incident_summary: On March 26, 2015, while performing drilling operations on the Noble Jim Day in Walker Ridge Block 508, approximately 3,107 feet of 22" casing was dropped to the seafloor. The rig was pre-running the 22" casing in an attempt to minimize downtime. Unforeseen loop currents increased from the forecasted 2-3 knots to 4.9 knots, causing the rig to lose dynamic positioning capability. A controlled drift was initiated to reduce loads on engines, thrusters, and the suspended casing string. At 09:00 on March 26th, uncommon rig movement indicated mechanical failure. Investigation revealed the Wellhead Running Tool (WHRT) Mandrel had snapped in two within an O-Ring groove due to excessive force from the casing weight combined with high loop currents. No subsea infrastructure or rig equipment was damaged. Metallurgical testing showed no equipment anomalies.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Slip Joint Packer Management System with Low Pressure Alarm
Role: Automatic detection and response to loss of packer seal pressure
LOD Basis: Automated system designed to detect pressure loss and engage secondary packer
supporting_text: ["The software logic for the packer management system will automatically adjust the low alarm set points based on fluctuations in the instrument air over a period of time", "The initial packer set point of 89 psi had increased to 99.1 psi without the crew's knowledge", "As the instrument air pressure was dropped from 106 psi to 98.7 psi, the low set point was reached and the system logic took over"]
incident_summary: While performing drilling operations on the Diamond Ocean Blackhawk for Anadarko Petroleum Corporation, two incidents occurred in which Synthetic Based Mud was inadvertently discharged into the Gulf of Mexico. The first incident on July 4, 2015 released 2.5 barrels of SBM, and the second on August 8, 2015 released 3.3 barrels of SBM. Both incidents were caused by a malfunction in the rig's software for the Slip Joint Packer management system, combined with inadequate crew monitoring of packer pressures. The software logic automatically adjusted low alarm set points based on instrument air pressure fluctuations without crew knowledge, causing the packer system to vent mud when pressure dropped below the adjusted set point.

incident_summary: On 26-NOV-2016 at approximately 1406 hours, a high pressure gas release occurred around the gas export metering skid on the FPSO Turritella at Walker Ridge Block 551-A. During an attempt to collect gas samples, an engineer mistakenly pulled a half-inch plug from a double block and bleed valve without first bleeding down the line, despite system pressure of up to 2500 psi. The escaping gas was detected by the gas detection system, triggering an automatic facility shutdown. The engineer sustained a minor injury and received onboard medical treatment. No evacuation was necessary.

LOD Basis: Management communication to prevent misunderstanding of work scope
supporting_text: ["It appears from information gathered during the investigation that either miscommunication or a lack of understanding of the instructions given to the workers by the production superintendant may have been a contributing factor in the chain of events that led to the incident."]
incident_summary: On 26-NOV-2016 at approximately 1406 hours, a high pressure gas release occurred around the gas export metering skid on the FPSO Turritella at Walker Ridge Block 551-A. During an attempt to collect gas samples, an engineer mistakenly pulled a half-inch plug from a double block and bleed valve without first bleeding down the line, despite system pressure of up to 2500 psi. The escaping gas was detected by the gas detection system, triggering an automatic facility shutdown. The engineer sustained a minor injury and received onboard medical treatment. No evacuation was necessary.

barrier_role_match_text:
Barrier: Pressure indication and awareness
Role: Visual awareness of system pressure before component removal
LOD Basis: Pressure gauges visible to personnel performing work
supporting_text: ["Both employees saw that there were up to 2500 pounds per square inch of pressure on the system in that area at the time."]
